When different files with the same name but of different types, or multiple versions of the same document, are found in the file system, the document discovery
task might attach multiple files to a document. In this situation, you need to resolve the duplicate files.
To review the files attached to the document and set status on each file, do the following:
1. In the Data Capture feature set, select Resolve Duplicate Documents.
2. In the Resolve Duplicates page, select a duplicate document from the left pane to display the files attached to it in the right pane.
 By default, each file is set to Unknown. If you or another reviewer previously changed the status of any of the files, they will display that updated
status.
3. For each file in the right pane, click in the Resolve Status column, and select a status from the list.
 You can type additional information about the duplicate file in the Notes column.
4. Click Save to save the updated status of the files and resolve the duplicates.
In the left pane, under the duplicate document name you can view the following:
A progress bar that shows progress made toward resolving this duplicate document.
The number of files attached to the duplicate document.
If you select more than one duplicate document from left pane of the Resolve Duplicates page, the files attached to the documents are not displayed.
An error message appears when two or more files with the same file name and file type are set to Master.
An error message appears when two files with the same name and file type are set to Master and Candidate.
Move duplicate files to a different document
You can move the duplicate files to an existing or new document by doing as follows:
1. In the right pane, select one or more duplicate files that you want to move.
2. Click to open the Move files to document dialog box.
3. In the dialog box, type the document name to which you want to move the selected files and click OK.
When you move two or more files to a new or existing document, a duplicate document is created.
When you move a single file
To
What happens
an existing document with
no files in the latest
version
the file gets attached to the latest version of the document
an existing document with
files attached to the latest
version
a duplicate document is created
a new document
a new document is created
Group files for a duplicate document
To group the files attached to a duplicate document and compare them, do the following:
1. In the Resolve Duplicates page, select one or more duplicate documents from the left pane to display the files attached to it in the right pane.
2. In the left pane, click the Group Files 
 option to categorize the files into groups, depending on the content similarity.
 The grouped output of the files is displayed in the right pane.
The group number of the files is displayed in the Group column in the right pane. Files with the same content are assigned the same group number, and
the files with unique content will be assigned a unique number.
When you resolve the status of one file within a group, the rest of the files in the group display the status Unknown. When you save the updated status
of the files in the group, the software automatically updates the status of those files from Unknown to Ignore.
Compare files for a duplicate document
To compare the content in the files attached to a duplicate document, do the following:
1. In the Resolve Duplicates page, select a duplicate document from the left pane to display the files attached to it in the right pane.
2. In the right pane, select two attached to the duplicate document. You can compare only two documents at a time.
 In the right pane, you can click the file to view its thumbnail along with the file properties.
3. Click Compare duplicates 
 to display the attached files in thumbnail view and side-by-side graphical view in the Compare duplicates window.
In the Compare Duplicates window, after comparing the files you can resolve the duplicates in the same window by updating the corresponding file
status to an appropriate option from the Resolved Status list of the file displayed in the side-by-side view left pane. The updated status of the file is
displayed in the Resolve Duplicate Documents page as well.
In the Compare Duplicates window left pane, default thumbnail icons are displayed for the files for which thumbnails are not attached.
In the Compare Duplicates window right pane, you can identify the path of the file by hovering on the 
 beside the corresponding file name.
Sign off a resolved document
Once the duplicate documents are completely resolved, you can do the following to sign off a document:
1. In the Resolve Duplicates page, select a duplicate document which is completely resolved.
 The progress bar below the document shows the progress made toward resolving the document.
2. Click Sign off 
 to sign off the resolved duplicate document.
 The signed off documents are automatically processed using the content discovery task, provided at least one document has a master file attached.
For more information, see Extract content using workflow template.
Use off-page connectors (OPCs) to view a continuation drawing
If more than one drawing file exists that have continuing information from a main file attached to the master document, such drawings are called continuation
drawings. The documents with continuing information are related to the master document.
The relationship with continuation drawings is created using off-page connectors (OPCs). When the drawing files are processed, using either Content
Discovery Task, Extract Content, or Check in with Content extraction, any information related to the continuation drawings is extracted and stored as the OPC.
The OPC in the drawing file allows you to navigate to the documents with the same name as the OPC, or any one of the alias OPCs.
 You can view continuation drawings using OPCs from drawings and PDF file types.
To view the continuation drawing from a master document, complete the following steps:
1. Click Documents > All Documents or Data Capture Documents.
2. Select any document from All Documents or Data Capture Documents list, and do either of the following:
Click View and Markup 
 to view file in the built-in compatible viewer, depending on the file type.
Click View and Markup 
 on the document's Actions 
 menu to view the file in a View and Markup window.
3. On the drawing, select and click any OPC to display its pop-up menu.
4. In the OPC's pop-up menu, click View Continuation Drawing 
 to navigate to its connected drawing.
5. If the document with the continuation drawing has both SPFN and FDW versions, you are asked to select an appropriate version in the Pick the version
to display for <document name> window.
 To view the details of a document related to an OPC, you can do the following:
1. On the drawing, select and click any OPC to display its pop-up menu.
2. In the OPC's pop-up menu, click Actions 
 > View off-page document.
Update an off-page connector (OPC) to relate it to a different document
You can change the file that opens when you navigate from an off-page connector (OPC). To open a different document, instead of the current document with
the same name as the OPC object, update the Alias Doc OPCs property on the OPC object.
1. Click Documents > All Documents or Data Capture Documents.
2. Select any document from All Documents or Data Capture Documents list, and do either of the following:
Click View and Markup 
 to view the file in the built-in compatible viewer, depending on the file type.
Click View and Markup 
 on the document's Actions 
 menu to view the file in a View and Markup window.
3. On the drawing, select and click any OPC to display its pop-up menu.
4. In the OPC's pop-up menu, click Actions 
 menu, and select Update off-page connector.
5. In the Document off-page connector details page, type the required target document name in the Alias Doc OPCs box.
6. Click UPDATE.
View and manage laser scans
 To use the Laser Scans feature set, you must install and set up a licensed version of HxGN SDx Connector for TruView software along with its
prerequisite software.
With the Laser Scans feature set, you can:
View and navigate across laser scans, sites, and servers.
View and manage laser scan tags.
Import Leica TruView Enterprise objects to Data Capture database, and relate them with Data Capture objects.
View j5 operations logbook entries in SDx
The j5 Operations Logbook application is used to record, track, and manage industrial events in a consistent and efficient way.
 An administrator must configure SDx to work with the j5 Operations Logbook application for you to view the logbook entries within SDx. For
information on how to configure SDx to work with j5 Operations Logbook, see Configure SDx to work with j5.
1. Select the relevant unit or maintenance unit.
2. Select Actions 
 > View Logbook. The j5 Operations Logbook page displays the events recorded for the unit.
 For information about the j5 Operations Logbook application, see j5 Operations Logbook.
Execute a project in SDx
Multi-organization collaboration is a key component in facilitating on time delivery of documentation throughout the design, construction, and handover phases
of the project.
SDx Projects provides a set of preconfigured collaboration work processes to manage document planning and receipt, technical queries, and action tracking.
 Click a box in the diagram to go to the associated procedure.
Steps
Description
Submittals and reviews
Contractors submit documents to the company for review.
Outgoing Technical Queries
Raised by the company to request information from contractors for their response compilation and
return.
Incoming Technical Queries
Raised by the contractor to request information from company for their response compilation and
return.
Internal Technical Queries
Request information from other company departments or users to support reviews.
Internal Action
Raised by the company user to perform an action within the organization, such as update or validate
engineering documents.
Outgoing Action
Raised by the company against the contractor to perform an action, such as update or validate
engineering documents.
Internal Review Action
Raised by the company user to get the working documents reviewed by other engineers within the
organization, before sharing the documents with other organizations.
Internal Draft Workflow
Raised by the company user to get the working documents updated by other engineers within the
organization.
Create a contract transfer request
Raised by the company user to associate multiple documents transferred from existing contract to
changed or reassigned contract.
Signature request
An electronic signature process raised by the company document controller to get the signed
approvals for the reviewed documents, so that these documents can be included in the transmittal and
shared with the contractors.
Transmittal Management
Issue of document packages to contractors and clients.
Handover request
Raised by the contractor to hand over information to the company on project phase completion.
Load the submittal documents/tags and perform document reviews
Submittals are the process by which the documents and tags are loaded into the SDx system. The loaded documents build the project Master Document
Register (MDR) and the loaded tags build the project Master Tag Register (MTR). Relations between documents and tags can be loaded to support easy
navigation from tag to document and vice versa. The submittals are run online by contractors from sources outside of the trusted domain and SDx handles the
transfer of the information to a secure location, scans the files for viruses, validates the content, and loads it into the database. The submittal's content may
include metadata and files, and an intermediate step can be configured for checking the malicious content. Where contractors are not allowed online access to
SDx, the submittals are run on behalf of the contractor by the global document controllers.
As the content is loaded into the system, it requires a review by one or more experts to ensure that it complies with the project specifications and standards. As
the deliverables are submitted into the system, they progress through the quality assurance (QA) workflow which manages their review with markup and
comment consolidation for return to the contractor. During this process, the reviewers can raise Review Actions that require monitoring to completion. The
Review Actions follow an independent lifecycle and workflow from that of the document, as they may impact many things and have a unique schedule for
closing.
The review process can be managed in one of two ways - document review matrix or review plan.
With the review plan option, a planning MDR is populated with the document names, their expected issue purposes, and dates against which the
company specialists' review plans are generated and managed. This plan-based approach allows the management of expected due dates and review
workloads across the projects.
Steps
Description
Manage the review assignment rules
Rules used to define how the review plan items are generated for each reviewer as the deliverable
planning CSVs are submitted.
Submit the planning deliverables
Download the template stored on Smart Community and available for download. Run the planning
submittal to build the MDR plan; document numbers, dates of issue at a given issue purpose, and
related major equipment, and so on. As the project progresses, additional documents may need
planning and the issue dates of existing documents may need revising.
Manage the review plans
Company engineers register against the MDR and manage their review plans - rules are rerun each
night to pick up new deliverables, new rules or changes to document and related tag metadata.
Submit the deliverables
Submission of documents for review.
QA Review Process
Submitted documents are run through the QA Review workflow. Reviewers are assigned according to
their plans or the Review Matrix. Reviews are consolidated and returned with a status to the
contractor.
Complete the review
The contractor receives notification of the completion of each review. They rework the deliverables as
needed and resubmit new revisions of the deliverables for review.
Review matrix routes the documents to reviewers as they come in based on the document type, discipline, and so on.
Steps
Description
Create a document review matrix
The review consolidator is specified on a review matrix that is related to the contract. This matrix can
also be used as an alternative to Deliverable Pre-planning where reviewers are also specified on the
review matrix.
Submit the planning deliverables
Download the template stored on Smart Community and available for download. Run the planning
submittal to build the MDR plan; document numbers, dates of issue at a given issue purpose, and
related major equipment, and so on. As the project progresses, additional documents may need
planning and the issue dates of existing documents may need revising.
Submit the deliverables
Submission of documents for review.
QA Review Process
Submitted documents are run through the QA Review workflow. Reviewers are assigned according to
their plans or the Review Matrix. Reviews are consolidated and returned with a status to the
contractor.
Complete the review
The contractor receives notification of the completion of each review. They rework the deliverables as
needed and resubmit new revisions of the deliverables for review.
Submittals
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | Submittals
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/paths/6200fd3404005657c008a4e1>
Planning submittal
 You must be a Contractor Document Control or Global Document Control to create the MDR planning submittal.
You can load the planned revision of a document before any data is loaded into the company's system, and relate it to plant equipment that is to be loaded into
a document revision later. You can use the planned revision to compare the data with the actual revision.
When you load the planned deliverables, the planned revision of the document has a status of RESERVED. When the actual deliverable is loaded into the
system, the planned deliverables' status automatically changes to RESERVEDSUPERSEDED. However, if the actual revisions are deleted for any reason, the
planned revision status is changed back to RESERVED.
Steps
Description
MDR planning submittal
The MDR planning submittal templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Create the MDR cover sheet
This file is stored on the MDR document and typically is a report from the contractor's system.
Populate the CSV file
Populate the CSV file with the deliverables and issue plan dates. The MDR document is usually listed
as the first row of the file, though this is optional.
Create the planning submittal
The submittal is created, and the documents and plan milestones are loaded into the system.
Review submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Any errors reported by the validation and export stages are addressed prior to re-submission.
Review deliverable submission
The MDR and the deliverable milestones of planned deliverables can be reviewed in the system.
What can be done on a planning submittal?
 While processing the MDR planning submittal, if one or more issue purposes in the attached file are not related to the current plant or project,
the validation rules stop the processing, and the user must relate the issue purpose to the current plant or project to process the submittal.
Create the first revision of the MDR with new attached planned revisions.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
The MDR document name, title, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and area must be supplied.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
The subsequent rows are repeated for each planned issue purpose of a planned document.
The planned revisions require a name, title, revision of ‘--', classification, area, and a document issue date though the issue date is ignored.
The issue purpose and the planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be supplied.
All other information is optional.
Update the current revision of the MDR to update the issue plan of the reserved revisions or add new ones.
The first row usually contains the MDR document.
The MDR document name, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and area must be supplied.
The issue date of the MDR is ignored if the name and revision match the current revision as this must not be changed on an issued
revision.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR title can be updated.
The MDR filename must be left blank.
Create a new revision of the MDR document to update the issue plan of the reserved revisions or add new ones.
The MDR revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision and the issue date must be later than or equal to that of the
current revision. A time stamp is automatically added to the issue date.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
Update the planning information of planned revisions.
This can be done by updating the current MDR revision or creating a new one as above.
The subsequent rows can contain planned revisions.
The planned revisions require a name, classification and area that matches that of the existing planned revision, and an issue date though the
issue date is ignored. The revision should still be ‘--‘. The issue purpose and planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be supplied.
The title can be updated.
All other information is optional.
Update the planning information of existing deliverables.
This can be done by updating the current MDR revision or creating a new one as above.
The subsequent rows can contain planned revisions as above or existing deliverables or a combination. The existing deliverables could be recently
submitted documents in the Current state or documents that have been revised from the plant to the project as part of a change in the Working
state.
The existing deliverable revisions require a name, revision, classification and area that matches that of the latest revision of that deliverable,
and an issue date though the issue date is ignored. The issue purpose and the planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be
supplied.
The title can be updated.
All other information is optional.
 When updating planning information, you do not need to send the complete MDR planning submittal. For example, if you planned more than a few
documents and only want to update certain information of one document, the updated planning submittal can contain that single document alone.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for a planning submittal
Planning submittals are used to create documents with a reserved status on the Master Document Register with the planned dates for the deliverables at each
issue purpose.
Pre-requisites
All submittals require the following information created in advance. This information is used when processing the submittal. The submittal will create or update
the MDR and all related documents.
All issue purposes, classifications, and disciplines referenced in the submittal must be related to the current project or its parent plant. If you relate an
issue purpose, a classification, or a discipline at the plant level to a project, it disables its inheritance from the plant.
A contract must exist between the contracting organization submitting the deliverables and the organization hosting the SDx system.
The contract must have a related review matrix that defines the consolidator for the document review work process.
This mapping is used to submit the planned deliverables. One row must contain the MDR document which is usually the first row. Two examples of content are
shown below, one for the MDR row and one for the planned deliverables.
All the columns listed below must be included in the CSV file.

Deliverable submittal
 This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Deliverable submittal (modified in an update).
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | Deliverable Submittal
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6200f339be7ad12e1d368f90>
A deliverable submittal contains documents that are ready to be delivered to the company. The submittal uploads all files to a "safe" area, creates new
revisions of the documents, and attaches the files.
The delivered revisions are routed to a document review workflow which automatically assigns documents to the reviewers and a consolidator.
MDR deliverable submittal
You can use this to submit the documents and route them to a document review workflow.
Steps
Description
MDR deliverable submittal
The MDR deliverable submittal templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Populate the MDR CSV file
Populate the CSV file with the deliverables and their associated filenames. The MDR document is
listed as the first row of the file, though this is optional.
Create the deliverable submittal
The submittal is created, and the document controllers get a step to review the metadata information
of documents. The document controllers then approve or reject the submittal.
Review the submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Any errors reported by the validation or export stages are addressed prior to re-submission.
Review submitted documents
The MDR and the deliverables can be reviewed in the system. You can also search for some of the
submitted tags and review their attribution and relationships. You can query by date to get the full list.
Review the document metadata
If the Grouped Review flag is enabled on the project, the submittal process pushes a review step to
the document controller for reviewing the metadata information of documents. At this stage, other
reviewers cannot see any of these documents.
You cannot submit a historical revision if the Grouped Review flag is enabled. However, the
historical revisions can still be loaded using the loader workflow. For more information, see
Loading documents and data.
If a document is submitted with an incorrect issue purpose and subsequently rejected during the
grouped review process, the contractor can resubmit the document with the corrected issue
purpose. Upon resubmission, the document revision will be updated to reflect the corrected
issue purpose.
MTR/TDR submittal
You can use this to load the tags and create the document to tag relationships.
Steps
Description
Tag MTR
The tag MTR templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Populate the MTR CSV files
Populate the two CSV files; one for the tag list and one for its attribution.
Create the MTR submittal
The submittal is created, and the tags loaded into SDx.
Tag TDR
The tag TDR templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Populate the TDR CSV files
Populate the CSV file to build the relationships between the documents and tags.
Create the TDR submittal
The submittal is created and the documents to tag relationships are created.
Review submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Any errors reported by the validation or export stages are addressed prior to re-submission.
What can be done on a deliverable submittal?
 While processing the MDR planning submittal, if one or more issue purposes in the attached file are not related to the current plant or project,
the validation rules stop the processing, and the user must relate the issue purpose to the current plant or project to process the submittal.
Create the first revision of the MDR with attached deliverable documents.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
The MDR document name, title, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and area must be supplied.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
The subsequent rows contain the deliverable documents.
The document name, title, revision, issue date, classification, and area must be supplied.
The filename is defined to link the correct updated file to the document.
The issue purpose is optional but is required if the planning submittals and the review plans are being used to manage the review process.
This is also required for any form of progress measurement or reporting.
All other information is optional.
Update the current revision of the MDR to update the deliverable revisions or add new ones.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
MDR document name, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and file name must be supplied.
The issue date of the MDR is ignored if the name and revision match the current revision as this must not be changed on an issued
revision.
The MDR title can be updated.
The MDR filename must be left blank.
New deliverable documents or new revisions of the existing documents can be added.
Create a new revision of the MDR document to submit new or updated deliverable documents.
The MDR revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision and the issue date including the time stamp must be later than or
equal to that of the current revision. A time stamp is automatically added to the issue date.
New deliverable documents or new revisions of existing documents can be added.
Submit new revisions of the existing deliverables.
The submitted deliverable revisions require a name, revision, classification and area that matches that of the current revision of that deliverable,
and an issue date though the issue date is ignored.
A new revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision and the issue date including the time stamp must be later than or
equal to that of the current revision. A time stamp is automatically added to the issue date.
The title can be updated.
The document filename is usually supplied.
The issue purpose is optional but is required if the planning submittals and the review plans are being used to manage the review process.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for deliverable submittal
Deliverable submittals are used to issue documents and their associated files to the company. The MDR is used to identify which documents are expected or
issued by the contractor for a review.
Pre-requisites
All submittals require the following information created in advance. This information is used when processing the submittal. The submittal will create or update
the MDR and all related documents.
All issue purposes, classifications, and disciplines referenced in the submittal must be related to the current project or its parent plant. If you relate an
issue purpose, a classification, or a discipline at the plant level to a project, it disables its inheritance from the plant.
A contract must exist between the contracting organization submitting the deliverables and the organization hosting the SDx system.
The contract must have a related review matrix that defines the consolidator for the document review work process.
This mapping is used to submit the deliverable documents along with the files. One row must contain the MDR document, which is usually the first row. Two
examples of content are shown below, one for the MDR row and one for the deliverables.
All the columns listed below must be included in the CSV file.

What can be done on an MTR submittal?
The MTR submittal process allows you to create, update, and delete tags with classification and properties.
Depending on the requirements of the contract, SDx Projects provides two options for managing tag numbering.
If the owning company wants to maintain control of the tag numbering, it creates and allocates the tag numbers to the contractor to use when submitting
the MTR.
The tag numbering is provided by the contractor as part of their MTR submittal. Allowing the contractors to provide the tag numbers prevents duplicates
in the system.
The tag status can be set as part of the submittal. Delivered values are NoValue, Reserved, Planned, and As-built. Your administrator can modify these values
as part of the project setup.
What information must be supplied?
The MTR submittal process allows you to load two types of CSV file formats. The basic format contains the tag data and must have the tag name, type, area,
and unit. The classification should be specified as MTR to create an MTR submittal. The inverted format contains the tag properties and must have the tag
name.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for MTR submittal
MTR submittals are used to load tags and their properties into the system. There are two files, one for the basic tag information and the other for its properties.
This mapping is used to submit the tags and their base data.

What can be done on a TDR submittal?
The TDR submittal process allows you to:
Relate tags to the planned revision to indicate the planned deliverables. For example, major equipment.
Relate tags to the delivered revisions to indicate the actual deliverable.
What information must be supplied?
The tag document must have a tag name, document, and revision. The classification should be specified as TDR in the csv file to create a TDR submittal.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for TDR submittal
TDR submittals are used to relate tags and their either reserved or deliverable documents.
This mapping is used to relate tags and their deliverable documents.

How do submittals work?
Create a submittal
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | How to Create a Submittal
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65adb800166be4fedd290c46>
1. Select the Submittals feature set.
Select this
To do this
Create MDR submittal
Submit planning deliverable and deliverables ready for review.
Create MTR submittal
Load tags for major equipment.
Create TDR submittal
Load tags to planned document relationships. These planned tag
relationships are separate to the actual document tag relationships that
will be created during subsequent deliverable upload so that it is possible
to run comparisons to see if the planned tags have been specified.
Create MDR/MTR/TDR submittal
Loads documents, tags and the relationships between documents and
tags.
2. Provide other details as needed.
a. Enter Title to the submittal.
b. Select From organization name from the list.
 It is a default value for most users, set by your global document control user on behalf of your organization.
c. Select the Contract to relate the documents, and verify if related objects can be submitted.
 To organization is automatically defined by your choice of contract.
d. Select Item owning group, the department or team within the organization primarily responsible for the document.
3. Upload the CSV control file that contains all the appropriate submittal details, such as planned or reserved deliverables.
 CSV control file examples are available for you to use and modify on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more
information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
 The CSV file formatting is validated after successful upload, before the submittal job is created. Delete the CSV file if any formatting errors are
displayed, and upload it again after rectifying the errors.
4. Upload supporting files to attach to the documents specified in the CSV control file.
 To avoid system generation of the submittal name, always select the Suppress ENS check box. This check box is enabled by your administrator.
Monitor the submittal progress
 This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Monitor the submittal progress (modified in an update).
1. In the Submittals feature set, select Show My Submittals.
2. Select  on My Submittals page, next to Actions 
.
3. Select Refresh 
 multiple times to see the change of status at every stage.
 You can select the following options from the Actions 
 menu:
Select this
To do this
Approve
Approve and trigger the next workflow step of the selected submittal.
Delete
Delete the selected submittal, along with any attached files.
View Summary
View the summary of the selected submittal.
Review validation errors
After the validation stage is completed, a validation report is available for download. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any
corrective actions.
1. On the My Submittals page, select a submittal, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Validation Report link available below the VALIDATION progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
For more information on how to troubleshoot the issues, see Validation.
Review export stage errors
An export report is available for download only if the submittal fails in the second stage of the export process. The information in the Microsoft Excel report
allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Submittals page, select a submittal, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Export Report link available below the EXPORT progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
 If any submittal jobs are awaiting approval by a user later than the value specified by DaysBeforeCleanUp setting, a file not found error may occur
during the export process. This is because the files have been removed from the SubmissionsShareDirectory. For more information on the
DaysBeforeCleanUp setting, see File Service Settings node properties.
Upload a document
If you want to submit a document without using CSV, then:
1. In the Submittals feature set, select Upload Document.
2. On the Upload Document form, select the document using the search functionality.
The search result filters the document based on the entry point given in parameter 2 of UploadDocument client API. For more information on the
client API, see UploadDocument client API and its delivered method.
On selecting the document, the system automatically populates the existing revision scheme and suggests the next major and minor revision.
How do revision schemes work?
Unlike other entities in SDx, revision schemes are not tied to specific configurations (like project or plant levels). This means they can be used
across multiple levels and projects. When searching for a revision scheme, the system follows a hierarchical approach. It starts at the current level
(for example, project) and works its way up through the configuration tree. If a revision scheme is found at any level, the search stops, and that
scheme is made available. If no revision scheme is found at any lower level, the system searches at the Configuration Top level, which is the
highest level in the configuration hierarchy and sets the available revision scheme.
For example: Relating Revision Schemes to Projects
Revision Scheme ONE is associated with Plant A.
Revision Scheme TWO (and possibly others) is associated with Project_1407.
In this scenario:
Revision Scheme ONE will be available for selection in Plant A.
Revision Scheme ONE will not be available for selection in Project_1407 due to the association with Revision Scheme TWO.
In essence, revision schemes provide a flexible way to manage different versions of data across various levels of your project hierarchy.
3. Select one or more issue purposes.
4. In New Files, drag the files or select the file system where you want to access the files to attach to the new version of the document.
 You cannot upload certain file formats or file name extensions. For more information, see Site Settings node properties.
5. Select Apply to upload the document into the system and stay on the form to upload another document or select Finish to upload the document and exit
the form.
 The new revision is routed to the QA Review Verification workflow, which verifies if the document revision has been reviewed at the same
revision and issue purpose, and if not reviewed, routes the revision to the QA Review workflow.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx Roles
<https://docs.hexagonppm.com/viewer/document/KV2OpDSbTEp81JHaojREVQ> matrix.
Reviews
Set up the reviewers/consolidators
The review process can be managed using either of the following ways:
Review plan: A planning MDR is populated with the document names, their expected issue purposes, and the dates set for the company specialists'
review plans. The plan-based approach helps manage tentative due dates and review workloads across the projects.
Document review matrix: The review matrix routes the documents to reviewers as they move in based on the document type, discipline, and so on.
Review plans
Review plans allow you to manage the documents that you should review.
 You will not be able to register interest or manage the review plan items if the Generate Reviews by Matrix? flag, available in the New Project or the
Update Project page, is selected for a project by your business administrator.
Register interest in documents to review
When you create a review plan, you are creating a checklist of items to review. After you have registered interest in a set of documents, items are added to
your review plan by predefined rules that automatically assign the documents to you or by manual selection of documents you are interested in.
1. In the Review Distribution feature set, search for Master Document Registers (MDR).
2. Select the Master Document Register (MDR), and click Actions 
 > Register for Interest.
3. Select a security code and an intensity level.
The security codes are configuration-independent items that are used to set different security levels and can be applied to objects in a configuration.
They can be used to control the confidentiality or restrict the availability of the objects, such as documents and tags.
The intensity determines which assignment rules are run. For example, you are a mechanical engineer and rules are in place to assign all mechanical
documents to you. Selecting High runs both high and low intensity business rules to determine which documents are assigned to you.
You can create review plans as needed.
Set your review plan to active
Once you have created your review plan, you receive a notification and can navigate to your review plans. You must set the review plan to active to begin
receiving documents to review.
1. In the side bar, click Notifications 
, and select the MDR notification.
2. Click View Context 
 to navigate to your review plan.
3. Click Actions 
 > Manage Review Plan.
 You can turn on 
 on the Manage Review Plan page, to receive e-mail notification about the review plan updates. The e-mail notification has
information about review plan updates along with a link which navigates to the Manage Review Plan page.
4. Select Active in the Status list.
Tell me more
Click this
To do this
DRAFT
Create and modify your review plan. The review plan is not active, but allows
you to specify documents or tags that you want to review.
ACTIVE
Activate the review plan. You then receive documents for review in your To Do
List.
INACTIVE
Deactivate the review plan. You no longer receive documents for review.
Add documents to a review plan
How do I change my review settings for a document issue purpose?
The following table details the settings available for documents in the document review cycle. For example, you can either accept or decline a document for
review.
Icon
Name
Description
Accepted
Indicates that the document has been accepted for document review.
Declined
Indicates that the document has been declined for document review.
Changed
Indicates that the document has been changed and a document review is
advised.
Information only
Indicates that the document has been accepted for information review.
Suggested for information
Indicates that the document has been suggested for information review.
Not in plan
Indicates that the document is available but not in the review plan.
You can also bulk edit issue purposes by clicking Bulk Edit 
.
When should I bulk edit documents?
You can bulk edit when there are multiple documents on the current page to which you want to apply the same changes. For example, you can select the issue
purposes required and apply them on all the documents for which you want to accept or decline a review.
If documents have automatically been added to your review plan, you can accept or decline the review. You can also search for and add documents to your
review plan.
1. Select the criteria that you are interested in for the document or tag; for example, select a mechanical discipline, and then select Search.
Search for documents or tags

2. Select a document and expand the issue purpose.
3. Click the Issue Purpose required to set the status to Accepted 
 and add it to your review plan.
To decline 
 or remove a document from your review plan, click the Issue Purpose twice.
If assignment rules are defined, document reviews based on issue purpose are preselected for you.
Search for documents or tags

2. Expand the hierarchy of the selected tab to display the object tag or document type hierarchy.

4. In the Search pane, enter details of the object tag or document type in the Name or Description boxes.
5. Select any additional options as required, and click SEARCH.
Filtering your search results
To further filter the documents and tags that you are interested in, select one or more of the filter options, and click UPDATE.
Click this
To do this
Show the documents in which you have registered an interest and you have
accepted to conduct a document review.
Show the documents that have changed in the search results and you are
invited to conduct a document review. You must accept the document for it to be
