 You can only move or copy pins to a target list in a configuration related to the selected create scope.
Create an object related to a pin
You can create new objects related to selected pinned items by clicking Create an object from the selected pins 
in the Pins menu ribbon. The create
method applicable to the object type selected creates a new object related to the pinned item using the appropriate create form, such as a change request.
 If multiple pins are selected, the list of create methods represents only those that apply to all the items. If only one create method matches the pinned
items type, it is the default.
Managing documents and files
Web Client provides you with the following document management features:
Document revision and version control
Life cycle control including revise, check out, check in, and sign off
Document, file, revision, and version user access and security
Create, update, copy, and cancel documents
Send documents through workflows
Comment on documents and related files
Generate PDF copies
User-subscribed notification of document changes
Upload and download of documents and files
Attach, edit, replace, and manage files
 All document management features are configurable and can be edited or hidden depending on your own required documentation life cycle.
Working with documents
You work and manage documents in the document life cycle using actions which are configured out of the box on documents for basic functionality.
How do you use the different document commands?
The following table shows the different document commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Revise
The CURRENT document is revised, which creates a new WORKING version.
Edit Files
A new WORKING document version is created and marked as checked out. Files attached to the
previous working version can be downloaded to a client device for editing. You must manually check
in the new document version after editing.
Check In
Check in the new document version, created when the Edit Files command was used, along with any
files that were checked out for editing.
Sign Off
Signs off a complete document and changes the status of the document revision from WORKING to
CURRENT.
Sign off with form
Signs off a complete document revision and allows you to update properties and revision notes. The
document revision status changes from WORKING to CURRENT.
Update Document
Update the master properties of the document.
Update Latest Revision
Update the properties of the latest revision of the document.
Copy
Creates a new copy of the document with a new name, and allows you to select the required
property details and values on the Copy form.
Document Controller role only
Action
What it does
Undo Sign Off
Changes the status of the document revision from CURRENT to WORKING.
Cancel
Cancels a document when it is in the RESERVED state.
Documentation life cycle
All revisable documents follow a controlled document life cycle using a revision scheme that includes current revisions, working revisions, versions, and their
attached files.
What is the revision scheme?
A revision scheme is a sequence of major and minor revisions, such as A1 and B1, that are used while creating or revising a document to keep track of its
progress as it undergoes change. This, in turn, also reflects any changes made to attached files that the document manages. All revisable documents in the
revision scheme are composed of document masters, revisions, and versions along with any attached files.
Document master
A document master represents the top revision-independent information, such as the document title.
Document revision
A document revision contains revision-specific information. As a document is created and revised, new revisions are created and attached to the document
master. In a single configuration, such as a plant or a project, there can be only one current revision and one working revision at one time.
Document version
A document version contains version-specific information. A version of a document can be made for each project in a plant. Files are attached to the document
version.
What is the document life cycle?
The standard documentation life cycle has stages, such as create, sign off, revise, check out, check in, and sign off. There can be some variation of this
depending on the number of revisions and versions needed and which edit command you use, such as Edit Files or Replace Files.
The revise, modify, and sign off process can be continually repeated.
New documents are brought into the system as Working versions.
How do I view all revisions and versions of a document?
Show all revisions of a document
The Show All Revisions command allows you to display all revisions of a selected document in a results grid. This command is available in the Actions
menu of a document.
On a document's Actions menu, select Master Relationships > Show All Revisions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Revisions displays the signed off
current revisions of the selected document by default.
Show all versions of a document
The Show All Versions command allows you to display all versions of a selected document revision in a results grid. If you use this command from the current
revision of the document, then all the versions associated with the current revision are returned. However, if you navigate to a superseded revision, the
command returns all the versions associated with that revision.
On a document's Actions menu, select Revision Relationships > Show All Versions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Versions displays the latest signed off
current versions of the selected document by default.
What do the different document icons mean?
The document revision state is indicated by its icon.
Icon
Document Revision State
Notes
Current revision
The Current revision. The
working revision is signed
off. No claim state.
Current revision superseded
A Current revision has
been revised for further
editing. No claim state.
Current revision result of a claim
The Current revision is the
result of a claim from a
higher configuration.
Current revision source of a claim
The current revision is the
source of a claim to a
lower configuration.
Current revision result of a claim and source of a claim
The Current revision has
been claimed from a
higher configuration and is
the source of a claim to a
lower configuration. Result
of a three tier-hierarchy
scenario.
Superseded revision
The revision that was
previously signed off as
the Current revision, but
has been superseded by
another signed off
revision. No claim state.
Superseded revision source of a claim
The revision is the source
of a claim to a lower
configuration and
superseded by a newer
Current revision.
Working revision/version
The working revision or
version. Either a previous
Current revision has been
revised to create a new
working revision and
version, or this is the first
revision of the document.
No claim state.
Working version superseded
The previous working
version has been
superseded by a check in.
No claim state.
Working version checked out
A new working version has
been checked out for
editing. No claim state.
Working version checked out superseded
The new working version
checked out for editing
has been superseded. No
claim state.
Working version result of a claim
A new working version is
the result of a claim from a
higher configuration by
revising the Current
revision.
Working revision source of a claim
The working version that is
the source of a claim to a
lower configuration.
Submitted revision
A submitted revision is
created when you submit a
document using an MDR
deliverable submittal with
the Grouped Review flag
enabled on the project.
Rejected submitted revision
The rejected submitted
revision is created when a
user with a document
controller role rejects an
incorrect submitted
revision.
 These documents
are not visible in a normal
search and can only be
found in the ToDo list or
with relationship
expansion.
Superseded rejected submitted revision
The superseded rejected
submitted revision is
created when you reject a
submitted revision and
submit a new version. The
earlier version is
superseded.
Planned revision
Using an MDR planning
submittal, you can create a
placeholder document.
Such a document is
reserved and has full
document representation
(master, revision, and
version), but does not
have a file attached. A
typical revision is ‘—’.
Superseded planned revision
The planned revision has
been superseded by the
submission of the actual
document. The document
is revised to a normal
revision scheme from a
planned revision scheme.
The following description and image details how a document progresses through the Document Life Cycle:
You create a new document from a master template as a new Working Revision. After sign off, the document is locked and set as the Current Revision
(A1).
The Current Revision (A1) is revised to create a new Working Revision (B1, Version 1), so you can edit the files in a document. Only working
revisions can be edited.
The Working Revision (B1, Version 1) is checked out to edit files, which creates Working Revision (B1, Version 2). Any requested attached files are
downloaded to your local machine for editing.
When you finish making edits to the Working Revision (B1, Version 2) or any later version, and it is complete, you can sign off the Working Revision to
create a new Current Revision (B1), which is locked to prevent any further change.
The document life cycle revision process in the image above is summarized by the following:
Create
A new document is created from a master and enters the system as the
WORKING REVISION A1.
Sign off
Files are attached to the document and ready to be issued and locked, sign
off the revision to create a CURRENT REVISION A1.
Current Revision
The status of the revision that was signed off is changed to CURRENT, and
the document is considered Issued.
Revise
The CURRENT REVISION A1 is revised, which creates a new WORKING
REVISION B1, VERSION 1.
The CURRENT REVISION A1 is still the Current revision and the most
recently approved and issued revision, but it is marked as undergoing change.
Edit Files
The Edit Files command is used, which creates WORKING REVISION B1,
VERSION 2, and the attached files are downloaded to a client device for
editing.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 1 marked as undergoing change.
Check In
The document's attached files are uploaded and checked in as a new
WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 1 is set to SUPERSEDED.
Replace Files
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2 is updated using the Replace
Files command, which replaces existing files with files of the same name.
This creates a new WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 3.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2 is set to SUPERSEDED.
Sign Off
The WORKING VERSION B1, VERSION 3 is signed off and is set as the new
CURRENT REVISION B1.
The previous existing CURRENT REVISION A1 is set to SUPERSEDED.
The example shown is for demonstration of the overall process only. This process can vary depending on your system, the revision scheme, access
permissions, and the commands used as shown.
Each time a working revision document is edited, a new version is created and the previous version is set as SUPERSEDED. However, all superseded
revisions and their versions are retained and can be viewed in the system. For more information, see Viewing the History.
Create a document
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | How to create a document
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/61c320bbcc031873ff592d58>
1. In the Documents feature set, select Create Document.
 You select the document type to create depending on the configured method.
2. On the Create document form, enter the details and values required for the document in the sections and tabs available.
When assigning classification to the object, you can use the Classification search bar to search and filter for the appropriate classifications based
on their Name and Description.
If your system is configured to use ENS, you can create multiple documents with ENS using the create or copy forms, and review them in the
Summary pane. For more information, see ENS preview summary.
 You can also assign the collaborating organizations from the Collaboration tab. This option is not available for Administration document
classifications.
3. In New Files pane,select the files you want to attach to the new document from the tabs.
In the LOCAL FILE SYSTEM tab, select CHOOSE FILES or drop files onto the area from a selected location.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of uploaded files must not exceed 2GB. The
application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these limitations.
If the file being attached reference other files and they are either already in the system attached to other documents or also selected to be attached
here, the software automatically creates a relationship between the master file and the referenced files along with the relationship between the
master file and the document version. For more information, see Working with reference files and relationships.
4. Select FINISH, and the document is created as a working version ready for sign off.
If the create document method has been configured to display the TEMPLATE DOCUMENTS tab.
Select the correct template document for the document classification type that have a set of predefined files. These files appear in the Template
Files pane.
You can see the revision, revision state, check out, checked in, and version of the template document so you can choose the correct template
document.
 The Rename template files with document name option allows the renaming of template files with the new document suffix. If ENS is enabled,
this option stays selected when you create more than one document.
You can select only a single owning discipline to relate it to a document. The primary or owning disciplines are mainly used in the security rules or the
document review matrix. The Discipline box displays the disciplines a user or a role is related to based on the following scenarios:
If a user or a role is not linked to any discipline, all the disciplines related to the current configuration are displayed in the box.
If a user or a role is linked to a few disciplines, all these disciplines are displayed in the box.
If a user or a role is linked to a single discipline, that discipline is displayed and automatically selected in the box.
You can select multiple secondary disciplines to relate them to a document. Secondary disciplines (or review disciplines) are mainly used in the review
assignment rules, where multiple disciplines can review a document. The Secondary disciplines box displays all the disciplines related to the current
configuration. If the current configuration has no selected disciplines, then the selected disciplines of the higher configuration is displayed in the box.
 The owning and secondary disciplines are not dependent. When applying the secondary disciplines for review, the owning discipline is not
automatically added to the list of secondary disciplines.
Copy a document
 The Copy Document command does not copy any files attached to the original document. Files must be added to the new document using the
Manage Files or Replace Files commands. This is by design to avoid file duplication in the system.
You can copy any document master or revision. This copies the original document details, type, and values to create a new document.
1. Select a document, and select Actions 
 > Copy Document.
2. On the Copy form, and give the document a unique name. Asterisks (*) indicate required information.
 The new document cannot have the same name as the original document. If you use ENS, the system will apply the next available number as
per your ENS settings.
3. Select the revision scheme and addition information values as required.
 You can also use the Copy Revision command, which copies the document and revision scheme, but allows you to edit the revision scheme as
required.
Create a document sheet
Some documents may need additional document revisions created as sheets in order to be complete and allow you to add a different file to each document
sheet separately.
You create additional document sheets numbered in a sequence for a selected document using a CreatNextSheet method created from the CreateNextSheet
client API.
When you select the CreateNextSheet method, the selected document is copied and creates multiple new document sheets, each with a separate sheet
number incremented from the original document ENS.
For example, for a document number starting at Doc-01-01 subsequent document sheets follow the sheet sequence, such as d Doc-01-02, Doc-01-03, Doc-
01-04, and so on.
The originating document must be configured with an ENS in order to increment the sheet number portion for each new document sheet.
Each document sheet shows the total number of sheets created from the selected document. This is also displayed in the selected document column set.
For more information on configuring a method to create multiple document sheets, see Configure documents with multiple sheets.
Revise a document
 To revise a document in the current scope configuration, the document must be signed off to set the document revision state to CURRENT.
To make any edits to a document, you must revise the document to create a new WORKING version in the project.
1. Select a current revision document to revise and select Actions 
 > Edit > Revise.
2. If required, type a new title, revision scheme, or issue purpose.
3. If required, select CHOOSE FILES, and select additional files to attach to the new working version.
 If you want to bring markups related to the previous version of the attached files forward and include them on the new working version, select
Copy Markups.
4. Select REVISE.
 To attach a file that references other files, you must ensure that the nested reference file level is less than or equal to the maximum
number of nested reference files allowed as set in User Preferences. For more information, see Set the user options.
 If the document to be revised has attached to it a master file that is associated with out-of-date reference files, after clicking REVISE, an Update
Reference Relationships window appears. In the window, you can update the reference relationship of the attached reference files. For more
information, see Update reference relationships.
If a master file references a file that is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the appropriate
reference file in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For
more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When you revise a document, the revision scheme creates the document as the next major revision by default. However, you can change the revision
scheme so that it revises a document to the next minor revision. For more information, see parameter 12 in the Revise client API.
If you want to revise a higher configuration or plant Current Revision document to a project, see Revise a document to a project.
If configured by your administrator, existing document relationships can be automatically inherited and assigned to the new document revision, when
revising a document at the plant level.
Revise multiple documents
1. Select multiple documents that you want to revise, and select Actions 
 > Edit > Revise.
The Bulk Revise page displays a list of selected documents which are valid or invalid for revise.
The documents selected for revise must be signed off.
The documents valid for revise are listed in the Valid Documents for Revise section.
If documents are not valid for revise, a validation error message is displayed in the Invalid documents for revise section.
 You can choose to either proceed with only the revisable documents, or cancel the operation to address the validation errors.
2. Select Revise.
 Revise notifications are created for each document in the Notifications 
 list. You can check the status of the revise process by refreshing the revise
notification.
 When you revise multiple documents simultaneously, the next major revision is created for all revised documents.
See the Bulk Revise page
Edit and check in a document
When you edit a WORKING version document, it is checked out and set as reserved for edit, and then downloaded to your device for editing. After a document
has been edited, you must check in the document as a new WORKING version.
How can I check in or replace a file along with content extraction?
 By default, this feature is not enabled. However, your administrator can change the default behavior to enable content extraction for the Check
in and Replace Files methods. For more information, see Configure content extraction on Replace Files and Check in methods.
To allow content extraction whenever you check in a document or replace a file related to a document, after attaching the file in the New Files page, you must:
1. Set any replaced file or checked in file as the Master, by clicking Set as Master to mark the file for content extraction and display the Extract Content
window.
2. In the Extract Content window,
a. Select a template group from the TemplateGroup/Template list to apply the processing rules using the corresponding Preprocessor Reader.
b. Click Ok.
 By default, the file name is displayed in the Select File list.
 You can use preprocessed content files for processing the file. For more information, see Extract data from a document.
3. Click FINISH to successfully replace a file or check in a document along with content extraction.
 To check the status of the content extraction, you can click Notifications.
1. Select Actions 
 > Edit Files.
2. On the Select files to download page, select the file or files you want to edit, and select Save.
 Depending on the browser used, you can either open the document directly or locate it saved in the download location on your device.
3. Edit the files locally.
4. To check in the updated files, select the document, and select Actions 
 > Check In.
5. On the Select Document(s) to Check In window, select a master document you want to check in from the list of documents displayed, you cannot
select its reference documents as the software displays them in the view mode.
6. Select Choose Files to locate the files to be checked in, and select FINISH.
You must select the files from the files displayed in the Files that are being edited section
To check in a document by attaching a file that references other files, including their nested reference files, you must ensure that the nested
reference file level is less than or equal to the maximum number of nested reference files allowed. For more information, see Set the user options.
The modified document must be uploaded with its original name. You can see the check in details in Object Details > History tab, which lists the
object's history details, such as the original name or creation date.
When checking in a document and a master file with its referenced files:
If a file is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see
Attach master file which references other files.
For files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more
information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When you edit a document, a new WORKING version of the document is created based on the revision scheme. The previous document version is set to
Superseded but is kept for history.
Edit Files allows you to download master files and copy files referenced by it, along with their nested reference files. As an administrator, you can
prevent the copying of nested reference files by configuring methods of client API EditFiles. For more information, see Configure Edit Files action to filter
nested reference files.
Replace the files for a document
You can attach and replace files that are attached to a document in the Working revision state.
How can I check in or replace a file along with content extraction?
 By default, this feature is not enabled. However, your administrator can change the default behavior to enable content extraction for the Check
in and Replace Files methods. For more information, see Configure content extraction on Replace Files and Check in methods.
To allow content extraction whenever you check in a document or replace a file related to a document, after attaching the file in the New Files page, you must:
1. Set any replaced file or checked in file as the Master, by clicking Set as Master to mark the file for content extraction and display the Extract Content
window.
2. In the Extract Content window,
a. Select a template group from the TemplateGroup/Template list to apply the processing rules using the corresponding Preprocessor Reader.
b. Click Ok.
 By default, the file name is displayed in the Select File list.
 You can use preprocessed content files for processing the file. For more information, see Extract data from a document.
3. Click FINISH to successfully replace a file or check in a document along with content extraction.
 To check the status of the content extraction, you can click Notifications.
1. Select a document to revise, and select Actions 
 > Replace Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Select CHOOSE FILES to select the files to attach to the new revision.
4. Select Copy Markups to copy any existing markups with the selected files, and click FINISH.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of uploaded files must not exceed 2GB. The
application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these limitations.
The file names being attached need to exactly match the name of the files being replaced.
If the new master file references other files, the reference files along with master file are attached to the document.
When replacing files for a document, a Reference Files Details window is displayed in the following scenarios:
If a master file references a file that is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the
appropriate reference file. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files and and Working with reference files and
relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the system, the missing files are shown in the Reference Files Details window. In such cases,
the file upload processing continues or aborts, but you can change the default behavior of file attach by adjusting the respective method
parameters. For more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
The following image shows an example of the Reference Files Details window which displays the duplicate and missing reference files
information.
Show Reference Files Details window
Sign off a document
When edits to a document are complete, you sign off the working revision, which sets the document as the CURRENT released revision and prevents users
from creating further working versions for that revision.
What happens when I sign off a document?
Signing off a document finalizes it:
The status for the revision is set to CURRENT. Any previous released revisions are SUPERSEDED.
The document is set to be the current released version and is made official.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
Document revisions that have been signed off and current are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
1. Select the document revision to sign off.
2. Select Actions 
 > Sign Off to display the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window.
3. If the document revision to be signed off is a:
Master document, then the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window displays the list of master documents along with their reference documents.
You can select a document revision from the list of documents to sign off, but you cannot select its reference documents as the software displays
them in the view mode.
Reference document, then the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window displays the document revision.
4. Select OK.
You cannot sign off a document revision with attached reference files in the SUPERSEDED or WORKING revision state. However, you can proceed with
the sign off, if the updated revisions of reference files associated with this document exist, but are not associated with this document. In such scenario,
you can update the reference relationships for its reference documents by following the steps given in Update reference relationships.
For a reference document, if the associated master document refers an out-of-date revision of the reference document, after signoff, an Update
ReferenceBy Relationships window is displayed. You can use the window to relate the master document with the latest revision of reference document.
For more information, see Update referred by relationships.
Before you sign off a document, it must be checked in.
To make changes to a signed off document, the document must be revised.
Select Undo Sign Off to cancel signing off the document.
You can update the collaborating organizations using the Update Collaborating Organizations method available in the Actions menu. This option is
not available for Administration document classifications.
Update a document
You can update the details of the document when it has a working version..
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update Document.
 You can also use Update Latest Revision command.
3. On the Update form, select the document properties, and values to update. Any related items are listed in separate tabs.
You can update the collaborating organizations in the Collaboration tab on the Update form. This option is not available for Administration document
classifications.
If you rename a document, the unique identifier definition (UID) or unique key definition, as well as the UIDs used in relationships, are also updated, as
necessary.
How do I assign an organization to multiple documents?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple documents:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the documents to the organization.
Sign off with form
The Sign Off with Form allows you to sign off on documents using a form in which you can update properties and add revision notes.
What happens when I use sign off with form?
When you use the sign off with form for a document:
The revised document allows you to update properties and add revision notes using a form.
The document is set to be the current released revision.
The status for the latest revision is set to CURRENT and the revise state is set to blank.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
The signed off revision of the document supersedes any previously released revisions.
Document revisions that have been signed off are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
The display items exposed on the sign off form are configurable. For more information, see Configure forms.
1. Select the document to sign off, and update the properties or add revision notes as necessary.
2. Select Actions 
 > Sign Off with Form.
You can sign off multiple selected documents and any changes made are applied to all of the selected documents.
You cannot sign off a document that has been reserved for editing without checking it in first.
To make changes to a signed off document, you must revise it and create a new Working version.
Undo sign off
When you undo sign off on a document, the latest WORKING revision is reverted back to the previous WORKING revision.
 This an administration command for document controllers only.
Select Actions 
 > Undo Sign Off
Insert a historical revision
The Insert Historical Revision command allows you to insert a superseded revision in a document between the current and superseded revisions or between
two superseded revisions. The revision is inserted with a date that falls before the selected revision and after the previous revision.
 Revised documents must be signed off before they can have a historical revision inserted.
1. Select a document revision before which you want to insert a historical revision, and select Actions 
 > Insert Historical Revision.
What can you see in the Insert Historical Revision page?
In the Insert Historical Revision page, you can see the following sections:
Historical revision information - displays the revision scheme, the major revision, the minor revision, and allows you to choose the issue date
before the selected revision date.
Previous revision information - displays the major revision, the minor revision, and the issue date of the SUPERSEDED revision (read only).
Selected revision information - displays the major revision, the minor revision, and the issue date of the CURRENT or SUPERSEDED revision
(read only).
2. In the Insert Historical Revision > Historical revision information section, select an issue date for a revision before the creation date of the selected
revision of the document, and click NEXT.
3. In the New Files page, choose the revision(s) to insert in the document, and select FINISH.
Cancel a document
You can only cancel a document when it is in the RESERVED state.
 This an administration command for document controllers only.
1. Select Actions 
 > Cancel.
2. Enter the reason for canceling the document in the Cancel Document(s) dialog box.
When you cancel a document, any steps attached to a workflow are also terminated.
You can see the details of the canceled document on the History tab of the Object Details form.
Primary and reference documents
You can distinguish between the different documents that appear in a communication action, comment, review or response, or query:
Primary documents are the main subject of a review or response and require action, comment, or query.
Reference documents are included for reference or provide additional information to support a review or response, action, comment, or query.

You can work with files in the document life cycle.

The viewing, conversion, hot spotting, printing, and redlining of files secured with Microsoft Azure Information Protection (AIP), or Information Protection
in general (MIP), is not supported. You can attach, store, and manage those protected files with SDx.
The viewer does not support Office 95 or earlier files. You must save such files in later Office versions before working with them.
How do you use the different edit commands?
The following table shows the different edit commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Edit Files or Download and Edit Files
Creates a new document version. Checks out the new version, and downloads it to a client device
along with copies of attached files for editing.
Check In
Checks in the files after being checked out for editing using Edit Files.
Replace Files or Revise and Replace Files
Creates a new document version, and allows you to update the document by replacing any attached
files.
Manage Files
Updates the existing document version so you can manage the attached files using remove, add, or
delete.
Save Target As
Downloads and saves a master file and any files referenced, including any nested reference files, to
a client device.
Undo Edit Files
Removes any new version created by the check out. Sets the most previous version back to Working
 This option is available for the Document Controller role only.
Edit files
When you want to edit a file attached to a document, the file is saved to the download location on your device for editing.
1. Select Actions 
 > Edit Files.
2. On the Select files to download page, select the file you want to edit.
The download operation can vary depending on the browser used. You can open the file and select Save, or save the file directly to your local device.
The document the file is attached to is set as undergoing change and the file is set as reserved for edit.
When you edit a file attached to a document, a new WORKING version of the document is created based on the selected revision scheme.
When you cancel an edit, the files on the local device may not be automatically deleted.
Edit Files allows you to download master files and copy files referenced by it, along with their nested reference files. As an administrator, you can
prevent the copying of nested reference files by configuring methods of client API EditFiles. For more information, see Configure Edit Files action to filter
nested reference files.
 You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
Replace files
You can replace files in a document with files with the same name that are already attached to a WORKING version.
How can I check in or replace a file along with content extraction?
 By default, this feature is not enabled. However, your administrator can change the default behavior to enable content extraction for the Check
in and Replace Files methods. For more information, see Configure content extraction on Replace Files and Check in methods.
To allow content extraction whenever you check in a document or replace a file related to a document, after attaching the file in the New Files page, you must:
1. Set any replaced file or checked in file as the Master, by clicking Set as Master to mark the file for content extraction and display the Extract Content
window.
2. In the Extract Content window,
a. Select a template group from the TemplateGroup/Template list to apply the processing rules using the corresponding Preprocessor Reader.
b. Click Ok.
 By default, the file name is displayed in the Select File list.
 You can use preprocessed content files for processing the file. For more information, see Extract data from a document.
3. Click FINISH to successfully replace a file or check in a document along with content extraction.
 To check the status of the content extraction, you can click Notifications.
1. Select a WORKING revision document, and click Actions 
 > Replace Files.
2. In New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Select CHOOSE FILES, and select the files to attach to the revision.
4. Select Copy Markups to copy existing markups on to the attached files, and select FINISH.
The file names being attached must exactly match the name of the files being replaced.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of uploaded files must not exceed 2GB. The
application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these limitations.
Replace Files creates a new WORKING version for the revision. The previous WORKING version is set to superseded.
When attaching a master file, if it references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the
appropriate reference file displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files and
Working with reference files and relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For
more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
Manage files
This command allows you to manage the files on an existing WORKING version without creating a new one. You can add new files or delete existing files.
1. Select a row item, and click Actions 
 > Manage Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system tab.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files, and click FINISH.
 You can access other local systems from which to upload files.
You cannot upload certain file formats or file name extensions. For more information, see Site Settings node properties.
If you close the File Management page before files have successfully uploaded, any files still in the queue are not uploaded.
When attaching a master file, if it references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates displayed in the Reference Files
Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files and Working with reference files and relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For
more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When downloading files that are attached to multiple documents, if more than one file has the same name in the downloaded zipped folder, the following
scenarios apply:
If the duplicate file does not have any reference files, it is automatically downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2
copies of the file MyDoc.rtf attached to multiple documents are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the file is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
If the duplicate file has reference files attached, then the duplicate file along with its reference files are downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the
zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the Master.dgn
along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
 If you want to search a Hexagon 3D model, you must attach the files in a specific order so that the label database file (.mdb2) is automatically
generated. You must attach the corresponding .xml file to the document before attaching the 3D WebGL viewer file (.binz).
Manage signed files
Upload signed files from a local system to manage them on an existing signed off or working revision and access other local systems.
1. Select a row item, and select Actions 
 > Edit > Manage Signed Files.
2. In the Attached Files pane, select the row item you want to manage.
3. In the Local File System pane, drag the signed file or select CHOOSE FILES to select the file.
You can attach only one signed file to a native file. If a signed file already exists for the row item, this new file will replace the existing one.
To delete an existing signed file, select 
 next to the signed file name.
When you revise a document, the signed file is not automatically copied and related to the corresponding native file on the new revision. You must
manually relate the signed file.
You cannot upload certain file formats or file name extensions. For more information, see Site Settings node properties.
If you close the Signed File Management page before the files have successfully uploaded, any files still in the queue are not uploaded.
 We recommend that you have unique names for the signed files attached to a document and that the signed file name be different from that of the native
file name.
View or save a signed file
To view or save the signed file related to a native file:
1. Select a document, and select Actions 
 > Show Viewable Files.
2. Select a row item, and select Actions 
 > Show Signed File.
3. To save the signed file select Actions 
 > Save Target As.
Check in files
When a document has been checked out to edit the files, the check in allows you to copy the edited files from the local system back to the server for storage.
1. Select the document version you want to check in, and click Actions 
 > Check In.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, and select the files to be checked in from the list and its displayed reference documents.
You can only select a checked out document version to check in.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of uploaded files must not exceed 2GB. The
application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these limitations.
4. Unselect Copy Markups if you do not want to attach markups on files in the previous working version to the updated files in the new working version.
5. Click FINISH.
The modified file must be uploaded with its original object name. You can see the check in details in Object Details > History tab, which lists the object's
history details, such as creation date.
When checking in master files, if the files referenced is attached to multiple documents, the information about the duplicate files is displayed in the
Reference Files Details window.
When checking in, if the master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing reference files are displayed in the
Reference Files Details window.
Undo edit files
 This is administration command for document controllers only.
If the files attached to a WORKING version are being edited, you can select Undo Edit Files to revert the version and its attached files back to the previous
version iteration.
1. Select Actions 
 > Undo Edit Files.
2. Select Yes to undo the edit.
You do not see the Undo Edit Files action for any document revision with only one version, as this action is controlled by a condition in the method so
the system will not delete the revision.
You can change the behavior of Undo Edit Files command by configuring the UndoEditFiles client API. For more information, see UndoEditFiles client
API.
 You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
Revise and replace files
When a selected document is a CURRENT revision, this creates a new WORKING version of the document. You can either replace the files that are attached
to the previous revision or attach new files to the new version.
1. Select a row item, and click Actions 
 > Revise and Replace Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files, and click REVISE.
When replacing a file for a document, the Reference Files Details window is displayed if:
A master file references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching appropriate
reference file displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files and and
Working with reference files and relationships.
A master file references the files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details
window. In such cases, the file upload processing continues or aborts based on the configuration of the method for the ReplaceFiles client API. For
more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
Download attached files
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Download attached files (modified in an update).
You can download selected files and save them to a location of your choice.
1. Select Actions 
 > Save Target As.
2. In the Select files to download page, select the files you want to download. The files are displayed in pages based on the total number of files returned.
 Non-viewable files are also displayed, which you can also download as required.
Save Target As returns all the files attached to the object independent of the default query page limit, such as the downloaded master file, any reference
files, and any nested reference files. For each document, the master file and its corresponding reference files are downloaded together in a single zipped
file.
Single files without any reference files are downloaded individually without any folder structure.
The maximum number of nested reference files that can be downloaded is set in the User Preferences. For more information, see Set the user options.
If you are not prompted to select a location when saving a file, the file will be saved to your personal Downloads folder or to the default download location
defined in your domain user profile.
When downloading files that are attached to multiple documents, if more than one file has the same name in the downloaded zipped folder, the following
scenarios apply:
If the duplicate file does not have any reference files, it is automatically downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2
copies of the file MyDoc.rtf attached to multiple documents are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the file is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
If the duplicate file has reference files attached, then the duplicate file along with its reference files are downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the
zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the Master.dgn
along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
Generate PDF renditions of files
 You can generate PDF renditions from one or more files attached to one or more documents. If configured, any associated reference files, markup
layers, watermarks, or labels are included.
There are two ways you can generate a PDF rendition:
Select a document in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate PDF Copies.
Select a file in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate PDF Copy.
You can consolidate two or more files into a single merged PDF rendition from the results grid using the following three ways:
Select a single document with two or more attached files, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Select two or more documents with attached files, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Select two or more files, and select Actions 
 > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Renditions in the SDx Projects workflow
In the collaboration process, a rendition is an alternate representation of the original document version that is created during the document review
consolidation process. Each rendition object is an alternate document version that is linked to the PDFs generated from the original file. Every alternate
rendition contains the markups and data applicable to a specific action or technical query. Each rendition file:
Records exactly what is issued to the contractor in a PDF with any embedded markups, action pins, watermarks, and labels.
Creates relationships to the original document and the project communication on which the rendition is issued.
Maintains access control so the recipient contractor can view and markup only renditions associated with their communication items.
During consolidation, the consolidator reviews the markups using the Manage action markup layers dialog box and selects the applicable markups for each
action in the document rendition object. The contractual compliance specialist then reviews the markups for compliance using the Manage action markup
layers dialog box and communicates with the consolidator to reach agreement on the final rendition.
After agreement is reached, the final PDF rendition of the document is created by a workflow process step. The document has separate rendition objects for
each action to ensure that the contractor sees only the markups that are relevant to each action in their communication.
If a single document is related to multiple concurrent work streams, you can potentially have multiple markups on the files for different purposes. A
rendition object is created for each purpose filtering out any markups that are not relevant.
The creation of a rendition file also applies to a standalone technical query or action outside of the document review process. The originator selects the
relevant markups to be made into the rendition and on completion, a process step submits the rendition object to a workflow to create the PDF rendition.
Generate thumbnail images of files
You can generate thumbnail images for 2D or 3D files attached to a document by completing the following steps:
How can I generate thumbnails for a document?
 Before generating thumbnails for a document, check with your administrator to ensure that following method
FileCompositionGenerateThumbnail is assigned to your username. This setting will display the Generate Thumbnail method in the document's Actions
menu.
1. Select a document and click Actions 
 > Show Files.
2. Select a file and click Actions 
 > Generate Thumbnails to generate thumbnails for the supported file types.
 To view the generated thumbnails for a file, you can click Show Thumbnails under the file's Relationship menu.
By default, you cannot generate a thumbnail for a file which already has one. However, if you are an administrator you can change the default behavior
to allow the regeneration of thumbnails, by updating the GenerateThumbnail client API.
If you are an administrator, you can enable auto generate thumbnails whenever a document is created, revised and checked in.
The thumbnails of a file are especially useful when you want to view the content of the file without opening it.
To view the status of thumbnail generation, you can view the List of Notifications.
Working with reference files and relationships
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | How to work with reference files
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/66348b14eaa969016ab22d0c>
In SDx, certain file types, such as MicroStation files, AutoCAD files, and Word files, are called master files, and when attached to a document, can reference
other files. The files referenced by the master file can be viewed and edited separately and can reference other files, which creates a nested reference
relationship.
The relationships are maintained between the master files and their referenced files using reference relationships during check-in, editing, viewing, and so on.
These reference relationships are created between the master files and the referenced files when a document is created and has files attached or when a
master file is checked back in after editing. These reference relationships are created and updated if you add, edit, or delete any reference files when you edit
a master file.
When a master file is attached or checked in, the software reads the headers of all the files. The software first searches for the reference files attached to the
master file and then searches for any referenced files that are attached to other documents to create any reference relationships. If the software does not find
the reference files, or finds duplicate reference files, you must resolve these issues to create the reference relationship by default.
Your administrator can change the default behavior to stop, report any issues, or continue with the file attachment and resolve any issues later. For more
information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
The following diagram shows various reference relationships between documents and files.

Attach master file which references other files
You can create, revise, check-in, signoff or replace a file for a document with a master file that references:
an existing file in the database which is attached to more than one document.
more than one file with the same name that is attached to different documents or revisions.
When attaching the master file, a Reference Files Details window is displayed in which you can resolve the duplicates as follows:
a. In New Files pane, select a master file to attach to the document.
b. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files.
c. In the Reference Files Details window, select one or more reference files to resolve duplicates, and attach the master file.
d. Select Apply
e. Click FINISH in the New Files pane to attach the master file by resolving duplicates and creating or resolving reference relationships.
Which Microsoft Office file formats are supported for reference files?
The latest Microsoft Office file formats such as docx, docm, pptx, and pptm are supported for reference files.
In the Reference Files Details window, you can:
Click View and Markup 
 to display a reference file inside the View and Markup window.
Click the hyperlink to display the reference file details on a form.
Hover on Show the detail form 
 to display the reference file type.
View the file type of the duplicate reference files. However, the file type of the missing reference file is not shown.
If the master file and reference file are attached to the same document, the software automatically creates a reference relationship, without searching for
other instances of the reference file attached to different documents. In such cases, the Reference Files Details window is not displayed.
If a master file references other files, the number of reference file relationships levels that are resolved is limited by the Nested reference file level
setting defined in the system options. For more information, see Configure the system options.
When attaching the master file, the software resolves and attaches the files with cyclic references when the Cyclic references allowed option in the
system options is set to True. For more information, see Configure the system options.
If the master file references files that do not exist in the database, then the missing reference files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window.
If you have administrative privileges, you can configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files in the Reference Files Details window. For
more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
View reference relationships
To view the reference relationships for a document
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Relationships.
3. On the Relationships menu, select Version Relationships > Show Latest Reference Relationships.
To view the reference relationships for a file
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Relationships.
3. On the Relationships menu, select Show All Files.
4. Select a file, and select Actions 
 .
5. On the Relationships menu, select Show Referred by Files to view the files referenced by the master file.
To view other documents that the reference file is attached to:
1. On the reference file, select Actions 
 > Relationships.
2. On the Relationships menu, select Show Attached To.
View nested reference documents
To view the list of nested reference documents associated with a document
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. On the Reference Documents menu, select Show Nested References.
The Show Nested References window displays the referenced documents along with nested reference files and their documents in a tree view.
You can view the file type of the nested reference files in the Show Nested References window.
If you are an administrator, you can configure the maximum number of levels of the nested reference documents to be displayed. For more information,
see Set the user options.
In the Show Nested References window, for any document you can:
Click this
To do this
Display the documents referred to in a tree view
Actions 
Display the Action menu for the selected document
View and Markup 
Display the attached file inside the View and Markup window
Document Hyperlink
Display the document details on a form
View nested reference files
To view the files referred to along with their nested reference files:
1. Select a file for which you want to view the reference files
2. Select Actions 
 > Show Nested Reference Files.
The Nested Reference Files window displays the following:
Referenced files along with nested reference files in a tree view.
File type of the referenced files along with nested reference files
In the Nested References Files window, for any file you can:
Select this
To do this
Display the files referred to in a tree view
Actions 
Display the Action menu for the selected file
View and Markup 
View the file inside the View and Markup window
File Hyperlink
Display the file details on a form
Update reference relationships
To update the out-of-date reference files for a document with master file, you must:
Which roles have access to Update Reference Relationships method?
The list of roles which have access to the Update Reference Relationships method are given below:
Engineer
Expert
Manager
Planner
Operations document control
For more information, see the HxGN SDx Roles <https://docs.hexagonppm.com/viewer/document/KV2OpDSbTEp81JHaojREVQ> matrix.
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. Select Update Reference Relationships.
4. Select one or more reference files that you may want to update.
a. For each reference file, select the reference file version in the Update Reference Relationship to column. This will update the relationship with the
master document.
 You can choose from the out-of-date version, the latest released version, or the latest working version of the reference file.
b. Click UPDATE to update the reference relationships for the master document.
You can update out-of-date reference files for a file as follows:
a. Select a file.
b. Click Actions 
 > Update Reference Relationships.
c. Repeat step 4 as given above.
You can customize the display of reference files in the Update Reference Relationships window based on their reference relationship with the master
document by using the Show All Items On Update References Dialog option in Options 
. For more information, see Set the user options.
For more information about the columns in the Update Reference Relationships window, see the image and its description below:
Show Update Reference Relationships window
Column Name
Description
Name
Displays the name of the master document and a list of any reference
files associated with it.
Parent Revision Name
Displays the name of the parent revision.
Update Reference Relationship To
Select the version of the reference file to which you want to create a
relationship for the master document. You can choose from the out-of-
date version, the latest released version, or the latest working version
of the reference file.
Out of Date Revision
Displays the name, revision, and version number of the superseded
version to which the master file is currently related.
Latest Released Revision
Displays the name of the latest revision that was released for
distribution.
Latest Working Revision
Displays the name of the latest revision that has been created but was
not yet released for distribution.
Reference Control
Indicates the option selected for managing the relationship between
the master file and this references file. This field is display only.
Update referred by relationships
Which roles have access to Update Referred by Relationships method?
The list of roles which have access to the Update Reference Relationships method are given below:
Engineer
Expert
Manager
Planner
Operations document control
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx Roles
<https://docs.hexagonppm.com/viewer/document/KV2OpDSbTEp81JHaojREVQ> matrix.
To update the out-of-date reference relationships for the master document and relate with the latest reference document, you must:
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. On the Reference Documents menu, select Update Referred by Relationships.
4. In the Update ReferenceBy Relationships window, do the following:
a. Select one or more files attached to the referred master documents that you may want to update.
b. Click UPDATE to update the reference relationship of a master document with the latest reference document.
To update the out-of-date referred by relationships for the reference files, you must:
a. Select a file.
b. Click Actions 
 > Update Referred by Relationships.
c. Repeat step 4 as given above.
 For more information about the columns in the Update ReferenceBy Relationships window, see the image and its description below:
Show Update ReferenceBy Relationships window
Column Name
Description
Name
Displays the name of the master file referred by the reference file.
Parent Name
Displays the name of the master document to which the master file is
attached.
Revision
Displays the current revision of the master document.
Revision status
Displays the current revision status of the master document.
Reference Control
Indicates the option selected for managing the relationship between
the master file and this references file.
Working with Microsoft Office files using OneDrive
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx | How to work with Microsoft Office files using One Drive
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/62c6adfd843259e978afc199> .
SDx interacts with Microsoft OneDrive allowing you to collaborate easily with other users when working with files, leveraging the capabilities of Microsoft Office
365 and OneDrive for Business.
You can do the following:
Access the files from anywhere
Work on different devices to view and update the files
Collaborate with other users to work on the files
For more information on configuring Office 365 in your SDx system, see Configure Microsoft Office 365 authentication for a Web Client site.
For more information on troubleshooting Office 365 issues, see SDx and Office 365 integration troubleshooting.
Edit files online using Microsoft OneDrive
To edit files online using Office 365 and OneDrive, do the following:
Ensure that your Web Client site's URL is added as an exception in your browser's pop-up blocker, to enable files to be opened from OneDrive.
Ensure that you are logged into your Office 365 account. You will be prompted for your login and password if you are not logged into your Office 365
account.
What commands are available for editing files online?
The following table shows the different commands available for editing files online and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision
schema.
Action
What it does
Edit Online
Checks out the file, uploads it to your OneDrive. Creates new version.
Check In
Checks in the files after being checked out for editing using Edit Online and deletes the file from
your OneDrive.
Go to OneDrive
Opens the OneDrive directory containing the checked out files in a new tab on your web browser.
View Online
Opens the checked out file in a new tab on your web browser.
Undo Edit Online
Deletes any new version created and files uploaded to your OneDrive by the Edit Online option.
Checks in the previous version.
 This option is available only for the Document Controller role.
When you edit a file online, it is reserved for edit, and saved to your OneDrive for editing.
Select Actions 
 > Edit > Edit Online. The file is saved to your OneDrive.
 The file is opened for editing in a new tab on your web browser if the file type is configured for editing in the browser by your administrator. If not,
the OneDrive directory containing the file will be opened.
If you are accessing this feature for the first time, you will be prompted for your consent to allow the Web Client to access your OneDrive.
When you edit a file, a new revision or version of the object is created based on the object's revision status.
If the object state is set to CURRENT, a revision of the object is created.
If the object state is set to WORKING, a new version of the object is created.
If the document contains multiple files, the Select files to upload to OneDrive page is displayed to select the files to be checked out.
View a file online
When you check out a file for editing using the Edit Online option, and then close the file, you can use the View Online option to view the file and continue
editing.
Select Actions 
 > Edit > View Online.
 The file is opened for editing in a new tab on your web browser if the file type is configured for editing in the browser by your administrator. If not, the
OneDrive directory containing the file will open.
Go to OneDrive
When a file has been checked out for editing using the Edit Online option, you can use the Go to OneDrive option to view the OneDrive directory containing
the checked out file.
Select Actions 
 > Edit > Go to OneDrive.
Check in files from OneDrive
When you have finished editing a file that has been checked out using the Edit Online option, you can use the Check In option to check in the updated file
from your OneDrive to the SDx vault.
Select the document version you want to check in, and click Actions 
 > Edit > Check In.
You must ensure that the file in OneDrive is updated with your changes and not locked for editing while checking in to avoid any unexpected problems.
The modified file from your OneDrive is moved to the SDx vault and deleted from your OneDrive.
Undo edit files online
If a WORKING revision is being edited, selecting Undo Edit Online reverts the revision to the previous iteration.
 This is an administration command for document controllers only.
1. Select Actions 
 > Edit > Undo Edit Online.
2. Select Yes to undo the edit.
The checked out file is deleted from your OneDrive.
You must ensure that the file is not locked for editing during the Undo Edit Online operation to avoid any unexpected problems.
View and manage laser scans
 To use the Laser Scans feature set, you must install and set up a licensed version of HxGN SDx Connector for TruView software along with its
prerequisite software.
With the Laser Scans feature set, you can:
View and navigate across laser scans, sites, and servers.
View and manage laser scan tags.
View details for an outgoing action on the View and Markup page
What is the difference between an action, an action pin, and a pin?
Actions are observations made during a review that might require additional follow-up.
Action pins are markup elements you can add to a markup layer to create an action. The action pin is linked to the newly created action and is a graphical
locator for the action.
Pins are selected objects that you add to your Pinboard, so you can quickly access these objects to manage while you are working.
Why can't I view details of the action pin I just created?
The details of an action associated with an action pin are available in the changemark associated with the action pin. For that changemark to appear after you
create an action pin, you must first save and close the markup layer containing the action pin.
1. Select Markup 
, and select Save Markup.
2. Select Markup again, and select Close Edit.
3. Select Markup once more, and select Open for Edit or Open for Review.
The action pin details are now available.
The details of an action associated with an action pin are available in the changemark associated with the action pin.
1. Select the markup layer containing the action pin from the Available markup files dialog box.
2. Select the action pin image, and select Details 
.
 You can also click the Changemark 
 icon associated to the action pin to view details in the right panel.
Working with objects
You can work and manage objects in your projects and plants using actions configured for basic functionality.
Create and manage objects
1. In the relevant feature set, select Create [object type].
 You can also create an object and relate it to the current object by selecting Actions 
 > Create [object type]. To create and relate a comment to
an object, the command is Actions 
 > New Comment.
2. On the Create object form, select the property details from the different tabs available, and type the values required that apply to the new object.
3. In Select Files, add a file to the object if required, and select Upload.
When assigning classification to the object, you can use the Classification search bar to search and filter for the appropriate classifications based on
their Name and Description.
If your system is configured with Engineering Number System (ENS), you can create multiple objects and the number is incremented for each new object
created. You can review the ENS objects created in the Summary pane. For more information, see ENS preview summary.
If you create an object that is attached to a workflow, it is attached asynchronously.
Each object displays its property details under multiple tabs, such as Main Details, Structure, Source, History, and so on.
In a wizard, you can query the required object type and, when you click Finish, this object type will be related to the main object type.
If you are an administrator, additional information on creating and managing objects can be found in Administration and Configuration of HxGN SDx.
Copy an object
1. Select an object, and click Actions 
 > Copy.
2. On the Copy object form, select the required property details, and values displayed and give the copy a new name.
View the object details
Select the name of an object to open the object's Details page. Any related items are listed in separate tabs.
 You can also select an object in the Results table, and select Actions 
 > Show the detail form 
.
Update an object
1. Click Actions 
 > Update.
2. On the Update object form, select the properties and values to update. Other related items are listed in separate tabs, such as version and modification
information in the Source tab.
When you rename an object, the unique identifier definition (UID) or unique key definition, as well as UIDs used in relationships, are updated, as
necessary.
If you are an administrator, additional information on creating and managing objects can be found in Administration and Configuration of HxGN SDx.
Delete an object
What's the difference between deleting and terminating an object?
When you delete an object or relationship, it is permanently removed from the database.
When you terminate an object or relationship, the object's status is changed to Terminated, but its history is preserved in the database.
Click Actions 
 > Delete.
Terminate an object
Select Actions 
 > Terminate.
 You can view the history of a terminated object in the History Viewer. For more information, see Viewing the History .
Relate the objects in a wizard
In a wizard, you can query the objects that must be related to the main object and relate using the relationship maintenance type control. When you click
FINISH, the main object is created and related to the queried objects.
Create a relationship between objects
You can create relationships between objects using the Relationship Builder, which is a found in the Actions menu. You can use it to see how objects can be
related to each other and help you understand how data is connected in your project and system. For example, you can select a new equipment design
document and create a relationship to existing equipment tags or select a class definition and add related classifications.
Create and edit a relationship
1. Select objects in a results grid, and click Actions 
 > Relationship Builder.
2. In the Relationship Items section, add one or more objects to relate to the selected objects from the following tabs:
Pins - shows objects previously saved as pins in your Pinboard or newly created objects in New Items.
Existing - shows objects that are already related to the selected objects.
Search - search for objects in your system using specific search criteria and Filter options.
3. Select RELATE.
You can only see items that you have access to in the Existing tab or returned in the search results.
If any security rules are applied, only related objects that match the security rules are displayed in the Existing tab.
In a search, you can use the FILTER options to help you select the search criteria. The default behavior of the search is to use the Contains operator.
You can use the search operator Is Equal To to return more targeted search results. For more information on configuring the default search operator,
see Update an API Entry Point display.
Terminate an existing relationship
1. Select the object from which you want to terminate a relationship, and select Actions 
 > Relationship Builder.
2. Click the Existing tab, and select the existing related item from the list.
3. Select TERMINATE.
Managing related items for an object relationship
You can configure and manage related items for a specified object relationship using the Manage Related Items functionality. For more information on
managing related items, see Manage related items.
Managing Tags, Models, and Assets
A single physical item in a facility is represented by three related objects in the database:
Tag - The physical part as designed
Model - The requisitioned item, including a model number
Asset - The physical part located in the facility, with its own serial number
In the Web Client, you can:
Create tag, asset, and model documents.
 When tags are created manually or imported from another system, the software maintains the tag's uniqueness in relation to its area.
Manage and view relationships between the tags, models, and assets and the properties of each, such as specifications, manufacturer serial number,
maintenance procedures, and more. For more information, see Create and edit a relationship.
Create a tag
1. In the Tag, Assets and Models feature set, select Create Tag.
2. On the New Tag page, select the tag classification from the tree view.
 When assigning classification to the object, you can use the Classification search bar to search and filter for the appropriate classifications based
on their Name and Description.
3. Provide other details on the remaining tabs.
The Contract controls ownership.
The Originator is the organization that initiated the tag. The default is the user's organization.
The Collaborating Organizations selected can find and update the tags. Collaborating Organizations are dependent on the contract.
Security Code controls access - who can see what and when.
The options on the Structure tab creates the WBS/PBS relationships.
 If ENS is set up, the number is incremented for each new tag when you select APPLY.
How do I assign an organization to multiple tags?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple tags:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the tags to the organization.
Create an asset
1. In the Tags, Assets and Models feature set, select Create Asset.
2. On the New Asset page, select an asset classification from the tree view.
3. Provide other details on the remaining tabs.
 If ENS is set up, the number is incremented for each new asset when you select APPLY.
Create a model
1. In the Tags, Assets and Models feature set, select Create Model.
2. On the New Model page, select the model classification from the tree view.
3. Provide other details on the remaining tabs.
 If ENS is set up, the number is incremented for each new model when you select APPLY.
