in general (MIP), is not supported. You can attach, store, and manage those protected files with SDx.
The viewer does not support Office 95 or earlier files. You must save such files in later Office versions before working with them.

GVC requires reference files to be in the Web Client. For more information, see Working with reference files and relationships.
Navigating Hexagon 3D file formats
The following section details how to navigate the following 3D file formats:
 The following limitations exist:

Touch screen navigation can be limited when medium to large, dense models are loaded.

In this section:
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using the menu bar
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using touch gestures
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using navigation and selection commands
Measure distances in Hexagon 3D model file formats
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using touch gestures
How do I do things like double-tap, drag, or pinch?
When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.
Pinch
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
Press and Hold
Touch the screen for an extended period.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
 You can zoom, pan, and rotate at the same time by combining the gestures for each.
How do I select more than one object in a Hexagon 3D file format?
You can enable or disable multi selection by:
Double-tap anywhere in the model if you are using a touch-enabled device.
Double-click anywhere in the model if you are using a computer.
 Alternatively, you can press Ctrl and click the objects in the model if you are using a computer.
After you enable multi selection, you can tap or click each object you want to select. The number of objects selected is displayed in the sidebar. Only actions
applicable to all of the objects are displayed.
Navigation
Gesture
Return to Home View
Double-tap with two fingers to bring back the home view of the
model.
-OR-
Press and hold the Compass button.
Enter Fly mode
Tap Fly Mode 
 to enter. Your cursor changes to a crosshair
located at the center of your view to indicate that you are in Fly
mode.
Exit Fly mode
Press and hold one finger, and then lift it. Your normal cursor then
displays and not the crosshair.
Move forward, left, or right
Press and hold two fingers, and then drag them to move up, down,
left, or right.
Move backward
As you are moving around in Fly mode, lift one finger to move
backward.
Move halfway to target point
Double-tap with one finger to move the view automatically halfway
to the target point. Press and hold the SHIFT key while tapping with
one finger to move back out halfway from the target point.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them
apart.
Pan
Drag one fingertip from side to side or up and down to pan the
view.
Rotate
Drag one finger in any direction to rotate the model view. You can
move in a clockwise or counter clockwise direction to rotate the
model about the center of the main view. If you select one object,
the point of rotation is the center of the selected object. If you
select multiple objects, the point of rotation is the center range of
the selected objects.
Zoom in
Place your fingertips close together around the focus area, and
then spread the fingertips apart.
Zoom out
Place your fingertips apart around the focus area, and then pinch
or bring the fingertips close together.
Select one object in the model
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Select multiple objects in the model
Press CTRL on a virtual keyboard and select the objects.
-OR-
Double-tap in an area outside of the model section to start multi-
selection, and then select each object. To end multi-selection,
double-tap again in an area outside the model.
 If you do not have a physical keyboard, select CTRL on the
device's virtual keyboard. Use your device controls to open the
keyboard.
What can I do with Fly mode?
Here are some tips for using Fly mode:
As you move through the model, the closer you get to your target, the slower the movement rate becomes. The further away from the target, the faster
the movement rate.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Walk through your model in the direction of the crosshair. Roll the middle mouse wheel forward and back, or press and hold the designated WASD key.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
There are many keyboard, keypad, and mouse options for navigating Hexagon 3D file formats. The following sections group the options by the type of
navigation movement or action.
How do I select more than one object in a Hexagon 3D file format?
You can enable or disable multi selection by:
Double-tap anywhere in the model if you are using a touch-enabled device.
Double-click anywhere in the model if you are using a computer.
 Alternatively, you can press Ctrl and click the objects in the model if you are using a computer.
After you enable multi selection, you can tap or click each object you want to select. The number of objects selected is displayed in the sidebar. Only actions
applicable to all of the objects are displayed.
General options
Navigation
Action
Fly mode
F
Fit all
V
Home view
H
Redo
CTRL + Y
Undo
CTRL + Z
Open or close the Settings panel on the Menu bar
P
Expand Menu bar
M
Exit Measure and clipping
ESC
Isolate
CTRL + I
Turn measurement on and off
N
Set home
CTRL + H
Zoom Area
Z
Look options
These options change what you see just as if you were standing in the model and turning your head.
Navigation
Action
Up
I
Down
K
Left
J
Right
L
Move options
Navigation
Action
Up
E
Down
C
Left
A
Left arrow 
Right
D
Right arrow 
Around (up, down, left, and right)
Middle mouse drag
In (or forward)

Up arrow 
Mouse wheel forward
In and left
W + A
Mouse wheel forward + A
In and right

Mouse wheel forward + D

S
Down arrow

S + A

Out and right

Mouse wheel backward + D
Press and hold the key for the direction to walk.
Click the middle mouse button to move halfway in to your target point. Use SHIFT + middle mouse click to move back out the distance of the eye point
from the target. The perspective angle does not change.
Press and hold the middle mouse button to pan left or right. This allows you to move the model without changing the direction in which you are looking.
Rotate options
Navigation
Action
Left
*
Multiply * on keypad
Right
/
Divide key / on keypad
Rotate
Select and hold. The model rotates around the current
center point of the view.
Rotate around a selected object (orbit rotate)
Select and drag an object to rotate the model around the
selected object for a 360-degree view. However, in Fly
mode, you cannot rotate around objects.
If multiple objects are selected, the point of rotation is the
center of the range of the selected objects.
 Press W and then drag your mouse in Fly mode for enhanced control and higher precision turning.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using the menu bar
The following options are displayed on the menu bar:
 Menu
Displays a vertical menu bar containing commands and settings. Select to open and close the menu bar. You can also open and close the menu bar by
pressing M.
The following options are available when you select Menu 
:
 Isolate
Hides the entire model and displays only the selected objects. When objects are isolated, you can zoom in and out, rotate, or pan.
You can also press CTRL + I.
 Environment
A 3D model environment consists of selected lighting, background, and surface settings. You can use the default preset view, select from a list of preset
environments, or modify an environment as required.
The options available include:
 Presets
Provides a list of delivered preset environments with specific lighting, background, and surface (ground plane) settings.
All edits made to preset environments are not persisted.
Because changes to presets are not persisted, you can reset any Lighting, Background, and Surface edits by selecting another preset environment
and then re-selecting the preset environment.
 Lighting
Simulates variations in lighting to provide a more realistic view of the model. Lighting options can show a more naturalistic view of the physical structures
represented by model objects.
Model Brightness - Specifies the level of brightness in the model. For darker object colors, select a low brightness (exposure) percentage. For brighter
colors, select a high brightness percentage.
Ambient Exposure - Controls the intensity of the ambient light. Also, it controls lighting intensity when the Realistic Rendering visual setting is
activated. This option is activated by default.
Direction Lights - Turns on the horizontal light direction in the model and displays options for casting shadows, setting the light orientation angles, and
source lighting. The following actions are available while adjusting light direction.
Shadows
Turns on and off the ability to cast shadows.
Light Rays
Displays beams of light shining across the model to simulate the effect of light passing through dusty or foggy areas. Other lighting settings such as
the Intensity, Shadows, Orientation, and Sunlight affect the light ray display. Turning off Direction Lights automatically turns off Light Rays. If
you have both Sun Light or Moon Light options enabled, the software uses the sun to cast the light rays.
Lens Flare
Simulates lighting behaviors based on the way light bounces inside the lens of a camera. Like the Light Ray option, Lens Flare is visible when
facing the sun. For the moon or other low light settings, Lens Flare has minimal impact on the display. To enable the Lens Flare option, turn on
Direction Lighting under the Environment > Lighting settings.
1. Open your 3D model, and then select Menu 
 > Environment > Presets 
.
2. Select the Sunset environment. The default sun location for this preset is 12:00 noon.
3. Select the Lighting tab 
, and then make sure Direction Lights is enabled.
4. Turn on Lens Flare, and then move the view to face the sun making sure the model does not block the sun.
5. You can change the location of the sun and use settings such as Model Brightness and Ambient Exposure to further customize your
display.
The direction of the sun and the model elevation are settings that define the basis of the Lens Flare effect.
When you enable Lens Flare, your camera view must be facing the primary light source (sun or moon).
Make sure the model is not blocking the light source.
If you have both the sun and the moon enabled in the Lighting settings, turn the view towards the sun to get the flar effect.
Change any additional Lighting settings such as the Model Brightness and Ambient Exposure to further customize the flare effect.
Orientation
Sets the Rotation and Elevation angles for the light sources.
 Show/Hide Light
Turns on and off the selected (Moon or Sun) light source.
 Elevation/Rotation Lock
Locks both light source settings so that the angle between the sources is maintained. When unlocked, you can move each light source
independently to set the angle.
Rotation
Enables you to move a light source to the required angle. The rotation angle ranges from -180° to +180°. Use the slider or enter the required
rotation value. Select the circle representing the light source and drag it to change the position.
Elevation
Specifies the elevation position in setting the direction of the light source. The elevation angle range is from -90° to +90°. Use the slider or enter the
required elevation value. Select the source light circle in the vertical toolbar to set the elevation.
Properties
Defines the source light to edit (Sun Light or Moon Light).
Show Light - Turns on and off the selected (Moon or Sun) light. You can also select the Show/Hide icon 
.
Color - Defines the color of the selected light source.
Rotation - Enables you to move a light source to the required angle. The rotation angle ranges from -180° to +180°. Use the slider or enter
the required rotation value.
Elevation - Specifies the elevation position in setting the direction of the light source. The elevation angle range is from -90° to +90°. Use the
slider or enter the required elevation value.
Intensity - Determines the exposure of light on the model, which changes the intensity of the model colors. Use the slider or enter a
percentage value.
 Background
Select either a solid Color, Gradient, or a SkyBox image background for the display.
 Surface
Enhances the model view by providing the effect of a ground, a floor, or other surface types for better depth and spacing perspectives of model objects. You
can define a solid color for a surface or select a texture image.
Visible - Turns on and off the selected surface display.
Elevation - Specifies the height of the surface plane in the model. The elevation measurement units depend on the measurement units selected for the
model.
Color - Defines the color. Use the RGB sliders or enter the hexadecimal color code.
 Select
Selects graphical objects and displays the following Floating Action Bar (FAB).
FAB Option
Description
Select FAB
Expands and collapses the Select control. When Fence Select is activated,
the software suspends mouse and touch navigation.
Select (Expand/Collapse)
Displays the Select control at the bottom of the view, and automatically
activates Fence Select. The software suspends mouse and touch navigation,
and default selection.
Fence Select
Turns fence selection on and off. When Fence Select is activated, the
software suspends mouse and touch navigation. Use commands in the Select
control to define fence selection options.
Inside Select
Selects objects that are entirely in the fence box. This command is activated
by default when Fence Select 
 is enabled.
Overlap Select
Selects all objects entirely inside the fence box and any objects that are
outside but touching the fence.
Cancel Select
Cancels and removes the current fence selection.
 Undo
Sets the view to the previous one. Use Undo to move back to the previous positions. You can also Undo by pressing CTRL + Z.
 Redo
Reverses your last view position change. You can also Redo by pressing CTRL + Y.
 Refresh
Updates the view with the latest data. Select Refresh whenever data from the source has been modified to see the latest model view.
 The 
 Refresh command reduces viewing performance since this re-runs the workspace query that creates the graphic tree structure.
 This command is only available in 3D Project As-Built (PAB) environments if an administrator has configured Force Refresh. For additional
information, see Force Refresh to update graphics in a Smart 3D model.
 Settings
Provides various settings to control the display and shows system information. Use the Information and Diagnostics settings to troubleshoot display
problems. You can open and close the Settings panel by pressing P.
The following options are available when you select Settings 
:
Visual
Projection Mode - Select either Orthographic or Perspective model view.
Anti-Aliasing - Reduces the jagged edges of object surfaces to provide a smoother finish.
Outline - Displays outlines on all model objects using the default outline color, which is white on dark backgrounds and black on light backgrounds.
Ambient Occlusion - Provides a more realistic lighting for objects by accentuating small surface details and adding soft shadows.
Transparency - Turns transparency on and off in the model. By default, transparency is turned on.
Optimize Performance - Automatically turns off all rendering effect settings such as Outline and Anti-aliasing during navigation to provide a smoother
and faster location transition. For 3D model viewing, this option is activated by default.
Fly-To - Provides a "fly-to" camera mode in which the camera moves from one view to another instead of an instant transition. When activated, this
option applies to the Fit, Home, Undo, and Redo commands.
Realistic Rendering - Applies reflectivity and textures to objects in a model to improve the visual quality.
View Cube - Displays a six-faced view cube to change the view direction of your 3D model. To change the view, select a face or corner on the view cube,
or select the More button to choose the view type and direction. A selected view is not persisted in the model. When enabled, the view cube displays
only in a 3D model. The More button does not display on devices with small screens such as tablets or mobile phones.
What can I do with View Cube?
1. Load your 3D model. The model opens using its pre-defined default view.
2. Under Settings, select Visual, and then enable the View Cube option.
The view cube displays in the graphic view.
3. Select a corner on the view cube for a perspective or isometric view, or select a cube face for an orthographic view.
-OR-
Select the More button beside the view cube to select the Projection Mode and the required Planar (Orthographic) or Isometric view.
 The More button does not display on devices with small screens such as tablets or mobile phones.
4. You can turn the option on or off at any time. A selected view is not persisted in the model.
Projection Mode options within View Cube:
Orthographic
Provides an orthographic views (front, top, bottom, and side) of the 3D model.
Perspective
Displays a 3D object on a 2D surface. Objects that are further away appear smaller, and closer objects are larger.
Display Axis - Turns the display axis on and off.
Navigator - Turns on and off the navigator display.
Continuous Highlight - Highlights graphics in the view when you hover your mouse over them. The highlight color is the color of the object. This option
is not enabled by default.
Information
Provides product information such as version numbers and data sources for the current model.
System Scan
Displays information such as the estimated available RAM, the number of threads in use, worker threads in use, and the maximum number of download
threads.
Diagnostics - Checks your network connection and performance.
Diagnostics Mode - Turns on and off a continuous check of your network connection.
Log - Displays the network connection. You can copy and delete the display.
WebGL - Provides information that can be used by customer support for troubleshooting purposes.
Aspects
Turns on and off the display of aspects. Aspects are parameters that represent additional information needed for placement, such as safety or maintenance
clearance.
 The Aspects option displays only for models containing aspects, and the aspects that display are those defined in the model file. If the file does not
contain the actual model geometry for the selected aspect, the aspect does not display.
 Home
Resets the display to the default view.
You can also press H.
 Fit
Zooms in to one or more selected objects. If you do not select any objects, Fit places all visible objects in the view.
If you have multiple items selected, this fits to the center of the selected objects.
You can also press V.
 Clip
Allows you to hide objects or portions of a model to focus on a specific area. HxGN SDx supports clipping and inverse clipping. Inverse clipping hides objects
inside a clipping volume and shows all objects outside the clipping volume.
 Measure
Turns measurement on (expanded control) and off (collapsed control). When expanded, use control commands to create point-to-point Distance
measurements or three-point Angle measurements. The measurement text displays the Δ (delta) notation for axis values ΔX, ΔY, and ΔZ to indicate
differences along each axis and see the directional breakdown of distance measurements.
How can I measure distances in Hexagon 3D model file formats
1. Select Measure 
.
2. Select Settings 
 to choose the measurement unit. You can also change the measurement unit after placing a measurement.
3. Select Angle 
 for three-point measuring. By default, the Distance measurement is enabled.
4. Select the first object or location point on the model.
5. Select the second point to see the distance measurement, or select the next two points for the angle measurement.
6. You can pause and resume measurement by selecting Place Point 
. Pause to perform other operations such as changing your view and selecting
other objects. When you resume, the measurement restarts from the next point you place.
The distance measurement text area displays the D (total distance) value with the option to expand the area to see the full measurement set (D, DX, DY, and
DZ values). The Expand 
 and Collapse 
 icons display beside the D value.
When you take measurements, you cannot interactively edit any control points. Turn off measurements to modify control points.
The following actions are available while taking measurements:
Expand/Collapse
Expands or collapses the measure control. When collapsed, measurement is turned off.
Start/Stop
Turns on and off placement of measure points. After you select the Distance or Angle measurement, select the first object or location of the measurement.
For Distance measurement, select the next object or location. The software displays the measurement between the two points. For Angle measurement,
select the object or location for the next two points. The software displays the angle between the three points. Select Place Point again to stop measurement.
Repeat this to start measuring at a different location in the model.
Angle
Measures the angle between three points to show the orientation of objects relative to each other.
Place Point
Turns on and off placement of measure points. After you select the Distance or Angle measurement, select the first object or location of the measurement.
For Distance measurement, select the next object or location. The software displays the measurement between the two points. For Angle measurement,
select the object or location for the next two points. The software displays the angle between the three points. Select Place Point again to stop measurement.
Repeat this to start measuring at a different location in the model.
Delete
Removes the last placed measurement.
To delete multiple measurements: Select Place Point to stop measurement. For each measurement to remove, select the measurement display, and then
select Delete.
Show/Hide
Toggles measurement display on and off. When you turn off the display, measurement placement is also off.
Settings
Displays the following options:
Units of measure
Selects the required measurement unit to be used when placing measurements. The default unit is meters (m).
Snap points
Draws the precision points as glyph and line geometries when you hover over 3D graphics. This option is on by default. You can toggle this option on and off.
As you hover over the object geometries, the software displays a tooltip identifying the precision point geometry type. The example figure below shows the
Edge tooltip indicating that the precision point is the edge of an object. Other tooltips that can display include EndPoint for a corner and MidPoint when you
hover over the midpoint of an object.
Exit
Select Measure in the menu bar to exit measurement and delete all current measurements.
 Fly
Represents a navigation mode in which you use the mouse and/or keyboard to move through the model. When you are in Fly mode, the cursor changes to a
crosshair at the center of your view.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Here are some tips for using Fly mode:
As you move through the model, the closer you get to your target, the slower the movement rate becomes. The further away from the target, the faster
the movement rate.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Walk through your model in the direction of the crosshair. Roll the middle mouse wheel forward and back, or press and hold the designated WASD key.
 Zoom Area
Enlarges the display in the view to a defined rectangular location.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using navigation and selection commands
The navigation and selection commands allow you to browse and review its models.
The following options are displayed on the navigation control:
Option
Description
Move in and move out
Select the + and – to move you forward and backward from the fixed center point of the current
view.
Compass
Select to face north.
Select and hold, and then drag to rotate the view around the center point.
2D View
Toggles the view between a top-down (2D) position (relative to the current center point) back to
the previous 3D view. The blue fill indicates when you are looking in a top-down view direction.
Walking View
Select the Walking View to rotate the view to a horizontal, forward-facing position. This locks the
view to the current elevation.
What can I do with the Walking View?
Here are some tips for using the Walking View:
To start a walkthrough, select Walking View, and then drag your cursor to a specific object in the view. When the object highlights, release or stop the
drag motion. The software updates the view and places you at the new location.
The blue fill indicates when Walking View is enabled.
To exit the command, select Walking View.
The elevation lock is enabled when you drag and drop the walking man icon.
Right-click to rotate the eyepoint around the center point of an object under the cursor.
Fly mode and the navigation IJKL keys allow you to look around and move the camera center point.
Navigation WASD keys move you through the model at the same elevation. If you select and fit to an object, the elevation changes to the elevation of
that object.
View tags by hierarchy
This functionality is available in HxGN SDx for Project As-Built (PAB) environments.
1. Tap Expand 
 to open the menu bar.
2. Tap  Select by Hierarchy.
"No hierarchy available" appears if one is not available.
The Select by Hierarchy panel replaces the Visual Intelligence panel on the right side of the display. Exit the panel to return to the Visual
Intelligence panel.
3. Select a node to display the tags belonging to that class of data.
The parent node is included when you select all of its child nodes.
You can tap 
 on a node to display or hide tags belonging to that class of data.
You can tap Actions 
 in the sidebar to create a change action or navigate to the Relationship Builder. All checked nodes are added to the
Actions menu.
4. Tap the background of the display to clear your selections.
View 3D Retrieve Filters
This functionality is available in HxGN SDx for Project As-Built (PAB) environments.
You can view 3D Retrieve Filters.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select 3D Retrieve Filters.
2. Select one of the following:
View and Markup 
 on a row in the search results table.
Actions 
 > View and Markup 
.
Create a snapshot of a 3D view filter, connection object, or streamed model
Using the Snapshot Gallery in the View and Markup window, you can work with markups for:
3D View Filters
3D connection objects via a Visualization Data Service (VDS) connection
Models streamed to HxGN SDx for Project As-Built (PAB) from the VDS
 From a workspace definition, you can view any related markup snapshots by selecting Actions > Show Markup Snapshots.
What can I do with a snapshot?
The Snapshot Gallery panel displays all snapshots created using a Hexagon 3D model. These snapshots appear as cards below the toolbar.
Using the Snapshot Gallery, you can:
Filter listed snapshots by owner.
Create a new snapshot.
Update, terminate, or delete a snapshot using the Actions 
 menu.
Select the thumbnail to zoom to that area in the model view.
Select the Markup Layers link to view, modify, or add markup layers.
Select the drop-down arrow to view snapshot properties.
 If open, collapsing the Snapshot Gallery panel closes the Actions 
 menu.
1 Collapse panel
2 Filter snapshots
3 Create a snapshot
4 Title of snapshot
5 Actions menu
6 Thumbnail
7 Mark up snapshot
8 Snapshot properties
Open the 3D View Filter you want to work with
1. Select Design Tool Integration > View Filters.
2. In the search results list, select Actions 
 for the View Filter.
3. In the Actions panel, select Actions > View and Markup.
Open the connection object you want to work with
1. Follow the instructions in Create a VDS connection.
2. Open the connection object using View and Markup.
Open the model you want to work with
1. Search for the document that has the attached model file.
2. In the search results list, select Actions 
 for the document.
3. In the Actions panel, select View and Markup 
 .
 If you do not know what document a file is attached to, you can search for the file instead and use its Actions panel.
Use the Snapshot Gallery to work with markups
1. Select the Snapshot Gallery - Snapshots panel to open the gallery if it is not already opened.
 If there are already snapshots of the model, the number of snapshots that you have access to is displayed on the collapsed panel.
2. Create a snapshot by selecting .
a. Enter the following details in the Create Markup Snapshot form, and select Finish when you are done.
i. Description: Type your own information for describing the snapshot.
ii. Is file markup locked: Check if you want to lock the markups created using this snapshot.
iii. Item owning group: Select an owning group for the snapshot and the markups created using this snapshot.
The snapshot appears as a card in the gallery panel.
3. Select the Markup Layers link on a snapshot card to switch to the markup view.
 If markup layers have already been added to the snapshot, the number of markup layers you have access to appears in the link.
For information on using markup tools, select Help 
 on the toolbar.
Navigating 2D file formats
The following section details how to navigate the following 2D file formats:
Non-Hexagon 2D File Formats:
AutoCAD Drawing (.dwg)
MicroStation Design File (.dgn)
Hexagon 2D File Formats:
SmartSketch (.igr and .sma)
Smart P&ID (.pid)
Smart Orthographic Drawings (.sha)
Smart Electrical (.spe)
Aspen Basic Engineering (.zyq)
 The following limitations exist:
The GVC is only compatible with 64-bit systems.
The GVC cannot load and display drawings greater than 100 MB for on-premises installations.
In this section:
Navigate 2D file formats using the menu bar
Navigate 2D file formats using touch gestures
View SmartFrame layers in a 2D drawing
View Smart Interop Objects in a 2D drawing
Navigate 2D file formats using touch gestures
How do I do things like double-tap, drag, or pinch?
When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.
Pinch
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
Press and Hold
Touch the screen for an extended period.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
 You can zoom, pan, and rotate at the same time by combining the gestures for each.
Navigation
Gesture
Pan
Drag one fingertip from side to side or up and down to pan the view.
Zoom In
Place your fingertips close together around the focus area, and then spread
the fingertips apart.
Zoom Out
Place your fingertips apart around the focus area, and then pinch or bring the
fingertips close together.
Select one object in the graphic
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Select multiple objects in the graphic
Press CTRL on a virtual keyboard and select the objects.
-OR-
Double-tap in an area outside of the model section to start multi-selection, and
then select each object. To end multi-selection, double-tap again in an area
outside the model.
 If you do not have a physical keyboard, select CTRL on the device's
virtual keyboard. Use your device controls to open the keyboard.
Navigate 2D file formats using the menu bar
 To open and close the menu bar, tap Expand 
 or press M.
The following options are displayed on the menu bar.
Option
Description
Home
Resets the display to the default view.
You can also press H.
Fit
Zooms in to one or more selected objects. If you do not select any objects, Fit places all visible
objects in the view.
If you have multiple items selected, this fits to the center of the selected objects.
You can also press V.
Markup
Toggle between viewer controls.
Zoom Area
Enlarges the display in the view to a defined rectangular location.
Undo
Sets the view to the previous one. Use Undo to move back to the previous positions. You can
also Undo by pressing CTRL + Z.
Redo
Reverses your last view position change. You can also Redo by pressing CTRL + Y.
Layers
Displays all sheet and SmartFrame layers in to the current deliverable associated to the model.
You can turn one or more sheet and SmartFrame layers on and off to filter the display and see
specific objects. When you turn sheet and SmartFrame layers off, you cannot see objects
assigned to the layers. If you turn off all SmartFrames associated to a sheet layer, the sheet layer
is also turned off.
Settings
Provides various settings to control the display and shows system information. Use the
Information and Diagnostics settings to troubleshoot display problems.
The options available include:
Information - Provides product information such as version numbers and data sources for
the current model.
Transparency - Turns transparency on and off in the model. By default, transparency is
turned on.
Display Axis - Turns the display axis on and off.
FlyTo - Provides a "fly-to" camera mode in which the camera moves from one view to
another instead of an instant transition. When activated, this option applies to the Fit,
Home, Undo, and Redo commands.
Visual:
Background - Select either a solid Color or Gradient background type for the
display.
Continuous Highlight - Highlights graphics in the view when you hover your mouse
over them. The highlight color is the color of the object. This option is not enabled by
default.
Optimize Performance - Automatically turns off all rendering effect settings such as
Outline and Anti-aliasing during navigation to provide a smoother and faster location
transition. For 3D model viewing, this option is activated by default.
Outline - Displays outlines on all model objects using the default outline color, which is
white on dark backgrounds and black on light backgrounds.
Soft Shading - Provides soft proximity shadows along narrow openings and intersecting
edges between objects in close proximity to each other.
Anti-alias - Reduces the jagged edges of object surfaces to provide a smoother finish.
Ambient Occlusion - Provides a more realistic lighting for objects by accentuating small
surface details and adding soft shadows.
Navigator - Turns on and off the navigator display.
Projection - Select either Orthographic or Perspective model view.
The following settings check your network connection and performance:
Diagnostics Mode - Turns on and off a continuous check of your network connection.
Log - Displays the network connection. You can copy and delete the display.
You can open and close the Settings panel by pressing P.
The following Floating Action Bar (FAB) is available at the bottom center of the viewer:
FAB Option
Description
Documents
Turns the sheet and layers display features on and off. You can see one sheet
at a time.
Layers
Toggles the selected layer display list on and off. You can select the layers to
view. Only objects assigned to the selected layer display. Turns sheet layers
on (expanded control) and off (collapsed control).
Sheets
Toggles the sheet display list on and off. The software displays sheets
available for the current drawing. Select each sheet to view.
View SmartFrame layers in a 2D drawing
1. Open the drawing. The Document control automatically displays below the drawing.
2. Tap Expand  > Layers 
 or tap Document  to expand the control, and then tap Layers 
. Layers available for the current sheet display in a vertical
pane.
3. Select the layer to view. When you select a layer, the software displays objects assigned to that layer.
4. Clear the layer check box to turn the layer display off. All SmartFrames and objects assigned to that layer are hidden.
5. When you have selected the required layers, tap the Back button on the vertical pane, or tap Layers 
 again in the Document control to close the layer
list.
View Smart Interop Objects in a 2D drawing
From a model view, you can view a Smart Interop Object in a 2D drawing.
1. Click a Smart Interop Object in a model view.
2. Click Menu 
 on the hotspot bubble, then View Item in Drawing on the Actions menu.
The 2D drawing displays, fitted to the object.
Navigating tags in split-screen mode
You can view a tag in both 2D and 3D files at the same time by selecting Show in Drawing or Show in Model on a tag's hotspot bubble. This split-screen
navigation offers excellent contextual information surrounding a tagged object. This reduces work time by providing large amounts of navigable data within a
single view.
To start:
1. Search for a tag or document in your system.
For help, see Search for data and objects in your system.
2. Open the tag or document for viewing.
3. Select a tag, and select Show in Drawing 
 or Show in Model 
 on the hotspot bubble.
If the tag is found in more than one file, select a file from the Pick the document to display for [tag name] dialog.
Only file formats supported in the Graphic View Control (GVC) can be viewed during split-screen mode. PDF file formats are not supported in the
GVC.
What file types are supported in the GVC view control?

You cannot access the Snapshot Gallery nor the Visual Intelligence panel during split-screen mode.
4. Select Close to exit the split-screen mode and return to the original view.
Show in Drawing or Show in Model opens the tag in the right viewing panel, highlights the tag, and zooms into it.
Tag selection is linked between viewing panels for easier navigation.
To adjust the viewing panels:
Double-tap the left or right split bar arrows.
Drag the split bar to the left or right.
Double-tap the split bar to evenly space.
Marking up and comparing your files
Marking up and comparing your 2D files is easy. The View and Markup Control delivers a number of features, which are listed below.
What is the View and Markup Control?
The View and Markup Control is a third-party tool utilized for View and Markup of certain file formats, including markup renditions (PDF).
Here is what's new if you are upgrading to the new View and Markup Control from the Brava! HTML Viewer:
You must enable the view control. For help, see Make sure your settings are enabled.
The stamps and symbols file types have changed from XSP to SVG. For configuration help, see Configure stamps and symbols.
Markup layers are saved as .xfdf files and the file type UID is FT_xfdf.
What file types are supported in the view and markup control?
The following file types are supported in this view control:
File format
Native application
File extension
CAD
AutoCAD Drawing
.dwg
AutoCAD Drawing Interchange Format
.dxf
MicroStation Design File
.dgn
Image
JPEG File Interchange
.jpg
Microsoft Windows Bitmap
.bmp
Portable Network Graphics
.png
Tagged Image File
.tiff
.tif
Microsoft Office
Excel
.xls
Excel Open XML
.xlsx
Excel (Macros)
.xlsm
PowerPoint
.ppt
PowerPoint Open XML
.pptx
PowerPoint (Macros)
.pptm
Word
.doc
Word Open XML
.docx
Word (Macros)
.docm
Text
Adobe Portable Document Format
 This includes markup renditions (PDF) generated from
2D drawings (.igr, .pid, .sma, .spe, .sha, and .zyq).
.pdf
Comma Separated Values
.csv
Text
.txt
Key Features
Navigate pages of a file, zooming in or out
Change how the file is displayed in the viewer
Pan and select objects within the file
Search text in the file
Add comments as well as view and search existing comments
Markup the file using stamps, symbols, or other annotations
Lock markups to prevent edits
Consolidate markups to avoid redundancy during document review
Download the file and markups locally
Print the required pages of the file with markups and watermarks
Compare files side-by-side to view differences
Things to Keep in Mind
You cannot view or navigate hotspots in a 2D file format when viewing and marking up the file. To view or navigate hotspots, view the file in the GVC.
Any embedded JavaScript in the PDF file is disabled for security reasons.
Several users can view and markup the same file at the same time.
A compare utility tool is delivered with HxGN SDx using Command Prompt.

In this section:
Navigate non-Hexagon file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
Navigate non-Hexagon file formats using touch gestures
Navigate non-Hexagon file formats using touch gestures
When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.
Pinch
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
Press and Hold
Touch the screen for an extended period.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
Navigate non-Hexagon file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
The following table sorts the action by navigation type.
Navigation
Action
Zoom in
Press CTRL + mouse wheel forward
Zoom out
Press CTRL + mouse wheel backward
Using the Markup Toolbar
From the Markup Toolbar, you can:
Download the document and markups locally
Print the document
Navigate pages of the document
Zoom in and out of the document
Change how the document is displayed
Pan and select within the document
Search the document
View and search multiple comments for editing
Select this
To do this
Menu
View and download the markups as comments and flattened.
Download the document (and any markups) as described in Download
a file with markup layers.
 Download (Markups as comments)
 Download (Markups flattened)
 Print
Print the document as described in Print a non-Hexagon file format.
Switch to view mode
Toggle the switch to view mode to view the original document to the
displayed document.
Panel
Toggle the panel open or close to view the following, if available:
Thumbnails
Outlines
Signatures
Zoom Controls
Zoom in to or out of the document. Use custom percentages to zoom or
enter a value.
Fit to Width
Fit to Page
Marquee Zoom
Page Controls
Page Orientation
Rotate Clockwise
Rotate Counterclockwise
Page Layout
Single Page
Double Page
Cover Facing Page
Display Controls
Change how the document is displayed.
 Black
Background Color
Converts the background to black and inverts the color of the content.
 The Background Color settings are ignored if you print or
download the document.
 Gray
Converts the background to gray.
 White
Retains the original background color.
 Original
Display Color
Retains the original color of the content.
 Grayscale
Converts the color of the content to different shades of gray.
 The Grayscale display color setting is ignored if you download the
document.
 Black and White
Converts the color of the content to black or white, excluding any shade of
gray.
The Black and White display option is available only when viewing
CAD file types (.dwg, .dgn, and .dxf). The option is not displayed when
viewing a non-CAD file.
The Main Scheduler must be enabled when using the Black and
White option.
Pan and Select
Toggle between panning around the document when the mouse is clicked
and dragged, and using the mouse to select items in the document.
 The currently selected option is shown in blue.
Search
Toggle the Search panel open or close to search the document for a
specific word or phrase.
Comments
Toggle the Comments panel open or close to view or search any
comments in the document. Multiple comments can be selected for viewing
or editing.
Download a file with markup layers
1. Select Menu
 from the Markup Toolbar.
2. Select a download option:
a. Download (Markups as comments) 
Only markups that are being reviewed or edited will be saved as editable comments in the PDF.
 Annotations change to a draft image if you download a file containing stamp, symbol, or image annotations into Adobe Acrobat with
Markups as comments selected and you rotate the annotations. This is an Adobe Acrobat limitation.
b. Download (Markups flattened) 
Only markups that are being reviewed or edited will be burned into the PDF, making the markups uneditable.
When you open a file in View and Markup, all layers must be fully loaded before users can interact with them, helping to prevent data loss or
rendering issues. The Download option remains unavailable while the layers are loading.
Print a non-Hexagon file format
1. Select Print 
 from the Markup Toolbar and select the required print options.
Print options
Option
Description
Pages to print:
All
Print each page of the file you are viewing.
Current Page
Print only the page you are currently viewing.
Current View
Print your current view. For example, if you are zoomed into a file, only the content that is
included in your view is printed.
 This is recommended for better performance.
Specify Pages
Print each page you enter in the text box. You can enter a single page number, a comma-
separated list, and/or a range of pages.
For example: 5, 9-15, 23, 62-86
Include comments
Include an additional page after each page printed. This page includes a summary of each
comment made on the printed page.
Include markups
Include all annotations in the markup layers that are open for review.
Print Grayscale
Print page elements, including annotations, in shades of gray.
Print Quality
You can select High or Normal print quality.
Watermark
Add a watermark to the printed file. See the "Watermark options" table.
Watermark options
Option
Description
Choose a location to edit watermarks
Select where the watermark is placed on each page.
Text
Enter the watermark text.
Font
Choose a font for the watermark text.
Size
Choose a font size (from 1 to 1600) for the watermark text.
Opacity
Set the opacity of the watermark.
Style
Choose a color, or set the watermark text to boldface, italicized, or underlined.
Reset All Settings
Resets to default settings and empties the Text field.
Cancel
Cancels changes and returns you to the print dialog box.
OK
Saves changes and returns you to the print dialog box.
2. When you are done, select Print.
When you open a file in View and Markup, all layers must be fully loaded before users can interact with them, helping to prevent data loss or
rendering issues. The Print option remains unavailable while the layers are loading.
Using the Markup Layers Manager
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Using the Markup Layers Manager
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63abcb25e0b03f43f0f762e9>
Markup layers are listed in the Markup Layers Manager panel, where you can:
Create, edit, consolidate, copy, or delete markup layers.
Add, delete, and edit annotations and Action pins.
View a list of elements belonging to each markup layer.
Select which markup layers you want to view or edit.
Lock or unlock markup layers, which disables or enables the markup layer for editing, consolidating, or deleting.
Display or hide markup layers, which disables or enables the markup layer for editing, copying, consolidating, or deleting.
What are markup layers?
Markup layers are database objects that are related to the PDF rendition of a file. Each markup layer can contain multiple annotation elements that are used to
add extra information, such comments, embedded callouts, or stamps and other graphical details.
What can I do in the Markup Layers Manager panel?
Click this
To do this
NEW LAYER
Create a markup layer.
CONSOLIDATE
Combine the markups on the selected layers into a new layer.
Create a new markup layer by copying the selected markup layer.
Delete one or more selected markup layers.
Stop editing the markup layer.
 This is only displayed when the layer is being edited.
Display or hide the markup layer. If a markup layer is hidden, you cannot edit,
copy, consolidate, or delete it.
Lock or unlock the markup layer. If a markup layer is locked, you cannot edit,
consolidate, or delete it.
 The Markup Layers Manager panel is hidden by default. Select 
 to expand the panel.

 You can configure custom file extensions of the supported file types through the Native file extension parameter. For example, isometric file types
have custom file extensions such as .i01, .i02, and .i03 for the .dgn native file extension. Custom file extensions such as t01, t02, and t03 use the .tif native file
extension. For more information, see Configuring custom file extensions.
In this section:
Create a markup layer
Edit a markup layer
View a markup layer
Copy a markup layer
Consolidate markup layers
Lock or unlock markup layers
Delete one or more markup layers
You can create and manage markup layers only in the scope in which the document is available. For more information, see Working with scopes.
When you open a file in View and Markup, all layers must be fully loaded before users can interact with them, helping to prevent data loss or rendering
issues. The Markup Layers Manager option remains unavailable while the layers are loading.
Create a markup layer
1. On the Markup Layers Manager toolbar, select NEW LAYER.
2. In the Create Markup Layer dialog:
a. Enter a Description.
b. Select an Item owning group and a Type of markup.
3. Select FINISH when you are done.
For annotation help, see Annotating a file using markup layers.
Markup layers remain in files attached to documents throughout a workflow.
Markup layers saved in a previous file format are automatically converted to a supported file format when viewing a file or when generating an alternate
rendition. You see a notification after the conversion.
Markup layers are automatically related to the markup rendition of a file. This applies to all file formats except PDFs, which do not require a markup
rendition.
View a markup layer
To view a markup layer, select it from the Markup Layers Manager panel.
The status is indicated as follows:
Status
Icon
Description
Displayed
The markup layer is displayed along with its annotations.
Hidden
The markup layer is hidden along with its annotations.
 To display a hidden markup layer, select Review Layer 
.
PDF files that contain third-party annotations created outside of SDx, such as embedded call outs, stamps, and so on, are displayed in the Default
Layer. The default layer is read-only and cannot be copied, modified, consolidated, or deleted.
If a markup layer is hidden, you cannot edit, copy, consolidate, or delete it.
Changing the visibility of a markup layer will have no effect on annotations hidden by default.
Edit a markup layer
To edit a markup layer, select it from the Markup Layers Manager panel. The layer must be either unlocked or visible.
For annotation help, see Annotating a file using markup layers.
 is displayed in the Markup Layers Manager panel to indicate the layer being edited.
You cannot hide a markup layer if it is marked for editing.
Layers must be under review to be edited, or the option will be unavailable.
Copy a markup layer
1. Select a single markup layer, and select Copy markup layer 
on the Markup Layers Manager toolbar.
2. In the Copy markup layer dialog:
a. Enter a Name and a Description.
b. Select an Item owning group and a Type of markup.
3. Select COPY. The markup layer is copied along with its annotations to a new layer.
 Layers must be under review to be copied, or the option will be unavailable.
Consolidate markup layers
To consolidate two or more markup layers into a single markup layer:
1. Select two or more markup layers from the Markup Layers Manager panel.
2. Select CONSOLIDATE.
3. In the Create Markup Layer dialog:
a. Enter a Name and a Description.
b. Select an Item owning group and a Type of markup.
4. Select FINISH when you are done.
 Layers must be under review to be consolidated, or the option will be unavailable.
Lock or unlock markup layers
You can lock a markup layer so the layer cannot be edited or deleted.
The status is indicated as follows:
Status
Icon
Description
Locked
The markup layer is locked from editing or deleting.
Unlocked
The markup layer is unlocked and can be edited or deleted.
Delete one or more markup layers
1. Select one or more markup layers from the Markup Layers Manager panel.
2. Select Delete Layer(s) 
.
 Layers must be under review to be deleted, or the option will be unavailable.

 to begin marking up the file. You can click Switch to view
mode to return to the previous viewer.

In this section:
Add annotations to a markup layer
Add action pins to a markup layer
Add dynamic stamps with token values to a markup layer
Format annotations in a markup layer
Add annotations to a markup layer
You can add different annotations, such as callouts, stamps, symbols, or uploaded images, to a markup layer using the annotations toolbar.
1. Select a markup layer from the Markup Layers Manager panel. Edit Mode 
 displays in the row.
 To exit edit mode, select Edit Mode 
 in the row.
2. Select an annotation type from the toolbar:
What are the different annotation types?
You can quickly and easily add annotations by selecting the annotation type from the annotation toolbar on the View and Markup page.
Type
Name
What is annotated
Text
Inserts typed text, text callouts, and so on. You can emphasis text in different colors.
Shapes
Inserts geometric shapes, straight lines, arrows, drawn lines, and so on. You can edit line
weight, opacity, and fill color.
Stamps
Inserts stamps, symbols, uploaded images, action pins, and dynamic stamps that can be
configured to display names and dates for approval and sign off or token values.
 Each annotation type has enhanced styling options that allow you to change colors, add comments, add links, and delete the annotation.
How do I make text annotations in a markup layer?
1. Select Text Annotations from the toolbar:
2. Select the required type of annotation from the Text Annotations toolbar.
Select this
To do this
 Highlight
Highlight the selected text.
 Underline
Underline the selected text.
 Strikeout
Add a line through the selected text.
 Squiggly
Add a squiggly underline to the selected text.
 Free Text
Add a text box.
 Callout
Add a text callout.
 Note
Add a note annotation.
How do I add shapes to a markup layer?
1. Select Shape Annotations from the toolbar:
2. Select the required type of annotation from the Shape Annotations toolbar.
Select this
To draw this
 Free Hand
A free hand line.
 Line
A straight line.
 Arrow
An arrow.
 Polyline
A line with multiple segments.
 Rectangle
A rectangle.
 You can change a rectangle into a cloud by changing the Style to
Cloudy. After you draw the rectangle, select the shape and select Style from
the toolbar.
 Ellipse
An ellipse.
 Polygon
A polygon.
 Cloud
A cloud. This is a polygon with a squiggly line as the border instead of a
straight line.
 For a better experience, press and hold the SHIFT key while drawing
the cloud shape. Double click when you are done.
How do I add a stamp to a markup layer?
1. Select Stamp Annotations from the toolbar:
2. Select the required type of annotation from the Stamp Annotations toolbar. Select:
 Stamp Image to select an image file to add to the markup layer.
 Stamps and Symbols to open the Stamps and Symbols tabs where you can select the required stamp or symbol to add to the markup
layer.
Some stamps prompt you to type some text that will be included in the stamp. For example, you may be requested to enter your name if a
stamp requires a signature.
Do not use the percent sign (%) in the stamp text.
You can view which annotations were added to a specific markup layer by selecting Expand annotations 
 in Markup Layers Manager panel.
You can locate annotations in a markup layer by selecting Locate annotation 
 in the annotation layer.
Add dynamic stamps with token values to a markup layer
Dynamic stamps can contain text strings or available token values. To add one:
1. Create a new markup layer or open an existing markup layer for edit using the Markup Layers Manager panel.
2. Select Stamp Annotations from the toolbar:
3. Select Stamps 
 from the Stamp Annotations toolbar.
4. Select a dynamic stamp from the Stamps panel, and click anywhere in the file to place it.
 The markup file is automatically saved.
The available tokens and their descriptions are listed in the table below.
Token
Description
%Time
The time the print was spooled based on a 12-hour clock (AM/PM). If the tags are viewed on screen,
the date on which the screen was last refreshed displays.
 Time is based on the browser locale.
%MilTime
The time the print was spooled based on a 24-hour clock.
 MilTime is based on the browser locale.
%Title
The name of the file.
%TotalPages or %Pages
The total number of pages.
%Page
The current page number.
%Hostname
The location.hostname of the server url hosting the site.
%SysDatePlusDays(n)
The date generated by a specified number of days past the system date. Replace n with the desired
number of days. Negative numbers are acceptable. Make sure you close the parenthesis when
adding the number of days.
%BatesPgNo(n)
Indicates the starting page number and the number of digits to use. Replace n with the desired
format and starting page number. For example, %BatesPgNo(0002) would place 0002 on the first
page, 0003 on the next page, and so on. Make sure you close the parenthesis when adding the
page number.
%Date
The date the print was spooled. If the tags are viewed on screen, the time at which the screen was
last refreshed displays.
 Date is based on the browser locale.
%Login
The login name of the user who is currently logged on.
%User
The user name of the user who is currently logged on.
%Prompt
Adds text strings entered by the user.
Format annotations in a markup layer
You can format existing annotations in a markup layer.
1. Select a markup layer from the Markup Layers Manager panel, and click Edit 
.
2. Click an annotation in the markup layer.
3. Select an option from the toolbar:
Click this
To do this
 Comment
Add a comment to the annotation.
 Style
Change the annotation style, such as color fill or line style.
 Delete
Delete the annotation.
 Link URL or Page
Link the annotation to a URL or to a specific page of the file.
You can view which annotations were added to a specific markup layer by selecting Expand annotations 
 in Markup Layers Manager panel.
You can locate annotations in a markup layer by selecting Locate annotation 
 in the annotation layer.
You can resize text annotations easily using the sizing handles on the text box by selecting Style > Text > Scale font size dynamically.
Comparing files using the viewer
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Comparing Files Using the View and Markup Control
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65658603989d2e938cc534aa>
You can see the differences between two files by comparing them in the viewer.
 You cannot compare files on a tablet or other mobile device.
What kinds of files can I compare?
Depending on how the software is configured for your company, you can compare these file types:
Hexagon 2D (.sma, .spe, .sha, .pid, .igr, .zyq)
CAD files (.dwg, .dxf, .dgn)
Word (.doc, .docm, .docx)
Excel (.xls, .xlsm, .xlsx)
PowerPoint (.ppt, .pptm, .pptx)
PDF (.pdf)
You can also compare different file types:
PDF to text
CAD, Word, Excel, or PDF to each other
You can compare files with any relationship to a document, including:
Files attached to a document
Reference files attached to any of those files
Alternate renditions of the files attached to a document
Alternate renditions attached directly to the document
 If there is more than one file that can be compared, you can tell which files are alternate renditions from the information that appears when you pick
which files you want to compare.
Why can't I see the text changes inline for all the files?
To show files containing a lot of text, such as Word or Excel files, the software uses a graphic representation of the file, like an electronic photocopy. That
means that the text is not available for the software to compare.
 You can generate PDF renditions and use the PDF renditions for comparison.
In this section:
Compare files attached to different documents
Compare design files
Compare text files
Compare files attached to different documents
If the files you want to compare are attached to documents that are difficult to find with a single search, you can use the Pinboard to compare them.
1. Search for each document, and select it in the Results table.
2. Click Actions 
 > Add to Pinboard.
3. Click Pins 
, and click View in Grid.
4. Select the two documents in the grid.
5. Click Actions 
, and choose either Compare Documents or Compare Documents as Text.
6. Use the toolbars to see the files in different ways or with different information, as described in the topics above.
Compare design files
1. Search for the documents with attached files that you want to compare.
2. Select the two documents in the Results table.
 If you want to compare files from documents that are difficult to find with a single search, see Compare files attached to different documents.
3. Click Actions 
 > Compare Documents.
4. If a list of files appears, all the retrieved results are returned in pages in the Select a file to view window. Select the two files you want to compare.
The two files appear side by side in the browser.
5. Use the toolbars to see the files in different ways or with different information:
If you want to
Then do this
See the files superimposed in one view
The superimposed view is generated automatically in the middle pane
with each file appearing in the left and right panes.
In the middle pane, shared graphics are colored in gray, graphics
appearing in the right file are colored in red, and graphics appearing in
the left file are colored in blue.
See just one of the files
Click Settings 
 on the toolbar in the pane, then select Full screen to
view the file in full screen mode.
Print one of the files
Click Settings 
 on the toolbar in the pane, then select Print 
.
 The superimposed view in the middle pane cannot be printed.
Align the graphics of the two files
Use the buttons on the nudge tool to scale, rotate, or translate the
graphics until the two files are aligned.
Zoom in or out of a file
Each pane has controls to zoom out or in:
 The three panes are synchronized so that they maintain the
same zoom level and graphics alignment.
Compare text files
You can compare the contents of two text files by doing the following:
1. Search for two documents, each with at least one PDF file attached.
2. Select the two documents and click Actions 
 > Compare Documents as Text.
If more than one PDF file is attached to one or both documents, the Select two files to compare dialog appears.
What can I do with the top pane?
The plain text content of the two files appears side-by-side in the top pane. Any differences in the two files are highlighted. You can expand condensed blocks
of text by clicking (...). The contents in the pane update as you navigate one of the original files loaded in the bottom pane.
What can I do with the bottom pane?
You can adjust the comparable text in the top pane by navigating one of the original files in the bottom pane. Use the power bar options in the middle of the
page to adjust your view.
Power Bar Options
Option
Description
[filename].pdf
Toggle your view between the two original files you are comparing
Navigate by thumbnails or outline
Page layout options
Zoom in or out of the original file
Navigate the original file by panning
Navigate directly to a page in the original file
Search for a word in the original file
Change settings, such as enter full screen mode, download the file you are
viewing, print the file you are viewing, or enter dark or light mode.
Using the hotspot bubble
Hotspot bubbles appear when you click tags in a published drawing or 3D model. The following options are available from the hotspot bubble:
Option
Description
Action
Opens the Actions menu for the selected object.
Info Map
Opens the Info Map for the selected object in a new tab.
For more help, see Using the Info Map
Show in Drawing
Show in Model
Opens the object in a drawing or model file in split-screen mode.
Pin
Adds the object to your Pinboard, where it can be managed and recalled in pins lists for future
use.
Details
Displays the Object Details form for the selected object.
Navigate related Smart Interop Objects from a hotspot bubble
This feature is available for Smart Interop Objects. Objects in an as-built model, called Smart Interop Objects, contain common schema property values that
associate model objects to physical objects.
If you tap or click a Smart Interop Object in a model view, a list of related Smart Interop Objects displays in the object's hotspot bubble. You can select an
object from the list to highlight that object in the model view or click Details 
 to view the object's properties.
Connected Smart Interop Objects
Pipe component to pipe run to pipe line
Trench part to trench part to trench run
Cable tray component to cable run
Conduit component to conduit run
Duct component to duct run
Using visual intelligence to analyze your data
Visual intelligence offers a very flexible and visual way to analyze large amounts of data in a single model view.
With Visual Intelligence, you can:
Search for tags in published models (that are transferred to the Facilities Data Warehouse using Data Validator) or models streamed from the
Visualization Data Service (VDS).
Group and view tags belonging to more than one change request or work package.
Run visual intelligence reports on published models (that are transferred to the Facilities Data Warehouse using Data Validator) or models streamed from
the Visualization Data Service (VDS).
Generate shareable URLs to directly open the results of a visual intelligence search or a report in a browser.
Search for tags
You can search for tags in the following file types using View and Markup:
File Format
Native application

Note
Hotspotted Hexagon 2D Drawings

these

formats. For
