These priorities are delivered by default and can be configured by your administrator.
Using List Edit
After a search or query, the object data returned is displayed in an organized results table. The object properties appear under headings in each column in a
set order, and the values for these properties appear in the table rows.
With the correct permissions, you can create, duplicate, and delete objects in the table rows, and edit their property values using List Edit.
How do I configure an object for List Edit?
The ability to use List Edit must be configured by the system administrator for each specific object and data type so you can edit the objects and their
properties.
 List Edit is disabled by default. For more information about configuring objects for List Edit, see Configure objects for use in List Edit.
What controls the column order?
The columns you see and their order in which they appear are determined by the object's column set. Each type of object can have its own configured column
set based on their classification that determines which properties display in the results table and the order in which they appear.
 For more information about configuring column sets, see Configure column sets.
Edit object data in the results grid
You can add, delete, or duplicate object rows, as well as edit the object details and property values in cells under the column headings in the results table.
Select the object(s) displayed in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > List Editing.
Why can't I see List Edit?
The ability to use List Edit must be configured by the system administrator for each specific object and data type. Access permissions must also be granted to
your user profile and role to use List Edit. Existing security permissions for any configuration can also mean that List Edit does not appear in the toolbar or
actions menu for some data types.
 List Edit is not configured for objects by default. For more information about configuring objects for use in List Edit, see Configure objects for use in
List Edit.
Why can't I edit data in a column?
The columns you see and the order they are displayed is determined by the access to the object's column set. The object values displayed in columns in the
List Edit window can be configured to be viewable, editable, mandatory, or read-only. Some objects may be configured to be only viewable by a specific role
but may be editable by another role. You might not have access to columns due to the column set access set for your role.
 For more information on your role's access to editing values in columns, contact your system administrator.
What are the List Edit commands?
The following commands are available in List Edit to manage and edit objects in the results table.
Select
Name
What it does
Add New Row
Creates a new row based on an object classification where you can add the
property values in the column headers.
Duplicate Row
Duplicates an existing row along with all its property values into a new row.
Delete Row
Deletes or terminates a row and all of its object property values.
Cell Highlighting
Highlights all the cells that have unsaved modifications.
UNDO ALL
Reverts all unsaved changes in the results table back to the original setting.
SAVE
Commits all the List Edit changes to the database.
All new objects are automatically added to the Pinboard.
The Delete Row command can be configured so that it does not display. For more information, see Configure the Delete command in List Edit.
You cannot revert changes after clicking SAVE.
You can duplicate any newly created row before it is committed to the database.
Why can't I see Delete in List Edit?
Your administrator can remove the Delete command for object rows in List Edit tool bar to help prevent users from deleting important records from the
database. The display of the Delete command can be configured for each object type. For more information, see Configure the Delete command in List Edit.
If you display related items from a starting object, newly created items need to be related to the original object. For example, if you are editing the related
tags from an area, any new tags created need to be related to the original area using the Area column.
The name column is only editable in newly created rows. When ENS is configured for the object type, the name column is not editable.
If the classification column is not present in the column set, you must choose the required class definition for the new object from the classification tree.
You can view the object's classification by clicking Expand Details  at the beginning of each row if it is not present in the column set.
For more information about configuring objects for use in List Edit, see Configure objects for use in List Edit.
Using the To Do List
Your To Do List displays all of the workflow objects assigned to you or your role that are relevant to the selected plant query scope configurations, which have
tasks that you need to action or complete.
Virtually all your interaction with workflows occurs in the To Do List, where you can easily view the workflow, view the related tasks, view the individual task
details and attachments, and action or complete your tasks.
Click the To Do List 
 in the side bar to open the To do List: Overview menu. A count indicates the number of tasks that need actioning.
You can reassign workflow tasks to another user when you are out of the office. For more information, see Reassign work to a different user.
You can display the To Do List as a function tile in a feature set. For more information, see Relate a method to a feature set.
To Do List overview
The To Do List: Overview menu is where you can select the workflow tasks that you have been assigned, you can claim, or are overdue. You can filter the
task types by selecting a task type filter from the list to display the available tasks in the results table.
 You can also select a task type in the pie chart to display the available tasks.
What are the To Do List overview task type filters?
The To Do List: Overview menu displays tasks grouped by task type using the following filter options:
Select this
To display
All
Tasks that are available to you or your role.
OVERDUE
Tasks that are overdue and require your immediate action or completion.
My assigned/claimed tasks
Tasks previously claimed by you or explicitly assigned to you for action.
My non-claimed tasks
Tasks that you are eligible to action, but which you must explicitly claim first.
These are tasks assigned to a role or group for which only a subset of people
need to take action.
[Object type]
Tasks that apply to a specific type of item, such as an Action, Document
Revision, or Technical Query.
 Overdue tasks are highlighted with the Task is overdue 
 symbol.
Select Refresh 
 to ensure the To Do List: Overview menu displays the latest list of assigned, claimed, or overdue tasks.
Select Configurations 
 to reduce the number of tasks displayed to only those in a specific query configuration.
Results table
The results table displays all of your tasks selected by the task filter. You complete your tasks by selecting each task separately and processing the actions in
order to complete the task including any workflow step checklists.
When you select a task in the results table, the To Do List task detail pane displays more information for each task under separate tabs, such as Task, [Task
Type], and Workflow.
What information is displayed in the task details pane?
The To Do List task details pane displays the selected task's information under the following standard tabs:
Name
Description
Task
Displays the details of the specific workflow task from the selected query configuration.
[Task Type]
Displays the class definition [Task Type] as the name of the tab, such as Document Revision, or
Change Request, and shows the details of the workflow task item type, including any attached files.
Workflow
Displays a brief view of the workflow focusing on the active task, the process steps, and the next
workflow tasks.
Checklist
Displays when a checklist has been configured for the workflow step. (Optional)
Checklists
A Checklist tab is displayed in the To Do List task details pane if a checklist is configured for the workflow step. Checklists can be mandatory (as indicated
with a *) and must be completed before the workflow step can be signed off. They can also be configured so the workflow step can still be signed off, even if
the checklist is not complete.
Only the workflow tasks that are relevant to your selected query scope plant configurations are displayed in your To Do List.
If you select multiple tasks in the results table, only actions that are relevant to all of the selected tasks are available. The individual workflow details for
each task is not displayed.
Tasks are displayed under separate configuration tabs for each query scope configuration. Selecting a different configuration tab automatically changes
the create scope to that configuration.
You can sort the order of how tasks are displayed in the results grid. For more information, see Configure automatic sorting of column items.
For more information on Workflow tab, see Workflow viewer.
Workflow step actions
The following actions are available specifically for workflow steps in your To Do List. You see these actions only when they are applicable to the selected
workflow item:
Action
When to choose
What happens next
Add recipients
You want to add one or more users to the original workflow
step that is already initiated.
The workflow remains at this step, and the recipients get this step in
their To Do List.
 The target date of the recipient step does not change. For
information on how to configure this setting, see Configure the system
options.
Claim
Because multiple users can have the same role, you must
claim a step to do the work required when it was assigned
to a role rather than individual users.
The workflow remains at this step until you take the necessary action
to move it forward.
Acknowledge
You need to confirm that work was done in previous steps.
The workflow moves to the next step.
Approve
You do not find any problems with the work done in
previous steps.
The workflow moves to the next step.
Reassign
You want to reassign the step to another user.
The workflow moves the step to the reassigned user.
 The target date of the step does not change. For information
on how to configure this setting, see Configure the system options.
Reject
You find a problem with the work done in previous steps.
The workflow moves to the defined rejection path, which can be
another workflow or another step in the same workflow.
Retry
You fixed an issue and want to execute the workflow step
again.
The workflow step gets executed and moves to the defined path,
which can be another workflow or another step in the same workflow.
Cancel
You cannot perform the work for the step.
The workflow moves to the defined cancelation path, which can be
another workflow or another step in the same workflow.
Complete
You have finished the work for the step.
The workflow moves to the accept step.
Unable to Complete
You cannot complete the step.
The workflow moves to the rejection step.
Document Review
You can conduct a document review.
The document review page opens so you can review the document.
Manage Review Plan
You want to update your review plans.
The review plan page opens so you can manage your review plans.
Workflow viewer
The workflow viewer displays your workflow tasks in a dynamic, organized, and systematic way. You can:
Show your task steps in the workflow for the selected object and see all of the next steps.
Quickly confirm the exact status and details of your workflow steps.
Action the workflow task to complete the required task steps.
See the number of recipients of the same task that are parallel to the one you are assigned.
Change the display options to view the workflow tasks exactly how you want.
Analyze the workflow path to see where your task is going to next.
Reposition and zoom steps using the mouse or touch screen controls.
Hide or display the process steps on the workflow path.
There are two ways to view your tasks in the workflow:
Workflow tab
Select the task in the results table, and click the Workflow tab to display your task in a brief view of the workflow.
View workflow
Select a task in the results table, and click Actions 
 > View Workflow to open the task in the View Workflow window.
Workflow viewer options
The following options are available on the tool bar in the Workflow tab and in the View Workflow window.
Icon
Name
Description
Legend
Lists the types of steps and paths by color or icon.
Filter
Provides options to change the display of the workflow steps.
Reset
Resets the workflow steps back to the original display state.
View Workflow
Opens the task in the full View Workflow pane.
Open in new tab
Opens the task in the full View Workflow pane in a new browser tab.
What are the Filter options?
The Filter 
 options allow you to change the display of the workflow steps.
Filter option
What it allows you to do
Brief View
Change the view to a brief view of only your task steps after the selected task, or expand all the
parallel workflow paths and steps.
Landscape
Change the workflow orientation between upright and landscape.
Minimize Process
Hide or expose all process steps in the workflow.
The following options are available on the workflow steps and the process steps.
Icon
Name
Description
Details
Lists the workflow step details and status, such as target date, overdue date,
and so on.
Parallel Tasks
Lists the action and any other roles that are assigned the same task to be
actioned in parallel to you.
Expose process steps
Display the process steps between tasks.
Hide process steps
Hide the process steps between tasks.
Task details
You can see the details of your task, such as description, status, recipients and so on, by clicking Details 
 on the task workflow step. The information
displayed also includes any message from the previous step, target date, or overdue date, as well as other relevant task information, such as the workflow
step definition. For example:
Parallel tasks
You can see the workflow step action required by you and other recipients by clicking Parallel Tasks 
 on the workflow step. The task window lists the action
required for you to complete the task, it's description, and target date. It also lists all the other users and their roles that are assigned the same task in parallel
to you. For example:
Working with transmittals
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | Transmittals
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/paths/631885a9b0860d9a21b430dc/>
There are three types of transmittals:
Incoming Transmittals – Transmittals created by the owner company to record the receipt of documents from the contractor. Documents are loaded into
the system and then related to the transmittal.
Internal Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the organization to send documents to internal users.
Outgoing Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the owner company to send documents to another organization. Information can be shared with
organizations that do not have a contract or access to the SDx system by selecting the Offline option while creating the transmittal.
 Contractor and supplier organizations can send documents to another company using the submittal process. For more information, see
Submittals.
 Your system administrator may modify the workflow so that the steps differ from those shown. The steps are still carried out using the procedures
described below.
How is the transmittal due date calculated?
The value specified in the Suggested due date box is set as the workflow step response date. If no value is specified, then it is calculated as given below:
Online transmittals
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
Offline transmittals
If a contract is selected:
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
If a contract is not selected:
The response days value equals the current date.
 For example, if the default response days is 10, the system adds 10 working days to the current date and sets the result as the due date.
Who receives the email in an offline transmittal?
After the transmittal is submitted, an email, along with a link to download the zip file, is sent to the email address available on the ToUser or the Organization.
If both ToUser and Organization are selected, precedence is given to the ToUser.
 If the email address is empty for the ToUser, a Wrong/Invalid email address step is sent to the To Do List of the sender so they can update the
email address and resend the transmittal.
 The zip file contains the transmittal cover letter and any files attached to the document.
In an offline transmittal, does the link (to download the files) in the email expire?
Yes. To add a layer of security, the link expires after 15 working days by default. However, this may be modified by your administrator.
In an offline transmittal, what should I do if I have problems downloading the zip file?
 This could happen when the link provided in the email expires or the zip file does not exist.
Reply to the email, providing the details of the error message. You will then be emailed a new link, or the validity of the existing link will be extended. For more
information, see Reshare a transmittal.
Create a transmittal
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Create a transmittal (modified in an update).
1. In a communication feature set, select the type of transmittal you want to send.
 You can also create a transmittal from the Actions 
 menu of one or more signed-off documents.
2. Create your transmittal and provide the other information as required.
For an internal transmittal, you must set the Due Date.
For an online outgoing transmittal, you must select the contract.
For an incoming transmittal, the transmittal number is not automatically generated by the system. You can enter the number provided by the
supplier or the vendor.
3. If you are creating an incoming transmittal, navigate to the next page, and attach the file or files by following the steps below:
a. In the New Files pane, select the LOCAL FILE SYSTEM tab.
b. Drag the necessary file or files, or select CHOOSE FILES to select the file.
 You can add new files to or delete existing files from an existing transmittal by using the Manage Files command. For more information,
see Manage files.
4. Navigate to the next page, and add the related document or documents.
 At least one signed-off document in the Current state must be related to a transmittal.
5. If you are creating an outgoing transmittal:
a. If one or more documents do not have the collaborating organization identified, set the collaborating organization in the Collaborate column.
 The Collaborate column is visible only if you select a contract. If a document does not have a collaborating organization set, it is not
defined to be shared with the transmittal recipient organization. This is indicated by 
 in the Collaborate column. To complete the transmittal,
you must set the collaboration by selecting the check box or, if the document is incorrectly included, remove the document from the transmittal.
b. Define the file type by selecting the PDF or Native check box on the selected transmittal documents. The default transmittal file type is set to PDF.
 To complete the transmittal, you must select at least one file type for each document or remove the document from the transmittal.
c. To include the signed files, select Signed check box on the selected transmittal documents.
 If a document has more than one file, the Signed check box is available for selection only if all the files have a corresponding signed file.
d. To include the QA review consolidated PDF renditions, select QA Review PDF check box on the selected transmittal documents.
 If a document does not have the QA review consolidated PDF renditions, the check box is disabled.
6. Select Finish.
For an outgoing (online), internal, or incoming transmittal, a Transmittal Issued task is sent to the recipient's To Do List.
For an outgoing (offline) transmittal, an email is sent to the recipient.
 You can also select Save as Draft to save the transmittal and send a prepare task to your To Do List to continue working on it later.
For an outgoing transmittal, you get a Prepare content and assign recipients task in your To Do List.
For an internal or an incoming transmittal, you get a Prepare Transmittal task in your To Do List.
Outgoing transmittals
The From CC users get a Transmittal issue notification step and the To CC users get a Transmittal receipt notification step. To acknowledge the step,
Select Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
 To know more about how to manage the users displayed in the From CC and the To CC boxes available in the New Transmittal window, see
Create distribution lists.
The submitter gets an Update Missing Information step in the To Do List if a collaborating organization is not set on any of the attached documents.
For an offline outgoing transmittal, the submitter gets a Wrong/Invalid Email Address step in the To Do List if the From CC user or the To CC user
does not have an email address in the system.
 The submitter can see the details of an issue by selecting on the step in the To Do List. The submitter must address any issue before issuing the
transmittal.
Preview the transmittal letter
For a transmittal that has not yet been issued, you can review the transmittal letter before sending it.
Select the transmittal, followed by Actions 
 > Transmittal > Transmittal Letter Preview.
Prepare the transmittal
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Issue a transmittal (modified in an update).
The following is applicable if you selected SAVE AS DRAFT while creating the transmittal.
1. In your To Do List, select
Prepare Transmittal task in the case of incoming and internal transmittals.
Prepare content and add recipients task in the case of an outgoing transmittal.
2. Select Actions 
 > Transmittal > Update.
3. In the Update form, update the information as needed on the first page.
4. Navigate to the next page to add or remove the documents.
 You can also select Actions 
 > Relationship Builder to add or remove the documents.
5. Select UPDATE to save the changes.
6. To issue the transmittal, select Actions 
 > Completed. The system sends:
an email to the recipient if the Offline option is checked for an outgoing transmittal.
the transmittal to the recipient's To Do List in the case of an online transmittal.
Outgoing transmittals
The From CC users get a Transmittal issue notification step and the To CC users get a Transmittal receipt notification step. To acknowledge the step,
Select Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
 To know more about how to manage the users displayed in the From CC and the To CC boxes available in the New Transmittal window, see
Create distribution lists.
The submitter gets an Update Missing Information step in the To Do List if a collaborating organization is not set on any of the attached documents.
For an offline outgoing transmittal, the submitter gets a Wrong/Invalid Email Address step in the To Do List if the From CC user or the To CC user
does not have an email address in the system.
 The submitter can see the details of an issue by selecting on the step in the To Do List. The submitter must address any issue before issuing the
transmittal.
Receive a transmittal
Online transmittals
1. In your To Do List, select the Transmittal Issued task.
 You can view the transmittal content by selecting Actions 
 > Transmittal > Export Transmittal Contents.
2. Select Actions 
 > Completed to accept the transmittal.
 You can reject the transmittal by selecting Actions 
 > Unable to Complete.
Offline transmittals
You will receive an email containing a link to download the transmittal zip file.
Acknowledge the transmittal
When a transmittal has been accepted by the receiver, or when an offline transmittal has been sent, it is shown as Completed in the system.
In your To Do List, select the Transmittal Completed task, followed by Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
Reshare a transmittal
 The Reshare Transmittal command is visible only for issued, offline transmittals.
On request, the administrator contacts the sender who then uses the Reshare Transmittal command available in the Actions 
 menu of the transmittal
object. The administrator or the sender will then email a new link to the requester or inform the requester to use the same link.
Cancel a transmittal
You can cancel a transmittal. If the transmittal has been issued or completed, the current workflow is terminated, and the transmittal is placed in a new
cancelled workflow. If the transmittal has not been issued, the workflow is terminated, but the details are saved for future reference.
 You can undo the cancel.
Learn more about transmittals
Transmittals are used to distribute a controlled set of documents in the system to a specific set of recipients. The term transmittal refers to the report used to
record and track the distribution of the documents. The report details how many, and what, documents are involved, the recipients, and the reason for their
issue. Transmittals play an important part in tracking a project's progress, such as releasing contractual construction documents to contractors at the start of
the project, and they can be used for the distribution of documentation to meet milestone objectives and payments.
SDx supports transmittals with one sender organization and one recipient organization. A transmittal also has one sender and one recipient user or role. To
inform other people about the transmittal on both the sender and the receiver side, you can copy in additional users using the To CC and From CC user
options.
What happens when a transmittal is issued?
When a transmittal is issued, the transmittal report along with any attached documents are available for recipients to download. Transmittals can contain
contractually agreed upon documents that require implementation. Transmittals can also be used to send documents for a documentation review.
In the case of offline transmittals, the recipients receive an email, along with a link to download the zip file.
In the case of online transmittals, the recipients receive transmittals in their To Do List.
What does the transmittal status mean?
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see What does the transmittal status mean? (modified in an update).
The following life cycle statuses are specific to transmittals:
Review Status
Outgoing
Internal
Incoming
Visible To
Description
RESERVED
Creator in originating
organization
Transmittal is pending
issue.
ISSUED
Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal is issued to
recipients.
REJECTED
Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal is rejected by
recipient organization.
COMPLETED
Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal action is
complete and finished.
CANCELLED
Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal is cancelled.
Viewing the History
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Viewing the History (modified in an update).
SDx has powerful information management capability for the safe and efficient management of data held in a database. It accurately records the history of
each object modification from its creation to the present time.
As the object history is preserved in the database, you can quickly view the history of attributes for a selected object or a document over their lifetime using the
History.
Select the object in the results grid, and click Actions 
 > History.
 You can also select History 
 from the object or document shortcut hover menu.
Object history
For all objects, the History page opens showing the object's name, creation date, and number of active and historical attributes. Each attribute is listed in a
grid view, as shown in the following example for tag CV-2021.
Document history
For all documents, the History page opens with the OVERVIEW tab, where you can view the document name, revision issue date, and number of revisions.
The history of changes to the document's revisions, versions, and their attached files are displayed in a train line display in issue date order, with the latest
revision displayed on the right. The following example shows the overview for document 01-0000-ENG-000A with the latest Working revision 03A.
 You can scroll the train line display to the left to expand the timeline to see any previous revisions. A revision issue date is not displayed by Working
revisions until it is signed off as a new Current revision. For more information, see Document overview.
View attribute details
The DETAILS tab shows all the active and historical attributes for the selected object displayed in a grid view. The grid view lists all the object attributes in
rows, and displays the changed attribute details under separate column headers, such as Value, Valid From, Set By, and so on.
For example, the following image shows the details and timeline for tag CV-2021.
How is the attribute's status displayed in the grid view?
The following table shows the attribute's status in the grid view.
Icon
Attribute description
The attribute property is active in the database.
The attribute relationship is active in the database.
The attribute property has been terminated in the database.
The attribute relationship has been terminated in the database.
You can see the history of an object's attributes even if they were set to Terminated as the history of the object is preserved in the database. Objects
that are Deleted are permanently removed from the database along with their history.
You can export the attribute list in the grid view to Excel by clicking Export to Excel 
. The export respects the state of the attributes in the grid, so if
the attributes have been grouped, Excel displays them as grouped.
View the object timeline
The Object Timeline displays all the changes made to the selected attribute over the lifetime of the object in an interactive timeline. You can select various
timeline options, which allow you to view each attribute change, or set of changes by date, by user, and so on.
Select an object attribute row in the grid view, an information box opens and points to where the attribute changed on the timeline.
How do you use the timeline options?
You can use the following time line options to change how the attributes display on the timeline:
Name
Button
What it displays on the timeline
Highlight single
row
Highlights where the selected row attribute impacted the object history timeline. This is the default setting.
Highlight Set By
Highlights every time a user has impacted the selected attribute in the timeline.
Highlight Attribute
Highlights every time the selected attribute was edited in the timeline.
Zoom to fit
Resets the timeline back to fit all of the attributes into the display.
An information box displays a summary of the attribute change including the description, who actioned the change, and the change date.
You can zoom into the timeline to expand when the attribute was changed or to see a set of changes.
Grouping attributes by column header
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Grouping attributes by column header (modified in an update).
You can see all instances of specific types of attribute grouped in a list by grouping the data together by a specific column header. This displays all the
changes to attributes that are the same type grouped together in rows.
You simply select a column header type and drag the header name onto the indicated grouping area to group the objects by that type. You can also add or
remove groupings or combine groupings to display the data you want to see.
For example, to group the attributes for a tag by Value, you drag the Value column header and drop it onto the grouping area, as shown by the following Tag
CV-2021:
:
 When you first select the document DETAILS tab, the different document types are automatically displayed grouped by the document Level column
header with the Document revision type selected by default. For more information, see View document details.
Filter and sort the attributes
You can easily sort the attributes alphabetically in the grid view by clicking the column header to change the order. You can also use the Filter 
, and select
the option on each column header to target the list of attributes.
 By combining filtering and sorting, you can narrow the list of attributes to a set you want to see
Document overview
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Document overview (modified in an update).
When you select the History for a document, the OVERVIEW tab opens by default. Here you can view the history timeline of the document's revisions,
versions, and files in a train line display starting from the latest Current or Working revision on the right-hand side. Each previous revision iteration of the
document since it was first issued displays to the left and includes a Revision Issue Date. This date is not displayed by a Working revision until it is signed off
and becomes the latest Current revision.
You scroll across the train line display to expand the timeline of the document to see all active current, working, and superseded revisions, along with their
versions and attached files, as shown in the following example:
 The OVERVIEW display follows the configured document life cycle, which displays the revision state using an icon. For more information on the
document life cycle, see Documentation life cycle.
By selecting Latest versions only, you view the latest active document revisions in issue date order, along with their versions and attached files. The
following example shows revisions for 01A, 02A, and the latest working revision, 03A.
 Working revisions do not display an issue date until sign off.
By selecting Removed Items, you also display any inactive terminated revisions, their versions and attached files. For example,in the following image
you can see the previously terminated revision of 03A, as highlighted in red. The original 03A revision was terminated and replaced by revising 02A to a
new Working revision 04A.
 The Total Revisions number includes any terminated revisions that have been removed.
View document details
When you select the document DETAILS tab, the master, revision, and version document types are automatically displayed grouped by the Level column
header with the Document revision type expanded by default.
You can quickly change the document type to another by expanding the level, such as the Document master or Document version. You can also remove the
grouping by column header or add more groupings to find and display the data you want to see. For more information, see Grouping attributes by column
header.
The following example shows the document attribute changes grouped by the Level column header, as shown by the expanded Document revision type.
Viewing attached files
When a document revision has attached files, the number of files is displayed under the version number as an attachment. When you select the version
number, the attached files are displayed as thumbnails in a grid view under the train line display.
For example, the following document revision 01-0000-ENG-000A has four attached file for revision 03A version 1 as shown by 
.
 Click on a thumbnail and the attachment file opens in View and Markup.
What are the thumbnail options?
The following options can be accessed from the document thumbnails:
Icon
Description
Displays the selected file in View and Markup.
Displays the image information for the selected thumbnail file.
Communication management
You respond to all communication objects, such as actions or technical queries using the Communication Management command.
Click Actions 
 > Communication Management.
Information on the communication object is displayed in various panes in the Communication Management Details page. The panes displayed depend on
the type of communication object selected.
You can request more information from other technical specialists using an internal technical query to complete your response by clicking CREATE
INTERNAL TECHNICAL QUERY. For more information, see Learn more about technical queries.
Select 
 next to a document to show the associated files. The 
 icon indicates that the file cannot be viewed, either because the web file has not yet
been generated or because the base Hexagon file is not available. See Configure WebGL renditions for details of how to generate the web file. Contact
your administrator if the base file is unavailable.
You can view previous revisions of the communication object in the revision box, such as the INITIAL version, but you can only respond to the LATEST
revision.
Contractors respond to document renditions specific to their contractor organization. They do not see other organization or contractors responses.
You can return to the previous object in a results grid by clicking PARENT SCREEN.
Using Concurrent Engineering
Concurrent Engineering allows you to create, view, modify, and delete changes within a project configuration, without affecting the as-built plant data. These
long-term transactions may span days, weeks, or months, while controlling changes to the project data and the original data.
To initiate a change, the user claims documents and objects from the plant to a project and makes changes, such as updating, deleting, or creating documents
or objects. When your project is ready, the claimed documents and objects can be merged back to the plant. If there are any conflicts, they must be resolved
before the data can be merged.
What are the basic principles of concurrent engineering?
The following examples show the basic principles of Concurrent Engineering, from managing changes withing the project by claiming objects from the plant to
resolving any conflicts before merging the data back to the plant.
Claim Objects
Objects are claimed from a higher configuration plant to a lower configuration project. For example, Project 1 and Project 2 claims Tag P-100 from Plant A.
Update as-built value
In Plant A, the Tag P-100 as-built object value is updated to a new value of 58 Kg and Project 1 updates the original claimed value to 56 Kg.
Merge and conflict resolution
Project 1 attempts to merge the project into Plant A but has a conflict that needs resolution. For example, the Tag P-100 value is different from both the original
claimed value and the updated value in Plant A.
Now you must decide which object value is correct as all the conflicts must be resolved before the project can be merged. This may involve communication
with other engineers and projects, and creates the opportunity for collaborative decision making in your organization.
What is the design basis?
The Design Basis is the information that is claimed from the Plant (As Built) to a project. This information, such as tags, documents, or assets, is used as the
starting point for the project. As you execute the project, the information can be expected to be updated or changed in line with the projects needs.
If the Plant (As Built) data gets updated as well, the original design basis can change. The Plant (As Built) data can also be updated due to a parallel project
based on the same information finishing and merging its changes to the Plant (As Built) data before your project. When this happens, there may be conflicts
when you merge your project data. These conflicts will need to be resolved before the merge can go ahead.
Modify objects in a project
Using the Web Client, you can modify and manage objects, such as tags, at the project level without impacting the object's data at the plant level. You do this
by claiming objects from the plant level into a project level configuration or by unclaiming objects which releases objects from the project level without making
any changes to the plant level objects.
When you claim the objects:
The current object is marked as the source for a claim.
The claimed objects are marked for change within the project.
Objects with any related items are automatically claimed, depending on their relationship configuration.
 Documents are handled differently from objects as they must be revised to a project from the plant level. For more information, see Modify
documents in a project.
Claim objects
 Your administrator can configure objects to be claimed and modified.
What objects are configured for claim?
You can claim objects to your project that have the ISPFClaimableItem interface definition defined on its class definition.
1. In the project scope, search for the objects you want to claim to the project.
2. Select one or more object(s) in the Results table.
3. Click ADD TO CLAIMBOARD to add the selected objects to the Claimboard for <ProjectName> 
 list.
4. In the side bar, click Claimboard for <ProjectName> 
 to display the objects to be claimed, and click PROCEED TO CLAIM to navigate to the Claim
page.
If you do not want to claim any one or more selected object(s), you can remove them from Claimboard by clicking 
 for the respective object.
When there are multiple methods assigned to claim an object, only one method is applicable to the object. To assign a preferable method, log on as
an administrator and configure access groups such that the preferable method is selected by default while proceeding to claim.
5. In the Items to Claim to <Project Name> section of the Claim page, based on the type of the object do the following:
If an object does not have any dependencies, select one or more such tags, and click CLAIM.
If an object has dependencies (optional or mandatory), you must resolve the mandatory dependencies to claim such objects. For more information,
see Claim and Unclaim related objects.
When an object is claimed, it is automatically removed from the Claimboard for <ProjectName> list.
In the Claimboard, you can select any object and click  to do any of the following:
Click this
To do this
Remove the tag from Claimboard
Open the Actions menu for the tag
Show object's details as a organized user-focused information
To remove any tag from the claim scope, click 
 for the respective tag on the Items to Claim menu.
Tags with the required dependent objects have the Item has dependencies 
 icon shown in the menu.
Delete objects from claimboard
To delete one or more objects from the Claimboard for <ProjectName>,
a. Click  > Select items to remove.
b. Select one or more objects and click Remove selected items 
.
You can click Select All 
 to select and remove all the objects in the Claimboard.
You can click Cancel Selection 
 to go back to the Claimboard.
Unclaim objects
The Unclaim command allows you to release objects from the control of a project without merging the changes to the plant configuration.
1. Search for the objects you want to unclaim from the project scope.
2. Select one or more object(s) in the Results table.
3. In the sidebar, click Actions 
 > Concurrent Engineering > Unclaim.
4. In the Items to Unclaim from <Project Name> section of the Unclaim page, do one of the following:
If an object does not have any dependencies, select one or more such objects, and click UNCLAIM.
If an object has mandatory dependencies, resolve them to unclaim such objects. For more information, see Claim and Unclaim related objects.
Tags with required dependent objects have Item has dependencies 
 icon shown in the menu.
Once an object is unclaimed, it is automatically removed from the Items to Unclaim list.
Click this
To do this
Remove the object from the Items to Unclaim and Dependencies list
Open Actions menu for the object
Show object's details as a organized user-focused information
Claim and Unclaim related objects
In order to claim or unclaim an object which is related to a dependent object, you must resolve the dependencies. This can be done by adding the related
objects also to the scope. However, it is not mandatory to claim the optional dependent object.
Claim/Unclaim mandatory objects
1. In the Items to Claim or Unclaim section of the Claim or Unclaim page respectively, select an object with Item has dependencies 
 icon from the list
in order to display the dependent object properties in the right pane.
2. In the right pane, click ADD TO CLAIM or UNCLAIM SCOPE to add the dependent mandatory object(s) to the Items to Claim or Unclaim section
respectively.
 By default, the mandatory dependent objects are selected.
3. In the Items to Claim or Unclaim section, select one or more objects, and click CLAIM or UNCLAIM.
You can also add optional dependent objects to the scope, by selecting them in the <Dependencies> section of the Claim or Unclaim page.
To remove any object from the Items to Claim or Unclaim section, click 
 or 
 for the respective object.
Merging objects
When you have finished making changes to the claimed objects or documents in your project, you can merge objects, groups of objects, structures of data, or
the entire project back to the plant. The project object's property values and relationships are reconciled with the plant objects in the higher configuration or
plant.
Related items may be automatically merged depending on the relationship configuration in the schema.
We recommend you to resolve any conflicts by deciding which engineering details or values are correct for the project object properties and relationships
before the objects can be merged to the plant. For more information, see Resolve conflicts.
When you merge objects there are three ways the values can be resolved:
The objects are merged with the same values they had when claimed from the plant objects.
The objects are merged and updated with new values that were assigned to the plant objects.
The objects are merged and the plant objects are updated with the new project values.
 You can apply a property value to all claimed objects being merged from a project or lower configuration, which can be seen in a results table. For more
information, see Configure a property value to be set on merged objects.
Merging a single object or group of objects
You can merge a single object or a selection of objects to a higher configuration or plant, along with any related dependent items that also need to be merged
at the same time.
Locate the object or objects to merge in a results grid, and select Actions 
 > Concurrent Engineering > Merge.
On the Merge page, all the objects and their related dependent items that are ready to be merged from their current configuration to a higher configuration are
displayed in the Merge Summary pane. They can be merged using the MERGE command. For more information, see Merge summary.
Merging a structure of data
You can merge a structure of data, such as a system, sub-system, or a workpackage, which contains all the mergeable objects along with any related
dependent items that need to be merged at the same time.
How is a data structure defined?
A system or a sub system is an example of a data structure that contains the data you want to merge as a collection. When you merge a system, for example,
all the sub-system objects that are ready to merge are merged to the higher configuration.
Another example of a data structure that you can merge is a workpackage. You can control what data is to be merged in the structure by using a defined graph
definition that is named on the MergeStructure method. In this way, you can determine the scope of the data that you want to merge by expanding the graph
definition from the starting object through the structure to the required data, such as Tags.
Locate the object in a results grid that contains the data structure, and select Actions 
 > Concurrent Engineering > Merge Structure.
All the objects and their related dependent items are displayed in the Merge Summary pane ready for review and resolving any conflicts before selecting the
MERGE. For more information, see Merge summary.
Merging a project
You can merge the entire project and all its objects to the higher configuration plant at the end of the project.
1. Open the Business Administration feature set, and select Projects.
2. Choose a project ready to merge, and select Actions 
 > Concurrent Engineering > Merge.
 All the objects being merged and any related dependent items are displayed in the Summary pane for review and conflict resolution before you can
use the MERGE command. For more information, see Merge summary.
Merge summary
You review the objects ready to be merged to the higher configuration in the Merge Summary pane. This lists all the objects and any related dependent items
that are ready to be merged in the following columns:
Type - The type of object.
Number of items - A count of all the objects.
Number conflicted - A count of the objects that have conflicts with the plant objects.
To review the objects, click the object type or the count to display the objects in a list. By default, objects with conflicts are displayed with an Item is in Conflict
 icon.
We recommend you to resolve all the conflicts before you select MERGE to merge the objects. For more information, see Resolve conflicts.
You can display all the objects with or without conflicts using the Show Conflicts Only option.
Filter objects
You can narrow the object list to a focused set of objects by entering a specific name or number sequence in the Filter by Name box. This is especially useful
if you are merging a large amount of objects or a complete project.
 By default, the Show Named Items Only option only lists the items that have a name, such as piping connector. With this option unselected, you see
all the objects, named and unnamed.
Resolve conflicts
A conflict can occur when an object claimed from its current configuration has values that differ from those in the higher configuration or plant. You must
resolve these conflicts before the object can be successfully merged into the higher configuration or plant.
How do you resolve conflicts?
You can resolve conflicts by comparing an object at the project level with the object at the higher configuration level. This shows any differences in the project
level object compared to the original design basis found at the higher configuration level.
The options you have to resolve the object conflicts are:
Merge the lower configuration object with the value as claimed from the higher configuration
Merge the lower configuration object with a new value from the higher configuration
Merge the lower configuration object and update the value in the higher configuration
Any conflicts occurring between the project objects and the original as-built objects must be resolved by deciding which data is correct. This may involve
communication with other projects, teams, and experts to determine the correct value and creates the opportunity for focused conversation and collaborative
decision making within your organization.
1. On the Merge page, in the Merge Summary pane, click the object displayed with the Item is in Conflict 
 icon.
2. In the Resolve Conflicts section, resolve the conflict by selecting the required object property or relationship option and choosing the required
parameters that are to be merged.
3. Select SAVE & RESOLVE.
 When a conflict is resolved, in the Merge Summary pane the indicator is removed from the object and the conflicts count reflect the change. We
recommend you to resolve all the conflicts, before they are merged to the plant.
4. Select MERGE to merge the selected claimed objects back into a higher configuration or plant.
Complete merge
The MERGE button allows you to merge the selected claimed objects back into a higher configuration or plant. On the Merge page, we recommend you to
resolve all the object conflicts in the Merge Summary pane, before you select MERGE. For more information, see Merge summary.
The merge commences and you can review the progress in the Merge Progress Notification message. For more information, see Merge progress
notification.
Merge progress notification
You can monitor how the merge progresses by reviewing the Merge Progress Notification message in Notifications 
.
During merge processing, the Merge Progress Notification message is automatically updated for the duration of the merge. The message is useful as you
can review the statistical information for the merge, such as:
Expected number of merged objects
Expected number of merged relationships
Actual number of processed objects
Actual number of processed relationships
Number of failed objects
Number of failed relationships
Merge status
Merge duration
The merge status is continually updated in the notification with the following states:
Processing - when the merge is in progress.
Completed - when the merge has successfully completed.
Completed With Errors - when the merge is complete but some objects were not merged due to errors.
Failed - when the merge failed due to connection or configuration issues.
 The merge process runs asynchronously in the background with the merge progress notification. As the merge progresses, you can continue to work
on unrelated tasks, which is useful when merging a large dataset.
Merge object failures
If objects, groups of objects, structures of data, or a project has been merged and then displays the Completed With Errors status, any objects that failed to
merge from the project to the plant are raised as errors and a log file is attached to the merge progress notification item.
Merging errors may arise for many reasons, such as:
When minimum or maximum cardinality is violated for a relationship.
When a schema change was made to the object or relationship between the claim and the merge operations.
Where a data mismatch situation occurs, such as a duplicate UID.
If any data mismatches occur in the merge process, the merge still progresses. Any objects that have errors can be reviewed later.
You must change your scope to the lower configuration project in order to edit or correct any failed objects.
Modify documents in a project
SDx Projects provides a set of preconfigured collaboration work processes for the management of change, including the Quality Assurance (QA) Review.
During a project, the external suppliers or contractors deliver multiple documents using the submittal process. The submittal documents are then routed for
review by the Quality Assurance (QA) Review workflow, which allows your company's technical experts to immediately review any changes to delivered
documentation. This ensures that critical changes to documentation receives the appropriate level of scrutiny.
SDx manages change to project documents using the submittal process.
External sources make changes to project documents and load them into the SDx system in a submittal.
The documents are revised directly into the project by the submittal process. No manual Revise of documents is required.
Documents in a submittal are automatically added to the QA Review workflow for review and approval.
Only documents delivered in a submittal go through the QA Review process.
 For more information on submittals and the QA Review process, see Execute a project in SDx and Load the submittal documents/tags and perform
document reviews.
When you work with concurrent engineering, documents are managed differently from objects. You can make changes to the working revision in the project
level without impacting the supplied current revision signed off at the plant level. Each document detail or file change to the working revision in the project
creates a new version, depending on the revision scheme and the command used. Finally, when the project is complete, the project document can be merged
back to the plant.
The following image displays the basic concurrent engineering process to claim a single document to a project using the Revise command:
When you revise a document from the plant to a project:
The current revision in the plant is revised and claimed to the project level as a new working revision.
The current revision in the plant is marked as the source of a claim to a project.
The working revision in the project is marked as the result of a claim from the plant.
You can create new documents at the project level for use by the project, subject to access permissions. For more information on creating a document,
see Create a document.
Documents with any related items are automatically attached to the working version, depending on their relationship configuration.
When complete, a document can be merged back to the plant. For more information on the merge process for documents, see Merging documents.
All documents follow the standard documentation life cycle. For more information, see Documentation life cycle.
Revise a document to a project
 To revise a document to a project, it must be signed off to set the document revision state to CURRENT in the plant. You cannot revise multiple
current document revisions simultaneously.
You can revise a single document at the plant level to create a new WORKING revision in the project so you can make edits.
1. Select your create scope to the project level to which you want to claim the document.
2. Select a current revision document at the plant level, and click Actions 
 > Edit > Revise.
If applicable, type a new title, revision scheme, or issue purpose.
3. Select CHOOSE FILES to attach additional files to the new working version.
 If you want to include markups related to the previous version of an attached file on the new working revision, select Copy Markups.
4. Click REVISE, and the revision scheme creates the document in the project level as the working revision by default.
For example, the following History for the document NEW_DOC_01 shows the plant level current revision 01A has been revised to working revision 02A in
the project level. In the project level, the working revision 02A version 1 has been edited in the project which has resulted in a working revision 02A version 2.
If the document has an attached master file associated with missing or out-of-date reference files, you can update the relationship of reference files
during the revise process. For more information, see Update reference relationships.
For more information on editing documents, see Working with documents.
If configured by your administrator, existing document relationships can be automatically inherited and assigned to the new document revision, when
revising a document to the project scope.
Merging documents
When a project document is complete, you merge the document back to the plant using the same merge process as used with objects, such as tags.
Documents can be merged to the plant singularly, in groups, or as part of a complete project. During the merge, the project document is reviewed and
compared to the plant document. Any changes between the project document or files with the plant document or files that are in conflict can either be rejected
or accepted.
 For more information on the overall merge process, see Merging objects.
When a project document is finally merged to the plant, the previous plant document revision is superseded. For example, the document current revision A1 in
a plant is revised to a project as working revision B1. You sign off the working revision B1 as the current revision B1 and merge it to the plant. In the plant,
current revision B1 becomes the latest revision and current revision A1 is marked as superseded.
If the plant document has been revised to the next revision before a project document is merged, the merge revises the current document to the next available
major revision. For example, the document current revision A1 is revised in the plant and signed off before the project working revision B1 is merged. This
creates current revision C1 in the plant. Current revision A1 is superseded. When working revision B1 is signed off in the project as current revision B1 and
merged to the plant, this creates the next available current revision D1 in the plant. Current revision C1 is superseded.
All project document revisions are terminated in the system after a merge.
Related items may be automatically merged depending on the relationship configuration in the schema.
For more information on the merge process, see Merging objects.
For more information on document revisions, see the Documentation life cycle.
Conflict resolution in documents
A conflict can occur when a document revised from a higher configuration or plant level does not match the document in the project level configuration
compared to the original design basis.
What is the design basis for documents?
The Design Basis is the document revision in the Plant (As Built) that was the source for the project level document. As you execute the project, the document
information can be expected to be updated or changed in line with the projects needs.
If a Plant (As Built) document revision is updated before your project is merged, such as when other projects merge before your project, the system knows
your project revision is based on old information in the plant. The document merge is stopped until you resolve any conflicts. When the conflicts are resolved,
the design basis relates the latest Plant (As Built) revision with the latest project revision so that there is confidence that the new design is not based on legacy
plant data.
When conflicts occur between the documents in the project and the plant, those conflicts must be resolved before the documents can be merged into the
higher configuration or plant. There are three ways conflicts can be resolved:
The project document is merged exactly the same as when claimed from the plant. The plant document is revised to the next major revision.
The project document is merged with updates added in the project revision. The plant document is revised to the next major revision.
The project document is merged with updates added in the plant revision. The plant document is revised to the next major revision.
As you resolve conflicts using conflict resolution, you decide which details or data and its relationships is correct to the design basis. This may involve further
collaboration with technical architects and engineers in your team to determine the correct details to be used for the merge.
To resolve document conflicts:
1. In the Merge Summary pane, click the document displayed with the Item is in Conflict 
 icon.
2. In the Resolve Conflicts section, resolve the conflict by selecting the required document property, file object, or relationship option and choosing the
required file object or parameters that are to be merged.
3. Select SAVE & RESOLVE.
When a conflict is resolved, the indicator is removed from the document and the counts reflect the change.
We recommend you to resolve all the document conflicts before you select MERGE. For more information, see Complete merge.
Any documents created at the project level can be merged without conflict to the plant level in their latest revision state, as they do not exist at the plant
level.
Loading documents and data
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Loading documents and data (modified in an update).
 You must define your target system to which to load the data before using Load Manager. For more information, see Define target systems.
The Load Manager is typically used to load items in bulk. When a large amount of structured data needs to be loaded, a load file can be used to speed up the
process. The Load Manager is an alternative for the interactive create or update process. It uses the underlying Data Validator feature capabilities to load,
validate, and export data. For more information about Data Validator, see How do I configure data loading and data exchange?
You can run a load job for the different types of objects based on their classification and configure the required properties or prompted values as dynamic
display items in the load job form for each classification.
The load job is automatically placed on to the Loader workflow and the object is imported into a staging system, where it is validated and exported to a target
system using the Data Validator. You can view your jobs and monitor their progress in the My load jobs page.
If you are a document controller or project engineer, you can use the Load Manager to load existing data such as documents, tags, assets, and models to your
system when you are setting it up or when you have completed or finalized a project and you want to load the handover information.
If you are a business administrator or an SDx administrator, you can use the Load Manager to load configuration data to configure the structure of an SDx
plant or project.
SDx administrators can load all the information that is applicable to all configurations on the system such as document types, tag types, organizations,
disciplines, sites, and so on.
Business administrators (and SDx administrators) can use the Load Manager to configure an SDx plant or project. Typical load actions are related to
plants, areas, units, locations, work packages and alternative grouping or breakdown objects like corrosion loops and so on.
 Any user mapped to the SDx Administrator, or Business Administrator role can access the Load Manager functionality.
Prepare a load file
You can use the delivered sample CSV load files to populate your configuration or data. Then, along with the preconfigured mappings and job definitions, load
the objects such as area, unit, and so on, into your system. For more information, see Pre-defined (submittal/loader) mappings in SDx.
You can also collect and organize data into CSV files using one of the following.
The Exchange Data functionality. For more information, see Configure the Exchange Data functionality.
The SDx load file generator to create CSV files used by the Load Manager. The load file generator can be downloaded from Smart Community
<https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
 You cannot upload certain file formats or file name extensions. For more information, see Site Settings node properties.
Run a load job
You can run a load job for different types of objects based on their classification. The load job is automatically attached to the Loader workflow and imported
into the staging system, validated, and exported to the target system using Data Validator.
1. In the Load Manager feature set, select Load <Object>.
2. Type a description for the load job.
3. Select the Suppress ENS check box if you want the ENS to be suppressed, and objects to be named according to the values in the CSV file.
 The Suppress ENS check box is enabled by your administrator.
What is the purpose of the Suppress ENS check box?
The purpose of this flag is to allow data to be loaded respecting the property values given in the load file rather than applying the ENS definition that is
configured for the class definition of this object. This would typically be used to load historical data with specified properties, for example, name, rather
than having ENS define the property. When data is created interactively or through the loader without this setting, the configured ENS is still applied to
the object.
4. Select Next, and upload the CSV file.
 The CSV file formatting is validated after successful upload, before the load job is created. Delete the CSV file if any formatting errors are displayed, and
upload it again after rectifying the errors.
 In SDx Projects, the software does not support the loading of planned documents using the Load Manager feature set. However, you can load the
planned documents using the Submittals feature set. For more information, see Create a submittal.
The following are some of the mappings delivered with the SDx database:
Disciplines
Areas
Units
Systems
Locations
Workpackages
Tags
Documents
For information on object and property mappings for all the classifications, see Pre-defined (submittal/loader) mappings in SDx.
 The above classifications relate to the Loader Types classification, which comes from the Data Validator loader schema.
Monitor the load job progress
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Monitor the load job progress (modified in an update).
1. In the Load Manager feature set, select Show My Load Jobs.
2. On the My Load Jobs page, select a load job, and select Actions 
.
 You can monitor progress of the load jobs you created in the current scope, or you can select one of the following options:
Select this
To do this
Approve
Approve and trigger the next workflow step of the selected load job.
Delete
Delete the selected load job, along with any attached files.
View Summary
View the summary of the selected load job.
The load job remains in the database if you do not delete it, even after the job is successfully processed.
The Review Validation Report step is in the three delivered workflows Incoming Submittal, Loader, and Exchange Load Job Import Validate
Export. The Review Validation Report step is related to the SDVApprove step definition, which ensures that the Approve or Sign-Off step is not
available in the To Do List for any load jobs that have validation errors.
Review validation errors
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see Monitor the load job progress.
After the validation stage is completed, a validation report is available for download. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any
corrective actions.
1. On the My Load Jobs page, select a load job, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Validation Report link available below the VALIDATION progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
For more information on how to troubleshoot the issues, see Validation.
Review export stage errors
An export report is available for download only if the load job fails in the second stage of the export process. The information in the Microsoft Excel report
allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Load Jobs page, select a load job, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Export Report link available below the EXPORT progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
Resolve issues, if any
Problem
You may get an error when trying to create a load job in the Load Manager.
Object successfully created, but failed to be attached to the workflow
Solution
This error may occur in the following cases:
In the CSV file, there are rows with columns that do not match the column header.
The number of columns in the CSV file do not match with the import mapping columns created in Data Validator.
You must ensure that the data in the CSV file matches the data in the specified import definition by comparing the CSV file and the import mapping columns in
the import definition. You must correct the data in the CSV file and create a new load job with the corrected CSV file.
 We recommend consulting the trace log for details. Browse to the trace log normally located at <drive>:\SDx Server
Files\TraceLogs\SiteName and view the SPFServerTraceRolling.log.
For more information on job processing issues in Data Validator, see Job Processing.
Use template documents to load data
You can use template documents delivered with HxGN SDx to manually prepare data, such as CFIHOS objects, for loading. CFIHOS template documents are
available to download. See Edit template documents.
The following template files are available to load CFIHOS-compliant data:
CFIHOS RDL Tag Classifications
CFIHOS RDL Tag Classification Properties
CFIHOS RDL Model Classification Properties
CFIHOS RDL Material Classifications
CFIHOS RDL Asset Classification Properties
CFIHOS RDL Disciplines
CFIHOS RDL Discipline Document Types
You can also use the SDx Load File Generator utility to populate your data and generate CSV files for loading. The utility is available for download from Smart
Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
Pre-defined (submittal/loader) mappings in SDx explains the input formats required, including an example and explanation of each column's purpose and
validation and mapping required. All source files are CSV (comma separated values) formats.
 Normal CSV formatting rules apply.
 Disciplines can also be created interactively in the Web Client. For more information, see Create a discipline.
Additional information
Connectors and Third-Party Software
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx® | HxGN SDx Connectors | Introduction and Sync
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63fde205031b25d11032853e>
