Find sample data on Smart Community
To access the sample data on Smart Community, complete the following steps:
1. Go to Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> , and log on with your support credentials.
2. On the home page, select DOWNLOADS.
3. Select the required product support page link.
4. Select Accept on the Hexagon AB Legal Notice that is displayed.
5. Go to the Freeware Tools and Utilities page on the product support page.
6. Download the required files.
 If you do not see the sample data you need, check back later. We are working on it.
Where do I find information on administering SDx?
Information on how to administer and configure SDx can be found in Administration and Configuration of HxGN SDx. This includes information such as:
How to create your digital twin
How to extend your system (including configuring the user interface and managing workflows)
Reference information on topics such as:
Client APIs
Environment variables
Workflow process step types
Frequently Asked Questions
Welcome to our Frequently Asked Questions page. Here you will find supporting information for HxGN SDx.
Security and user access
Which security features determine what I can see or do in the system?
What you can see or do is determined by which objects you need to see and which actions you need to perform to do your job.
Your administrator can use many different components of the security model to control what you can do or see in the system.
For more information on the security model, see Security model overview.
Security model feature
What it does
Role and role assignment
Determines your level of access to data and
functionality in a specific plant or project configuration.
You can belong to more than one role per configuration.
Roles are associated with related access groups,
domains, and owning groups.
Access group
Determines what actions you can perform in the system
and which functional components you can access in
your system. Users are related to roles, which are
related to access groups.
Security rule
Limits the types of data that you can query in the
system. Your associated access groups are used to
determine which security rules are relevant.
Security code
Sets different security levels for users and data in the
system. Security codes are used by security rules,
which determine your access to documents and data
items that can be secured by these codes. You may be
assigned different sets of security codes in different
plants or projects.
Owning group
Sets up ownership of data, typically by department or
discipline, as well as controls your access to an object
or parts of an object based on its ownership. Objects
can be owned by a user or by an owning group. Owning
groups are associated to roles. The default owning
group is engineering to which everyone has access.
Configuration
You can be assigned different roles in different plants
and projects.
You can create and manipulate data in a single plant or
project. This is also known as the create scope. Objects
created in projects are not visible from parallel projects
or its parent plant.
You can query across multiple plants and projects. This
is also known as the query scope.
How do I know which roles and configurations I can use?
You can see which roles and configurations you can use two different ways:
You can see which roles and configurations are currently being used in the upper right corner of the Web Client. Select Query, Create, and Role to view
the options available to you.
Select Settings 
, and then Scope to view your configurations, or select Roles to view which roles are available to you.
I can’t find a document that I’m supposed to work on. Where is it?
There might be several reasons why you can’t find the document in your system:
A security rule might be applied that limits access to specific organizations.
The document might be in a plant or project that you don’t have access to.
You might not have the appropriate role to view or edit that specific document.
The document might have a security code that you don’t have access to, so specific documents might not be accessible or available.
It’s important to communicate with your administrator to see if you’re allowed to access that document.
Why do I get a restriction exception when I query certain data types?
What you can query for, or how you can navigate to certain data types in the database, is controlled by restrictions related to various user and role types. If you
receive a restriction exception message and believe you should have access to the data you're querying, contact your system administrator. For more
information, your system administrator should see Restriction exception error on query or navigation.
I can create an object in Plant A, but not in Plant B. Why?
You can only create objects in a single configuration at a time selected as your Create Scope. For example, if you select Plant A as your create scope, you
can only create objects in Plant A. You can query for data and objects in other configurations, such as Plant B, but you cannot create objects in Plant B until
you switch your create scope to Plant B.
Which security rules are defined out of the box in my system?
There are several security rules configured by default that define who can see, or use, features in the Web Client:
Web Client features and functionality
Who can see
Comments
Users of organization that created them.
Markup layers
Users of organization that created them.
Documents and tags
Users with matching security codes.
The following rules apply:
Company users can see
documents and tags from any
organization.
Company users can create and
edit only documents from their
organization
Contractor users can see only
documents originating from their
organization or ones on which their
organization has been identified as
a collaborating organization.
Transmittals, submittals, technical queries, and actions
Only users with matching security codes
who belong to the organization that
issued or received the communication
items.
Which security codes are delivered?
There are three security codes delivered: Unclassified, Proprietary, and Classified; however, your administrator can configure any security code that meets
your business's needs.
Security codes can be associated with both objects in the system and user roles - setting different security levels and controlling access and visibility of
objects.
What is the difference between a create scope and query scope?
The Create Scope is the one plant or project configuration, that you select from a list of query configurations, in which you can create and modify data or
objects.
The Query Scope displays an access-defined set of plants or project configurations that you can search and query for data and objects. When you select the
plant or project configurations, this selection determines what data and objects are returned and displayed after you run a search or query.
When you select multiple plants or project configurations as your query scope, the returned data is displayed in the results grid under separate tabs that
correspond to each configuration. Each tab is shown in alphabetical order.
When you query a project configuration that exists under a plant, the query returns objects found in both the plant and the selected project configuration.
For example, if you set your query scope as PlantA and Project2, your query will return objects that exist in both PlantA and Project 2.
How many plants or projects can I select for each scope?
You can only select one plant or project as your Create Scope.
You can select multiple plants or project configurations as your Query Scope. The returned data is displayed in the results grid under separate tabs that
correspond to each configuration. Each tab is shown in alphabetical order.
When you query a project configuration that exists under a plant, the query returns objects found in both the plant and the selected project configuration. For
example, if you set your query scope as PlantA and Project2, your query will return objects that exist in both PlantA and Project 2.
 The maximum number of scopes you can select as your Query Scope is restricted to ninety nine (99). This is a system limitation. To reduce the
amount of data returned, you should refine your query scopes to a more targeted selection.
Change requests
What is the difference between a change request, a change request pin, and a pin?
Change requests are requests to execute simple changes to your plant within your company, or execute and collaborate on large projects with contractors
using SDx Projects.
Change request pins are markup elements you can add to a markup layer to create a change request. The change request pin is linked to the newly created
change request and is a graphical locator for the change request.
Pins are selected objects that you add to your Pinboard, so you can quickly access these objects to manage while you are working.
Why should I evaluate the feasibility of a change request?
Once a change request is created, the feasibility of the request can be evaluated. If one or more users can carry out the task, claim the step first and then
evaluate it. You can review the impact analysis and identify the potential conflicts of the submitted change request.
What happens when I assign a project to the change request?
When a project is assigned, the workflow will claim the tags and revise the documents to the assigned project automatically.
If configured by your administrator, existing document relationships can be automatically inherited and assigned to the new document revision, when
claiming the change request to project scope.
If the changes are not necessarily to be implemented at project scope, you can opt out of assigning the project and continue at the plant scope.
What does the change request status mean?
Status
Description
Open
The change request has been created.
In Progress
The change request has been prepared, and has not yet been approved.
Approved
The change request has been approved, but not yet carried out.
Completed
The closure approval step has been completed and any conflicts have been resolved.
Rejected
The change request has been rejected.
Extension Requested
The temporary change request has had an extension requested.
Implemented
The temporary change request has had an extension approved.
Closed
Temporary changes have been performed and the temporary change request has been closed.
Why is a Discipline Lead role required to review the scope?
A Discipline Lead role allows the engineering department to review the requirements of the change request. As a discipline lead, you can review the current
scope and add documents and tags (if any) that are impacted by the change. You can also design a proposed solution for the change request.
If the proposed solution requires a new project, then a business administrator should step in and create the project before to implement the proposed solution.
Assign a Discipline Engineer or an Engineer role to the users using the Discipline Lead role.
What actions can you perform during final approval?
You can view all information of the change request. You have access to review and understand the documents, impacts and conflicts.
If you are required to assign a project to the change request, do assign at this step. Going further without assigning a project would result in the following:
Your action items or to do list items are missing; to find them set the configuration at the same level as your change request, For example: plant
scope or project scope
If you have assigned a project to the change request, make sure you change the configuration to project scope to view your action or to do list
items.
When a project is assigned, the workflow claims tags and revises the documents to the assigned project.
What does a coordinate change request imply?
You are now responsible to execute the change request. You can execute the change request using the document lifecycle workflow using the internal change
action. If your change request involves external contractors, please contact your sales representative to purchase and configure HxGN SDx Projects.
You can now access the current and latest version of the documents that represent the engineering changes.
For HxGN SDx Projects users
Documents are assigned to relevant contractors to allow the communication with external contractors. For more information, see Communicate with
stakeholders in your project.
Why do I need to assign a project?
Assign a project to implement the requirements of the change request at the project scope.
 Your scope must be set to a project to implement the change request at the project level.
How do I assign a project?
1. Select Update on any of the workflow steps you have come across till now to set your change request to a particular project scope.
2. Change your scope from plant level to the assigned project level.
3. Select 
 and select your project from the Query list.
4. Select Save.
Who can associate a document to different contracts in different projects and what follows?
 A document can be related to different contracts in different projects.
A project engineer can associate multiple documents to a contract through the relationship builder. The software then sets the contract's external organization
by default as the collaborating organization for the documents according to the contract needs.
Data books
Why do you need data books?
As a document controller, you maintain a lot of maintenance records, fabrication records, vendor records, and so on. A data book helps you maintain records
as a collection of structured documents.
How are documents attached to a data book organized?
When you generate the PDF of a data book, the documents and files attached to the data book chapters are organized according to the order arranged while
managing the data book.
Documents and files
How do you use the different document commands?
The following table shows the different document commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Revise
The CURRENT document is revised, which creates a new WORKING version.
Edit Files
A new WORKING document version is created and marked as checked out. Files attached to the
previous working version can be downloaded to a client device for editing. You must manually check
in the new document version after editing.
Check In
Check in the new document version, created when the Edit Files command was used, along with any
files that were checked out for editing.
Sign Off
Signs off a complete document and changes the status of the document revision from WORKING to
CURRENT.
Sign off with form
Signs off a complete document revision and allows you to update properties and revision notes. The
document revision status changes from WORKING to CURRENT.
Update Document
Update the master properties of the document.
Update Latest Revision
Update the properties of the latest revision of the document.
Copy
Creates a new copy of the document with a new name, and allows you to select the required
property details and values on the Copy form.
Document Controller role only
Action
What it does
Undo Sign Off
Changes the status of the document revision from CURRENT to WORKING.
Cancel
Cancels a document when it is in the RESERVED state.
How do you use the different edit commands?
The following table shows the different edit commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Edit Files or Download and Edit Files
Creates a new document version. Checks out the new version, and downloads it to a client device
along with copies of attached files for editing.
Check In
Checks in the files after being checked out for editing using Edit Files.
Replace Files or Revise and Replace Files
Creates a new document version, and allows you to update the document by replacing any attached
files.
Manage Files
Updates the existing document version so you can manage the attached files using remove, add, or
delete.
Save Target As
Downloads and saves a master file and any files referenced, including any nested reference files, to
a client device.
Undo Edit Files
Removes any new version created by the check out. Sets the most previous version back to Working
 This option is available for the Document Controller role only.
How do I know what file to choose for markups?
The easiest way to choose the right file is to use the View and Markup action on the document. If there is more than one file attached to the document, you
will get a list of files to pick from. Pick the .vecz file that corresponds to the file you want to work with. For example, if you want to mark up the area2031.pid
file, pick the area2031.pid.vecz file.
 A .vecz file is a 2D WebGL viewer file, which is used to show Hexagon 2D drawings in the Web Client.
How do I assign an organization to multiple documents?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple documents:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the documents to the organization.
How do I assign an organization to multiple tags?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple tags:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the tags to the organization.
What do the different document icons mean?
The document revision state is indicated by its icon.
Icon
Document Revision State
Notes
Current revision
The Current revision. The
working revision is signed
off. No claim state.
Current revision superseded
A Current revision has
been revised for further
editing. No claim state.
Current revision result of a claim
The Current revision is the
result of a claim from a
higher configuration.
Current revision source of a claim
The current revision is the
source of a claim to a
lower configuration.
Current revision result of a claim and source of a claim
The Current revision has
been claimed from a
higher configuration and is
the source of a claim to a
lower configuration. Result
of a three tier-hierarchy
scenario.
Superseded revision
The revision that was
previously signed off as
the Current revision, but
has been superseded by
another signed off
revision. No claim state.
Superseded revision source of a claim
The revision is the source
of a claim to a lower
configuration and
superseded by a newer
Current revision.
Working revision/version
The working revision or
version. Either a previous
Current revision has been
revised to create a new
working revision and
version, or this is the first
revision of the document.
No claim state.
Working version superseded
The previous working
version has been
superseded by a check in.
No claim state.
Working version checked out
A new working version has
been checked out for
editing. No claim state.
Working version checked out superseded
The new working version
checked out for editing
has been superseded. No
claim state.
Working version result of a claim
A new working version is
the result of a claim from a
higher configuration by
revising the Current
revision.
Working revision source of a claim
The working version that is
the source of a claim to a
lower configuration.
Submitted revision
A submitted revision is
created when you submit a
document using an MDR
deliverable submittal with
the Grouped Review flag
enabled on the project.
Rejected submitted revision
The rejected submitted
revision is created when a
user with a document
controller role rejects an
incorrect submitted
revision.
 These documents
are not visible in a normal
search and can only be
found in the ToDo list or
with relationship
expansion.
Superseded rejected submitted revision
The superseded rejected
submitted revision is
created when you reject a
submitted revision and
submit a new version. The
earlier version is
superseded.
Planned revision
Using an MDR planning
submittal, you can create a
placeholder document.
Such a document is
reserved and has full
document representation
(master, revision, and
version), but does not
have a file attached. A
typical revision is ‘—’.
Superseded planned revision
The planned revision has
been superseded by the
submission of the actual
document. The document
is revised to a normal
revision scheme from a
planned revision scheme.
What is the document life cycle?
The standard documentation life cycle has stages, such as create, sign off, revise, check out, check in, and sign off. There can be some variation of this
depending on the number of revisions and versions needed and which edit command you use, such as Edit Files or Replace Files.
The revise, modify, and sign off process can be continually repeated.
New documents are brought into the system as Working versions.
What is a revision scheme?
A revision scheme is a sequence of major and minor revisions, such as A1 and B1, that are used while creating or revising a document to keep track of its
progress as it undergoes change. This, in turn, also reflects any changes made to attached files that the document manages. All revisable documents in the
revision scheme are composed of document masters, revisions, and versions along with any attached files.
Document master
A document master represents the top revision-independent information, such as the document title.
Document revision
A document revision contains revision-specific information. As a document is created and revised, new revisions are created and attached to the document
master. In a single configuration, such as a plant or a project, there can be only one current revision and one working revision at one time.
Document version
A document version contains version-specific information. A version of a document can be made for each project in a plant. Files are attached to the document
version.
How do I view all revisions and versions of a document?
Show all revisions of a document
The Show All Revisions command allows you to display all revisions of a selected document in a results grid. This command is available in the Actions
menu of a document.
On a document's Actions menu, select Master Relationships > Show All Revisions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Revisions displays the signed off
current revisions of the selected document by default.
Show all versions of a document
The Show All Versions command allows you to display all versions of a selected document revision in a results grid. If you use this command from the current
revision of the document, then all the versions associated with the current revision are returned. However, if you navigate to a superseded revision, the
command returns all the versions associated with that revision.
On a document's Actions menu, select Revision Relationships > Show All Versions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Versions displays the latest signed off
current versions of the selected document by default.
What happens when I sign off a document?
Signing off a document finalizes it:
The status for the revision is set to CURRENT. Any previous released revisions are SUPERSEDED.
The document is set to be the current released version and is made official.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
Document revisions that have been signed off and current are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
What happens when I use sign off with form?
When you use the sign off with form for a document:
The revised document allows you to update properties and add revision notes using a form.
The document is set to be the current released revision.
The status for the latest revision is set to CURRENT and the revise state is set to blank.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
The signed off revision of the document supersedes any previously released revisions.
Document revisions that have been signed off are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
The display items exposed on the sign off form are configurable. For more information, see Configure forms.

The viewing, conversion, hot spotting, printing, and redlining of files secured with Microsoft Azure Information Protection (AIP), or Information Protection
in general (MIP), is not supported. You can attach, store, and manage those protected files with SDx.
The viewer does not support Office 95 or earlier files. You must save such files in later Office versions before working with them.
What is a converted non-Hexagon 2D drawing?
It is a MicroStation (.dgn) or AutoCAD (.dwg) drawing file with a related 2D WebGL viewer file (.vecz) attached. The drawing file (.dgn or .dwg) is displayed in
the Markup tab, and the 2D WebGL viewer file (.vecz) is displayed in the View tab.
What kinds of files can I compare?
Depending on how the software is configured for your company, you can compare these file types:
Hexagon 2D (.sma, .spe, .sha, .pid, .igr, .zyq)
CAD files (.dwg, .dxf, .dgn)
Word (.doc, .docm, .docx)
Excel (.xls, .xlsm, .xlsx)
PowerPoint (.ppt, .pptm, .pptx)
PDF (.pdf)
You can also compare different file types:
PDF to text
CAD, Word, Excel, or PDF to each other
You can compare files with any relationship to a document, including:
Files attached to a document
Reference files attached to any of those files
Alternate renditions of the files attached to a document
Alternate renditions attached directly to the document
 If there is more than one file that can be compared, you can tell which files are alternate renditions from the information that appears when you pick
which files you want to compare.
Why is viewing not working like I expect it to?
Here are some reasons why viewing files or models might not work like you would expect:
Files will not open if your browser blocks pop-ups.
Any markup elements placed outside of a file's boundaries are clipped.
A markup rendition must be available to mark up Hexagon 2D drawings and 3D models.
You must use View Native to view non-Hexagon 3D models, including PDF files containing 3D content.
If you view a master file, its reference files also open.
Why is printing not working like I expect it to?
Here are some reasons that printing might not work like you expect it to:
You cannot change the color settings if printing to PDF.
All displayed markups are automatically printed.
Watermarks and labels (banners) configured in the Desktop Client are not printed.
If you print a master file, all of its reference files print too.
When using a tablet or other mobile device, printing might not work.
If you are printing without viewing the file and an alternate rendition exists, the alternate rendition is printed. You might need to regenerate the alternate
rendition to get the latest markups included.
Why are markup layers not working like I expect them to?
Here are some reasons that view and markup might not work as you expected:
Labels in a drawing might not match the corresponding tag name.
The markup layer must be opened for viewing and you must have permissions to delete the markup layer.
Why can't I view the hotspot bubble when I select a hotspotted object?
When viewing hotspotted PDF files using a touch-enabled device, you might need to double-tap the hotspotted object to open the hotspot bubble.
The hotspot bubble does not appear if the linked URL is invalid.
How do I identify which markup layers are consolidated?
Navigate to the markup layer file and view Details. If Is the markup consolidated? is checked under Markup file details, the file is a consolidated markup
layer.
Why can't I see text changes inline for all files?
To show types of files that contain a lot of text, such as Word or Excel files, the software uses a graphic representation of the file, like an electronic photocopy.
That means that the text is not available for the software to compare.
 You can generate PDF renditions and use the PDF renditions for comparison.
Document reviews
How do I change my review settings for a document issue purpose?
The following table details the settings available for documents in the document review cycle. For example, you can either accept or decline a document for
review.
Icon
Name
Description
Accepted
Indicates that the document has been accepted for document review.
Declined
Indicates that the document has been declined for document review.
Changed
Indicates that the document has been changed and a document review is
advised.
Information only
Indicates that the document has been accepted for information review.
Suggested for information
Indicates that the document has been suggested for information review.
Not in plan
Indicates that the document is available but not in the review plan.
You can also bulk edit issue purposes by clicking Bulk Edit 
.
What does the document reviews status mean?
The following review statuses are found in the document review user interface.
Review status
Visible to
Description
CLOSED AUTOMATICALLY
Originating organization
The document review is closed automatically
because the review process is overdue or by the
consolidation process.
COMPLETED
Originating organization
The document review is complete and finished.
DRAFT
Creator in originating organization
The document review has been created as a draft,
but it has not been completed.
WAIVED
Originating organization
The document review recipient chooses to ignore
the review.
 Document reviews are not visible to contractors.
What can I do as a document reviewer?
As a document reviewer, you can:
Create a review plan, which is used by the system to generate the documents for the reviewer.
Explore all the data related to the document during the review.
Conduct the document review, add markups on top of the original document, and add actions and comments to the review.
Raise a technical query inside your company to request help or information and outside your company to ask questions of the contractor.
Update and delete your draft comments.
Update and delete your reserved actions before the document review is completed.
View, as read-only, the reviews, actions, and comments that are in the initiated state from other reviewers.
Set your document review to waived.
Set your document review to completed, which changes the state to INITIATED and makes the review available to other reviewers and the consolidator.
What is a document review consolidation, and when should it occur?
A consolidator can complete the consolidation step even without any review completed. The consolidator compiles all the document reviews, comments, and
actions as part of the document review consolidation workflow step.
The consolidator responds to each document review, either with approval or by creating a sub-review as an internal technical query asking for further
information. These appear in the document reviewers To Do List for rectification. The consolidator can reject or terminate any duplicate or inappropriate
actions.
The consolidator creates the consolidated review, which contains a marked-up PDF rendition file with all applicable actions. Finally, the consolidator sets the
return code on the document before selecting COMPLETE to finish, which places the consolidated document on a workflow to the recipient contractor.
What can a consolidator do?
The consolidator can:
Determine how a review proceeds by either:
Rejecting the review and sending any rejected actions back to the reviewer for rework.
For any rejected actions, the state of the review and actions are set to DRAFT REJECTED. If the rejected actions are associated with a markup
layer and that layer is selected to return to the contractor, warnings are given that the selected layer contains unapproved actions. The consolidator
cannot complete the process to issue the document reviews to the contractor until the action is resolved, deleted, or moved to a markup layer that
is not selected to return to the contractor.
Completing the consolidation of reviews, actions and comments and issuing them to the contractor.
Add one or more users as document reviewers during the review process.
When a consolidator adds a user, who has a document receipt notification step, as a reviewer, the document receipt notification step is
replaced by a review step.
The new review step is assigned the same target date as that of the original step. If the review is already overdue, the added reviewer will
receive an overdue step.
View and edit any reviews, actions, and comments in a DRAFT or RESERVED state, prior to submission for contract compliance review.
View all supporting documentation for every review, action, or comment.
View the markup layers and select which markup layers you want to associate with a specific action.
Make changes to the reviews, actions, or comments and then reconsolidate if a document review is rejected after a contractual compliance review.
Send technical queries if more information is required on an action or comment.
What are Contributor markups?
Documents can also be marked up by users who are not assigned to a QA review task. The users who create the markups and comments outside the context
of QA review are called as contributors.
The reason why a contributor might want to create markups outside of a QA review is to provide general observations that are shared as comments/markups
and the markups provided in other context like technical queries or actions context.
What can I register interest in?
You can register interest in documents that have a specific issue purpose, documents that are specific to a discipline, and documents with a specific tag
criticality.
Your administrator can also automatically register your interest in different types of documents, depending on your job requirements.
What happens when you waive a document review?
When you waive a document review, the following happens:
Your To Do List opens, and the document is removed from your list.
Your document review status is set to WAIVED.
The workflow step moves the review onto the next consolidation workflow step.
What happens when you waive a consolidation review?
When you waive a document review consolidation, the following occurs:
The workflow step is signed off.
The consolidate document review status is set to WAIVED.
The workflow step is removed from your To Do List.
The document continues processing on the defined workflow path.
What happens when you complete a consolidation review?
When you complete a consolidation review, the following occurs:
The document consolidated review status is set to COMPLETE.
The completed date on the document review consolidation is set.
The consolidated document review is available to be seen by other reviewers.
The consolidation workflow step is signed off.
Your system can be additionally configured to move the consolidated document review onto the compliance review workflow step ready for a contractual
compliance review.
How to handle the rejected actions during the document review process?
The QA Action Rejection workflow handles the rejected actions during the document review process. The workflow identifies the originator of the action and
sends a rejection notification.
How is the review of a document revision handled?
The QA Review Verification workflow handles the document revision attached through the submittal workflow and checks if the document revision has been
reviewed at that issue purpose.
1. If the document revision has been reviewed at the same revision and issue purpose, the system sets the return code on the document to Acknowledged
and exits the workflow.
If the document revision has not been reviewed at the same revision and issue purpose, the system directs this workflow to the next step.
2. The system checks if the issue purposes on the document are related to the current project.
If yes, the system directs this workflow to the QA Review workflow.
If no, the system does not allow the document revision to be submitted.
What happens when a consolidator requests additional reviewers for a document that is in review?
The QA Append Reviewer workflow handles the request from a consolidator to add one or more reviewers to a document that is in review. The workflow
creates a review object for the appended reviewer and adds a task in the user's To Do list.
What happens when an approver requests additional reviewers for a document that is in review?
The Append Internal Reviewer workflow handles the request from an approver to add one or more reviewers to a document that is in review. The workflow
creates a review object for the appended reviewer and adds a task in the user's To Do list.
Add an Internal Reviewer
1. In your To Do List, select a document review task for Approve the Review step.
2. Select Actions 
 > Add Internal Reviewer.
3. In the Add Recipients page, select the Role and User, and then select Finish.
How to identify step progress as a Approver?
When a Perform Internal Review step is completed, it reflects in the step progress column as a percentage. The percentage of the progress depends on the
number of reviewers to the internal review action. For example, If an internal review action has two reviewers assigned:
Reviewer action
Step progress
Review is started but not completed by any of the reviewers
The review progress is 0.
One of the reviewers completes or waives off the review
The review progress is 50%.
You add a new reviewer
The review progress is 33.33%. (Now the number of reviewers are three
and only one of them has completed the review)
You reassign a reviewer who has already completed the reviews
The review progress is calculated accordingly.
All the reviewers complete the review task
The review progress is completed and is shown as 100%.
Contractual compliance reviews
What is a contractual compliance review, and what does it involve?
A contractual compliance review can be configured to review any changes to contractual deliverables and actions that extend outside of the company. This
ensures that all communications and requests that are submitted to the contractor are within the scope of the contract terms and can be issued to the supplier
or contractor for implementation.
The consolidator creates a communication with a concise set of actions that can be sent to the contractor, and the contractual compliance reviewer checks the
communication contents, technical queries, and actions for compliance with the contract. The contractual compliance reviewer receives the communication in
their To Do List and reviews each of the related changes, markups and actions. The contractual compliance reviewer can reject any of the communication
contents, technical queries, or actions and may make additional actions and markups identifying any changes.
Any rejected communications, technical queries, or actions are returned to the consolidator using a dedicated rejection workflow. The consolidator can alter or
request alterations to any rejected communications, technical queries or actions before reconsolidating the actions and markups.
What does a contractual compliance review involve for a technical query response?
A contractual compliance review, when configured, can review the technical query responses that may have changes to contractual deliverables or actions that
extend outside of the company. This ensures that all technical queries are completed within the scope of the contract terms and can be returned to the
originator.
The contractual compliance reviewer receives the technical query in their To Do List and checks the content of the technical query for compliance with the
contract. The contractual compliance reviewer can reject a technical query. Any rejected technical query is returned to the consolidator to make additional edits
or comments to the documentation that require changes to the response.
What does a contractual compliance reviewer do?
The contractual compliance reviewer can:
View any markup layers associated with an action as view only in the Manage Markup Layers page.
Set a review action status to DRAFT REJECTED when any actions are not valid. Those actions are sent back to the consolidator for rework.
If the rejected actions are associated with a markup layer and that layer is selected to return to the contractor, warnings are given that the selected layer
contains unapproved actions. If the compliance reviewer rejects any actions, the consolidator cannot issue the document review until the actions are
resolved, deleted, or moved to a markup layer that is not selected to return to the contractor.
Set the document review action on a workflow path for reconsolidation by the consolidator.
Submittals
How do I view the progress of my submittals?
You can use the My Submittals page to monitor the progress of submittals you created in the current scope and view their summary, or you can find your job
in Data Validator.
What information must be supplied for an MDR submittal?
The MDR document must be listed in the first row of the .csv file in all the MDR submittals. It must have a name, revision, issue date, and file. It should
not have an issue purpose. For each submittal, a new revision must be supplied with the revision and issue date. The classification should be specified
as MDR to create an MDR submittal.
The planning submittal must have the document names, title, planned issue purpose, and date. For each submittal, a new revision of the MDR document
must be supplied in the first row with a new revision and issue date.
The deliverable submittal requires each document to have a revision, issue date, file, and issue purpose. For each submittal, a new revision must be
supplied with the revision and issue date, or the same revision must be supplied with a different issue purpose.
What information must be supplied for an MTR submittal?
The MTR submittal process allows you to load two types of CSV file formats. The basic format contains the tag data and must have the tag name, type, area,
and unit. The classification should be specified as MTR to create an MTR submittal. The inverted format contains the tag properties and must have the tag
name.
What information must be supplied for a TDR submittal?
The tag document must have a tag name, document, and revision. The classification should be specified as TDR in the csv file to create a TDR submittal.
What does a deliverable milestone include?
Deliverable milestones help track the progress of documents or deliverable items, and their issue purpose, that a contractor submits. A deliverable milestone
includes the following information:
Issue purpose - Specifies a single reason for sending a document.
Planned issue date - Specifies the date on which the actual revision of a deliverable item for a specific issue purpose is scheduled to be sent by the
contractor.
Receipt date - Specifies the date on which the document review workflow starts for a specific issue purpose.
Issue date - Specifies the revision issue date for the deliverable item for a specific issue purpose.
Issued revisions - Specifies all the revisions of a deliverable item.
Review Status - Specifies if the revision of a deliverable item is in review or completed.
Reviewed revision - Specifies the latest reviewed revision of a deliverable item.
Date returned - Specifies the date on which the document sent for a review was returned.
Return code - Specifies the current state of the review set by the consolidator for the deliverable item for a specific issue purpose.
When is a deliverable milestone created?
When a deliverable is created as part of a Master Document Register submittal, the software creates a progress deliverable milestone for the deliverable item.
The deliverable milestone stores the planned and receipt dates of the deliverables for the issue purposes along with the review completion information, such
as review status, date returned, and return code.
What is a review plan, and what does it include?
Registering interest in a Master Document Register (MDR) creates your review plan. A review plan allows you to control what documents you want to review
when new documents are added to the system or when existing documents have been revised or updated in the system. Documents in the MDR are
periodically updated and revised by the contractor.
The system sends notifications to your To Do List that new revisions or documents are available for review when the options match what you set in your
review plan.
 The system can also be configured to automatically preselect types of documents for you to review when they change.
In the review plan, you can:
Add or remove documents from a document review.
Select documents for review based on a project area, unit, discipline, tag classification, document type, WBS, FEED, or issue purpose.
View any documents preselected for you to review.
View every document and sign up for review using the individual issue purposes.
Filter the document results to return specific statuses of review plan items.
Accept or decline preselected document suggestions.
Search for documents to review based on specific object tag and document type criteria.
View the status of deliverable items.
View issue purposes based on a project.
Technical queries
What is a technical query, and what does it include?
Technical queries are managed communications between appropriate stakeholders that provide a consistent path and tracking to ensure that the query and
any responses are completed in a timely manner.
Technical queries between stakeholders:
Require a response within a contractually-specified timeframe
May be related to one or more documents or tags
May have responses from multiple responders
A technical query is a report raised on a document, process, or equipment by an engineer or any other authorized user to report an item for further action, such
as gathering information, notification of a change of design, maintenance, discussion, and so on. The technical query is attached to a defined workflow
definition asynchronously to distribute the query details to contractors and their technical experts or internally to technical experts within the organization.
Each technical query contains all the relevant information, images, or documents for the item or process being reported, which can be opened directly from
within the technical query. When you create a technical query, PDF renditions are generated and issued to the recipient or contractor instead of the original
documents. The technical query can also be configured so that the recipients must respond within a designated time frame to meet any contractual obligations.
What types of technical queries can I send and receive?
The three types of technical queries are:
Incoming Technical Query – Initiated by the contractor to request information from the originating company.
Outgoing Technical Query – Initiated by the originating company to request information from the contractor.
Internal Technical Query – Initiated within the organization, company, or contractor to request information across departments or disciplines.
What happens when a technical query is issued?
The technical query is automatically attached to the Technical Query workflow, and a notification is sent to the recipient, alerting them that the technical query
is in their To Do List.
What does the technical query status mean?
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see What does the technical query status mean? (modified in an update).
Review status

 For internal technical queries, the originating and receiving organizations are the same.
What are the main responsibilities for each responder?
The following table shows the standard responsibilities by role for a technical query response.
Initial recipient
Responder
Consolidator
Open Communication Management
Open Communication Management
Open Communication Management
Assign responders and consolidator
Edit, attach, and save a response
Edit, attach, and save a response
Edit, attach, and save a consolidated response
View completed responses

query

with a message
What can a technical query response consolidator do?
The technical query response consolidator determines how a response proceeds by:
Completing the consolidation of responses, markup layers, and updates before issuing them back to the originator.
Suppressing the responses to stop further processing.
Viewing or editing any documentation for each response before submission for a contract compliance review.
 The consolidator cannot complete a response until all responses are either resolved, suppressed, or moved to a markup layer selected for the
response.
What happens when you cancel a technical query?
When you cancel a technical query, the attached workflow is terminated and a new workflow is attached based on the issue states specified on the method
argument.
What happens when you undo a cancel on a technical query?
When you undo cancel on a technical query, it is reverted to the Reserved state. An undo cancel workflow is attached to the technical query based on the
method argument.
Which reports are available?
Technical Query Notification Report - Report of technical queries that:
Are overdue based on the due date specified on the technical query.
Have upcoming due dates based on the reminder days to notify users of the upcoming due date. Reminder days are specified on the contract.
Are not overdue or coming due.
Technical Query - Report of all technical queries, regardless of due date.
What happens when a consolidator requests additional responders?
The Append Responder workflow handles the request from a consolidator to add one or more responders to the current technical query workflow. The
workflow creates a review object for the appended reviewer and adds a task in the user's To Do list.
1. Select the Consolidate responses step in your To Do List.
2. Click Actions 
 > Add Responder.
3. In the Add Recipients page, select the Role and User, and then click Finish.
How to identify step progress as a consolidator?
When a Prepare response step is completed, it reflects in the step progress column as a percentage. The percentage of the progress depends on the number
of responders to the technical query. For example, If a technical query has two responders assigned:
Responder action
Step progress
Response is started but not completed by any of the responders
The step progress is 0.
One of the responders completes the response
The step progress is 50%.
You add a new responder
The step progress is 33.33%. (Now the number of responders is
three and only one of them has completed the response)
You reassign the Prepare Response step to a responder who has already completed
the technical query
The step progress of incoming or outgoing technical query is
calculated accordingly.
All the responders complete the prepare response task
The step progress is completed and is shown as 100%.
Actions
What is the difference between an action, an action pin, and a pin?
Actions are observations made during a review that might require additional follow-up.
Action pins are markup elements you can add to a markup layer to create an action. The action pin is linked to the newly created action and is a graphical
locator for the action.
Pins are selected objects that you add to your Pinboard, so you can quickly access these objects to manage while you are working.
What does the action status mean?
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see What does the action status mean? (modified in an update).
Review status
Outgoing
Internal
Visible to
Description
RESERVED
Creator in originating
organization
Action has been created as a
draft, but it is not ready for review.
INITIATED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Action is ready for consolidation.
DRAFT REJECTED
Originating organization
Action was rejected by the
consolidator before it was issued
for rework.
ISSUED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Action was issued to the
contractors.
RECEIPT ACCEPTED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Action was accepted.
REJECTED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Action was rejected by recipient
organization.
RESPONDED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Action has been responded to.
RESPONSE ACCEPTED
Originating organization

in use.
RESPONSE REJECTED
Originating organization

the originator.
Superseded = True
Originating and receiving
organizations

organizations

Action was canceled.
 For Internal actions, originating and receiving organizations can be within the organization or between two internal organizations.
What does the internal review action status mean?
Review status
Icon
Description
RESERVED/DRAFT
Review action has been created as a draft, but it is not
ready for review.
ISSUED
Review action was issued to the internal user.
REJECTED
Review action was rejected by the internal user.
COMPLETED
Review action was completed.
SUPERSEDED
Review action was revised.
What happens when an action is created?
The action is automatically attached to the Action workflow, and a notification is sent to the recipient's To Do List.
What happens when an action is cancelled?
When you cancel an action, the attached workflow is terminated and the action is attached to a new workflow based on the issue state specified.
What happens when you undo a cancel on an action?
When you undo cancel on an action object, the action is reverted back to the Reserved state and attached to an undo cancel workflow.
What happens when you reject a review action?
When you reject a review action, the following happens:
An action created by a document reviewer is set to REJECTED, and the action is marked with the rejected 
 icon.
A rejection reason is recorded.
The action is put on a rejection workflow path, and the original reviewer who created the action is notified.
Why can't I view details of the action pin I just created?
The details of an action associated with an action pin are available in the changemark associated with the action pin. For that changemark to appear after you
create an action pin, you must first save and close the markup layer containing the action pin.
1. Select Markup 
, and select Save Markup.
2. Select Markup again, and select Close Edit.
3. Select Markup once more, and select Open for Edit or Open for Review.
The action pin details are now available.
Comments
What does the comments status mean?
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see What does the comments status mean? (modified in an update).

 Comments are not visible to contractors.
Transmittals
What happens when a transmittal is issued?
When a transmittal is issued, the transmittal report along with any attached documents are available for recipients to download. Transmittals can contain
contractually agreed upon documents that require implementation. Transmittals can also be used to send documents for a documentation review.
In the case of offline transmittals, the recipients receive an email, along with a link to download the zip file.
In the case of online transmittals, the recipients receive transmittals in their To Do List.
Who receives the email in an offline transmittal?
After the transmittal is submitted, an email, along with a link to download the zip file, is sent to the email address available on the ToUser or the Organization.
If both ToUser and Organization are selected, precedence is given to the ToUser.
 If the email address is empty for the ToUser, a Wrong/Invalid email address step is sent to the To Do List of the sender so they can update the
email address and resend the transmittal.
 The zip file contains the transmittal cover letter and any files attached to the document.
In an offline transmittal, does the link (to download the files) in the email expire?
Yes. To add a layer of security, the link expires after 15 working days by default. However, this may be modified by your administrator.
In an offline transmittal, what should I do if I have problems downloading the zip file?
 This could happen when the link provided in the email expires or the zip file does not exist.
Reply to the email, providing the details of the error message. You will then be emailed a new link, or the validity of the existing link will be extended. For more
information, see Reshare a transmittal.
How is the due date on the transmittal calculated?
The value specified in the Suggested due date box is set as the workflow step response date. If no value is specified, then it is calculated as given below:
Online transmittals
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
Offline transmittals
If a contract is selected:
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
If a contract is not selected:
The response days value equals the current date.
 For example, if the default response days is 10, the system adds 10 working days to the current date and sets the result as the due date.
What does the transmittal status mean?
This functionality was modified in an update. For more information, see What does the transmittal status mean? (modified in an update).
The following life cycle statuses are specific to transmittals:

organization

issue.

receiving organizations

recipients.

Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal is rejected by
recipient organization.

Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal action is
complete and finished.
CANCELLED
Originating and
receiving organizations
Transmittal is cancelled.
What workflows are available in SDx?
Workflows used when operating and maintaining a plant in SDx
Change request
The change request workflow provides a simple process for tracking, reviewing, and coordinating change requests. Your administrator might change the
change request workflow to meet your company requirements.
For more information on the change request workflow, see Create and manage changes to the plant.
Temporary change request
A temporary change request is used to manage a change that has an expiration date. The change request is created and progresses through a workflow
where the change is carried out. When the expiration date is approaching, the request is sent into the Review temporary change workflow where the change is
either reverted back to its original state or extended until a later date.
For more information on the temporary change request workflow, see Create and manage changes to the plant.
Review temporary change
When the expiration date on a temporary change is approaching, the temporary change request is sent into the Review temporary change workflow where the
change is either reverted back to its original state or extended until a later date.
For more information on the Review temporary change workflow, see Create and manage changes to the plant.
Internal change action
The internal change action workflow allows you to update documents with a less rigorous approval process than the formal change request workflow.
For more information, see Create and manage changes to plant documents.
Work package
For more information, see Using work packages for plant operations.
Workflows used when executing a project in SDx
Incoming technical query
An outgoing technical query is initiated by a contractor to request information from the originating company. This becomes an incoming technical query for the
originating company.
For more information, see How do the technical query processes work?
Outgoing technical query
An outgoing technical query is initiated by a company to request information from the contractor.
For the work process diagram, see Incoming technical query. For more information on the workflow, see How do the technical query processes work?
Internal technical query
An outgoing technical query is initiated within an organization to ask questions of other disciplines or departments.
For more information, see How do the technical query processes work?
Internal action
An internal action is initiated by a user, and sent to other users of disciplines or departments within an organization, to perform an action, such as update or
validate engineering documents.
For more information on the internal action workflow, see How does the action process work?
Outgoing action
An outgoing action is initiated by the originating company to a contractor, to perform an action, such as update or validate engineering documents.
For more information on the outgoing action workflow, see How does the action process work?
QA actions
QA actions follow the same workflow as outgoing actions with the only difference being that the QA review needs to be consolidated before the action is sent
to be performed.
For more information on the outgoing action workflow, see How does the action process work?
Internal review
As an engineer working on a document, you can send it to other engineers within the organization for review using the internal review workflow.
For more information, see Create an Internal Review workflow.
Internal draft
As an engineer working on a document, you can send it to other engineers within the organization to update the working documents using the Internal Draft
workflow.
For more information, see Create an Internal Draft workflow.
Handover requests
Project handover is the process of handing over information by the contractor to the client on project phase completion. This marks the change of ownership of
the information and is usually a process in which the client checks for the completeness of the handover. Information is typically handed over in packages or
segments.
When a handover request is created, a handover workflow is attached, and the company user receives the data.
For more information on the handover requests workflow, see Create and manage handover requests.
Signature request
SDx interacts with DocuSign, an eSignature tool, to support electronic signatures on communication documents and original documents, and store those
documents with the correct relationships.
 To use DocuSign on SDx, you need to have your SDx deployed on a secure website that can be accessed from the public internet.
For more information on the signature request workflow, see Create and manage signature requests.
Transmittals workflow
There are three types of transmittals:
Incoming Transmittals – Transmittals created by the owner company to record the receipt of documents from the contractor. Documents are loaded into
the system and then related to the transmittal.
Internal Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the organization to send documents to internal users.
Outgoing Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the owner company to send documents to another organization. Information can be shared with
organizations that do not have a contract or access to the SDx system by selecting the Offline option while creating the transmittal.
 Contractor and supplier organizations can send documents to another company using the submittal process. For more information, see
Submittals.
For more information on the transmittal workflow, see Working with transmittals.
Is there any difference in functionality when using an Apple iPad or Android tablet?
You interact differently with the user interface on Apple iPads or Android tablets as there is some variation in functionality depending on the device being used.
When using Android tablets, we recommended you use Portrait mode or use a Floating Keyboard in Landscape mode to give you the maximum screen real
estate when using a tablet.
